segments.s165-g697: This clause gives a measured height: the preposition به introduces the measure ('by/at a hundred farsangs'), followed by the past copula بود 'was'. بالای او literally 'its top/height' (بالا + ـی as a noun modifier + pronoun او) names what is being measured. Word order (measure + بود + possessed noun) is typical of literary Persian.
segments.s165-g698: نشایست is a negative/formal past form (ن + شایست) meaning 'was not fitting/possible' or 'could not be done' used impersonally; the implied agent ('anyone') is omitted. پیمود is the past of پیمودن 'to traverse', and پهنای او 'its breadth' is the object; together the clause says that traversing its breadth was impossible.
segments.s165-g699: نوشتند is a past plural verb 'they wrote/inscribed'. منشور 'charter/inscribed tablet' is the direct object and بر + پرنیان 'on silk' is a prepositional phrase showing the surface/material on which the charter was written (بر = 'on, upon'). The order Verb + Object + Prepositional phrase is common in epic prose/verse.
segments.s165-g700: همه پادشاهی is a nominal phrase 'the whole kingdom' with همه ('all/entire') modifying پادشاهی ('kingdom/kingship'). به رسم کیان is an adverbial expression 'in the custom/manner of the Kayanids' where به (here) functions like 'by/in accordance with' and رسم 'custom' is governed by it; کیان names the royal house whose custom is meant.
segments.s165-g701: This is a prepositional indirect-object construction: به خان سیاوش 'to Khan Siavash' (به = 'to/for' + title + name), followed by the past verb فرستاد 'sent' and then the subject شاه 'the king' at the end — a flexible word order found in older Persian where the verb can precede the subject.
segments.s165-g702: یکی introduces an indefinite direct object 'one/a certain' and here specifies the gifts: تخت زرین 'a golden throne' and زرین کلاه 'a golden hat' (repetition of زرین for both nouns, joined by و 'and'). Word order is modifier after noun (تخت زرین = 'throne golden' → 'golden throne'), typical of Persian adjective placement.
segments.s165-g703: ازان پس ('after that / thereupon') is a temporal connector; بیاراست is the past form of آوردن/آراستن 'he adorned/arranged'. میدان سور is a compound noun 'the festival field/square' (میدان 'field/square' + سور 'feast/festival'), the place being prepared.
segments.s165-g704: هرآنکس که ... is a relative clause 'whoever/anyone who ...'. رفتی is a past form 'came/went' here meaning 'came', and ز نزدیک و دور 'from near and far' uses poetic ز for 'from'. The whole clause qualifies the guests for whom the feast/field was prepared.
segments.s165-g177: بیامد is a simple past verb 'he came'. بران + تخت زر is a compact locative phrase 'on/onto that golden throne' (بران = 'on/that', تخت زر = 'golden throne'). The final بر + نشست repeats the preposition + verb 'sat upon' (نشست here = 'sat'), so the segment describes two successive past actions: he came and (then) sat on that throne.
segments.s165-g178: هشیوار جان is an adjective + noun 'alert (or watchful) soul' — adjective precedes the noun in this poetic register. اندر is the preposition 'in', اندیشه is 'thought', and بست (from بستن) is used transitively in a poetic sense 'to bind/fix' — together 'he fixed (his) soul in thought' i.e. he became absorbed in thought.
segments.s165-g179: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خوان سپهبد is a noun–noun construct 'the spahbed's table/banquet' (poetry omits an explicit ezâfe), and بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they adorned/arranged' — 'when they arranged the spahbed's table'.
segments.s165-g180: کس آمد is an indefinite subject phrase 'someone came'. سیاووش را uses the object marker را to mark Siyâvash as the definite object, and خواستند (past plural of خواستن) here means 'they requested/summoned' — syntactically the verb follows the object, giving 'someone came; they asked for / called for Siyâvash.'
segments.s165-g181: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. هر گونه‌ای functions as a distributive/indefinite 'each kind' or 'people of every sort'. رفت is a past singular 'went' used with the distributive sense ('each went'), and بر خوان سخن is a locative 'onto/onto the speaking table' (بر + خوان سخن = the platform/banquet for speech).
segments.s165-g182: همه is 'all/everyone' and شادمانی is 'rejoicing/joy'. فگندند is past plural 'they threw/cast' (here idiomatically 'cast aside' or 'put down'), and بن (literally 'base/ground') is used adverbially 'to the ground' or 'away' — together: 'they cast aside all rejoicing' (i.e. joy was put away).
segments.s165-g183: چو is 'when'. از introduces the ablative 'from', خوان‌سالار is a compound meaning 'master of the table / chief steward', and برخاستند is past plural 'they rose/departed' — 'when they rose from the steward's table.'
segments.s165-g184: نشستنگه is a noun meaning 'seat/place to sit' or 'the seating area'. می‌بیاراستند is the imperfective/progressive past (mi‑ prefix with a past-stem plural) used in narrative to mean 'they were arranging' or 'they set about arranging' — so together 'they arranged the seating.'
segments.s165-g1201: فرنگیس را marks Ferangis as the definite direct object (را is the object marker) and appears before the rest of the phrase for poetic word order. هدیه is the noun 'gift' that is being given; بر here functions as a relational particle 'to/toward' linking the idea of the gift to the recipient or action. همچنین is an adverb 'likewise/similarly' that modifies the implied act of giving and connects this phrase to the following command.
segments.s165-g1202: برو is a 2nd-person imperative 'go' (an instruction). با introduces a manner phrase 'with', and زبانی (the noun زبان with the -ی ending) here means 'a tongue/words' or 'in speech' — it forms the instrument of the action. پر از is the idiom 'full of' (از = 'of'), and آفرین is 'acclamation/praise', so the whole phrase با زبانی پر از آفرین gives the manner: 'with words/tongue full of praise.'
segments.s165-g1203: اگر is the conditional 'if'. آب دارد is the present-tense predicate 'has water' (دارد = 3rd-person singular). ت + را combine to form ترا (تو را) — the 2nd-person object 'for you' (ت is the 2nd-person enclitic and را is the object marker). میزبان is the subject 'the host' placed after the verb in this poetic word order, so the clause reads 'if the host has water for you.'
segments.s165-g1204: بران is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative/adverb 'there/on that' linking to the place; شهر خرم is a noun + adjective 'joyful/pleasant city' ( Persian adjectives follow the noun). دو هفته is a duration 'two weeks' placed before the verb, and بمان is the 2nd-person imperative/subjunctive 'remain/stay', so the clause means 'stay there in that pleasant city for two weeks.'
segments.s165-g865: نیابد is a classical negative present/impersonal verb (ن + یابد) meaning "is not found / cannot be found." بریشان is بر + ایشان (= "for/to them"), indicating the beneficiary/target of the lack. گذر ("passage") is the direct object: together the clause means "a passage for them is not found."
segments.s165-g866: This is a noun phrase specifying what the "hundred thousand" are: زره‌دار ("armored [men]") and برگستوان‌ور سوار (a compound adjectival phrase meaning "strong-armed/mighty riders"). Persian places modifiers after the noun here, and the whole phrase completes the preceding numeral "صد هزار."
segments.s165-g867: چو is the temporal conjunction "when." زین ("this saddle") is the object of بگذری, a second-person singular present/subjunctive verb meaning "you pass" (classical style). The following words شهر بینی فراخ are the result clause: city (شهر) precedes the verb بینی ("you see"), and فراخ is the adjective "spacious/wide" modifying شهر.
segments.s165-g868: همه is a quantifier "all/entire," here applied to a list of city features. The nouns گلشن, باغ, ایوان, کاخ are coordinated by و meaning "and," yielding "all gardens and orchards and iwans and palaces."
segments.s165-g869: This is a verbless nominal clause describing the city's features: همه شهر ("the whole city") followed by a coordinated list گرمابه و رود و جوی ("bathhouse(s), river, and stream"). The verbs are implied ("there are").
segments.s165-g870: به هر برزنی is a prepositional phrase meaning "at/in each public square/threshing-floor" (به = "at/in/to" + هر = "each"). The following coordinated nouns آتش و رنگ و بوی enumerate what is present in each square: "fire (lamps/fires), color (decoration), and scent."
segments.s165-g871: همه introduces a collective scene: کوه نخچیر و آهو (literally "mountain game and gazelles"). به دشت is a locative phrase "to/into the plain" or "on the plain" (به + دشت), describing where those animals are found. The clause is a descriptive nominal phrase; the verb is implied or carried to the next clause.
segments.s165-g872: چو این شهر بینی is a temporal clause "when you see this city" (چو = "when," بینی = 2nd-person present "you see"). نشاید is an impersonal/classical negated modal meaning "it is not proper / it is not possible," and گذشت here functions as the verbal noun "passing/passage" — together meaning "one cannot pass (it)."
segments.s165-g993: Literal word order is somewhat inverted and poetic: 'چه داند بدو رازها کی گشاد' = 'How/what does he know, to him, when the secrets opened'. 'چه' is an interrogative/exclamative ('how/what'), 'داند' is the present/epistolary form of 'to know' (he knows/can know), 'بدو' is the dative clitic 'to him', and 'رازها کی گشاد' places the temporal question 'when the secrets opened' after the object — a typical Persian poetic order.
segments.s165-g994: Poetic inversion: 'همانا ز ایرانش آمد بیاد' literally places the verb and the nominal phrase in an unusual order. 'همانا' is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed', 'ز' = 'from/of', 'ایرانش' = 'his/its Iran' (the homeland), 'آمد' = 'came', and 'بیاد' here means 'into memory' (i.e. 'came to mind'). Together: 'indeed, memories of Iran came to him.'
segments.s165-g995: 'ز کاووس و ز تخت شاهنشهی' is a enumerative prepositional phrase: each 'ز' = 'from/of', so it names what is remembered — 'of Kavus and of the imperial throne'. 'تخت' means 'throne' and 'شاهنشهی' is an adjectival/attributive formation meaning 'imperial'.
segments.s165-g996: This clause uses the idiom 'بیاد آمدش' = 'came into his mind' with the verb 'آمدش' (آمد + enclitic ش = 'came to him'). 'روزگار' here denotes the times/period ('times'/'days') and 'بهی' is an adjective 'good/excellent', so the whole means 'a good time/days came to him (in memory)'.
segments.s165-g997: 'دل خویش زان گفته خرسند کرد' = 'he made his heart glad with that statement.' Grammatically, 'دل خویش' = 'his heart' (دل + possessive clitic split into خوی + ش in the tokenization), 'زان' = 'from that/therefore' or 'by that', 'گفته' = 'that said/statement', and 'خرسند کرد' = 'made (it) pleased' (کرد = cause/make + خرسند = pleased).
segments.s165-g998: Negative contrast: 'نه آهنگ رای خردمند کرد' = 'he did not take/adopt the intention/opinion of the wise.' 'نه' is the negation, 'آهنگ' here means 'intention/plan' or 'adoption', 'رای' = 'counsel/opinion', 'خردمند' = 'the wise', and 'کرد' = 'he did' (here 'he took' or 'he made' in sense of adopting).
segments.s165-g999: 'همه راه زین‌گونه بد گفت و گوی' is an adverbial phrase describing continuous manner: 'همه راه' = 'all the way/always', 'زین‌گونه' = 'in this way', 'بد گفت و گوی' = 'he spoke ill and argued' (بد here is adverb 'badly', گفت و گوی is a nominalized 'speech/conversation/argument'). Word order is compressed as typical in verse.
segments.s165-g1000: 'دل از بودنیها پر از جست و جوی' = 'the heart, from/with regard to beings (things that are), full of seeking/search.' 'دل' = 'heart/mind', 'از' introduces the object of concern 'بودنیها' = 'the beings/that which exists', 'پر' = 'full (of)', and the compound 'جست و جوی' is 'search/quest' (جست = seek, جوی = seeking/search). The phrase expresses a heart filled with curiosity/quest about existence.
segments.s165-g937: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که (that). افراسیاب is the subject; از بلا is a prepositional phrase meaning “from calamity.” پشت here is used figuratively as “support/protection.” تست is an archaic/poetic ending meaning “is yours/your …” (here functioning as a copular/possessive), linking the predicate to the listener.
segments.s165-g938: This continuation names the item of status: به شاهی نگین اندر انگشت تست literally orders modifiers before the noun: به شاهی (with/regarding kingship) qualifying نگین (the signet), اندر انگشت (within/on the finger) gives location, and the final تست repeats the archaic possessive/copriate sense “is yours.” اندر is a classical preposition meaning “in/on/within.”
segments.s165-g939: مرا نیز تا جان بود در تنم is a formulaic way to say “as long as I live.” مرا نیز (literally “for me too”) places the speaker as subject/beneficiary for emphasis; تا + noun + بود expresses duration “as long as there is …”; در تنم = “in my body” (تنم = my body).
segments.s165-g940: بکوشم is 1st-person subjunctive/imperative-form with the prefix ب- expressing intent/effort (“I will strive / I shall try”). که introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result. پیمان تو is the object/genitive “your pledge/covenant.” نشکنم is the negative 1st-person subjunctive of شکستن (“that I not break”), completing the purpose: “I will strive that I not break your oath.”
segments.s165-g941: نمانم is a 1st-person present/future negative-form meaning “I will not remain / I will not let (it be).” که introduces the consequence clause. بادی is “a breeze/wind” and به تو بگذرد uses بگذرد (3rd-person subjunctive) “pass by you” — subjunctive here expresses a feared/avoided occurrence (“that a breeze pass you”).
segments.s165-g942: وگر is conditional “and if / or if.” موی بر تو = “a hair on you.” هوا here denotes the element/air (the wind/atmosphere) and بشمرد (3rd-person subjunctive) means “were to count/ reckon”; together the clause means “or if even a hair were to be counted against you by the air.” The subjunctive expresses a hypothetical or feared action.
segments.s165-g943: Standard narrative order: subject (سیاوش) + indirect object (بدو, “to him”) + verb (گفت, “said” — past). کای is a vocative particle “O” and نیکنام is an epithet “well‑famed / honorable,” addressing the addressee.
segments.s165-g944: نبینم is the present/subjunctive negative “I do not see / I see nothing.” جز + از forms the restriction “except/from;” نیکنامیت is the noun ‘your good name/reputation’ (possessive suffix -یت), and کام here means “fulfillment/satisfaction.” The whole clause reads idiomatically “I see nothing except the fulfillment that comes from your good name.”
segments.s165-g1169: This is a fronted nominal phrase: 'و' (and) links this clause to the previous line; 'دیگر دو کشور' = 'the two other countries/realms' (adjective قبل از اسم as usual). The repeated preposition 'ز' (poetic form of از) governs two nouns joined by 'و': 'ز جنگ و ز جوش' = 'from war and from tumult/upheaval'.
segments.s165-g1170: 'برآسود' is a past/stative verbal form meaning 'was at rest/was relieved' (classical Persian often omits explicit plural marking). 'چون' here functions temporally/concessively ('as/when'); 'مهتر آمد به هوش' is a small clause 'the chief came to awareness' — 'آمد' past of آمدن and 'به هوش' = 'to (his) senses/into awareness'. The whole segment yields 'were at rest when the chief came to his senses.'
segments.s165-g1171: 'بماناد' is an optative/stative classical form meaning 'may ... remain' (third-person wish). 'بر ما' = 'upon/with us' (prepositional phrase); 'چنین' = 'thus/so' qualifies the state; 'جاودان' = 'eternal/everlasting' completes the predication: 'May ... remain with us thus eternally.'
segments.s165-g1172: This is the nominal subject/thing wished to remain: 'دل هوشمندان' = 'the hearts/minds of the wise' (دل + genitive adjective), 'و' = 'and', 'رای ردان' = 'the counsels/opinions of the ردان'. The verb is in the previous segment; here the line lists the items that should 'remain'.
segments.s165-g1173: Fronting the prepositional phrase 'زگفتار او' ('from / at his words') puts the cause before the result. 'شاد شد' is a two-word past predicate ('became happy'); 'شهریار' is the subject (positioned after the predicate in this classical word order): 'The king became glad at his words.'
segments.s165-g1174: 'که' introduces the explanatory clause ('for/because'); 'دخت برومندش' = 'his noble daughter' (دخت here = daughter, and -ش is the possessive). 'آمد' is simple past 'came' and with the idiom 'آمد به بار' means 'came to bearing / bore a child' (i.e. she bore/get into the state of childbearing). Word order here is subject then verb then the idiomatic phrase 'به بار'.
segments.s165-g1175: 'به گرسیوز' = 'to Garsivaz' (indirect object fronted); 'این داستان برگشاد' = 'he unfolded / opened this tale' — 'برگشاد' is a past participial/perfective verb 'opened/unfolded' that takes the preceding object 'این داستان'. The implied subject ('he') is omitted as usual in Persian.
segments.s165-g1176: 'سخنهای پیران' = 'the sayings/speeches of the elders' (construct/genitive). 'همه' ('all') emphasizes totality, and 'کرد یاد' is an idiomatic compound where 'کرد' ('made/did') + 'یاد' ('remembrance') = 'recalled/mentioned'. The verb comes at the end, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s165-g129: پیاده functions as an adverbial modifier 'on foot' placed before the motion clause; به + کوی (به = towards/into) marks the destination 'into the quarter'; آمد is the past verb 'came'; the proper name افراسیاب is postposed after the verb for poetic emphasis (Persian often allows subject after the verb).
segments.s165-g130: از introduces a source 'from' and governs the following noun phrase; ایوان میان بسته is a noun with a postpositive description: میان = 'middle' modified by بسته = 'closed' (i.e. 'the iwan whose middle was closed'); و پر شتاب is a coordinated phrase meaning 'and full of haste' where پر is a prefixed adjective 'full (of)' modifying شتاب 'haste' (copula is omitted, as is common in Persian).
segments.s165-g131: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks the definite/direct object); پیاده modifies او ('him, on foot') and follows the object; بدید is the past form 'saw' (classical/poetic past with b- prefix), so the clause reads 'when Siyâvash saw him on foot.'
segments.s165-g132: فرود آمد is a compound meaning 'alighted' or 'dismounted' (فرود = alighting/fall + آمد = came); از اسپ = 'from the steed/horse' (از = from, اسپ = archaic 'horse'); پیشش دوید = 'ran forward to him' where پیشش = 'in his presence/to his front' and دوید is past 'ran'.
segments.s165-g133: گرفتند is past plural 'they took/seized'; یکدگر را is the accusative 'one another' with را as object marker; به بر is the idiomatic phrase 'into the arms' (به plus بر forming 'to the bosom/into the embrace'). The element مر is archaic/ambiguous here (an old/pronominal particle whose precise function is uncertain in the text) and does not affect the principal syntactic structure 'they took one another into their arms.'
segments.s165-g134: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/many' (classical usage) modifying the action that follows; بوس دادند is a light-verb construction 'gave kisses' = 'kissed' with دادند as the past plural of 'give'; بر چشم و سر means 'upon the eyes and head' (typical respectful kisses on the eyes/forehead).
segments.s165-g135: ازان پس is a fixed temporal phrase 'after that' or 'from then on'; چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = thus, گفت = 'said', past); the proper name افراسیاب is again postposed after the verb for emphasis. Note that گفت here is the past verb 'said' (sense list given in the prompt did not include the verbal sense).
segments.s165-g136: که introduces a subordinate/reported clause 'that'; گردان جهان is a genitive phrase 'the troop/host of the world' (گردان = troop/host, جهان = world); اندر آمد به خواب is an archaic word order meaning 'entered into sleep' (اندر = 'in/into', آمد = 'came', به خواب = 'to sleep' — i.e. 'fell asleep' or 'came into dream').
segments.s165-g1049: The repeated particle چه is an exclamative (classical/poetic) introducing a list of luxurious items; there is no verb here — it is a nominal catalogue linked to the surrounding context. The nouns are coordinated with و (and) in the normal order: مشک (musk), کافور (camphor), عود (agarwood/oud) and عبیر (fragrance/ambergris).
segments.s165-g1050: Again beginning with exclamatory چه, followed by nouns in coordination: دیبا (fine brocade/silk) and تختهای حریر ('takhthā' + حریر) where حریر postmodifies تختهای (the ezāfe is omitted in poetry), so تختهای حریر = 'couches/thrones of silk'. The structure is a parallel nominal phrase to the previous segment.
segments.s165-g1051: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin/source and introduces a list of national/commodity adjectives used substantively: مصری (Egyptian), چینی (Chinese). The phrase then repeats از before پارسی ('and from Persian'), a stylistic/device of classical Persian to balance the list.
segments.s165-g1052: همی + رفت forms a classical past progressive/iterative sense ('he went / there went'), with همی functioning as a continuous/aspectual particle. با او = 'with him' (accompanying him), and the noun phrase شتر بار سی (here rendered as 'thirty laden camels') follows the verb; note classical word order often places the verb before the subject/phrase it governs. بار is 'load' and combines with شتر to indicate pack-carrying camels.
segments.s165-g1053: چو آمد = 'when he came' (چو used as 'when'). بران ('بر + آن' contracted) means 'to/onto that', شارستان = 'city/town', and دست آخت is an idiomatic compound meaning 'he applied his hand' or 'he set to work' (classical poetic usage). The verb-like idiom has no explicit auxiliary here and functions to introduce the ensuing building action.
segments.s165-g1054: دو فرسنگ بالا = 'two farsangs high' (فرسنگ = parasang; بالا = high/lofty). The coordinating و links to پهناش ساخت where پهناش ('its breadth' with the enclitic ـش = 'its') is the object and ساخت is the past-tense verb 'he built/made'. The clause describes the height and the broadening he effected.
segments.s165-g1055: از introduces a list of architectural features: ایوان (iwan/porch), میدان (public square), and کاخ بلند (palace + adjective 'lofty' following the noun). The nouns are coordinated with و; this is a nominal series describing elements of the city.
segments.s165-g1056: ز پالیز = 'from orchards'; وز گلشن ارجمند here is poetic contraction of و ز ('and from') + گلشن ارجمند = 'noble/esteemed garden'. Note وز is functioning as the fused 'و ز' and should be read as 'and from' rather than its literal lexical senses.
segments.s165-g657: پرستنده is a noun (here 'worshipper' or 'devotee') placed first as a topic. The prepositional piece به زرین کلاه follows it to mark the attendant attribute or manner—literally 'with/ in a golden cap'—so the whole fragment means 'worshippers wearing golden caps.' This is a compact, poetic noun phrase rather than a full clause.
segments.s165-g658: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing provenance or belonging. خویشان نزدیک is a compound-like phrase meaning 'close relatives,' with نزدیک used in its numeral-scope sense 'nearly/about' when followed by صد. نیک‌خواه is a postposed adjective/noun ('well-wisher' / 'benevolent one') describing those relatives. Overall: 'from (his) close relatives, about a hundred well-wishers.'
segments.s165-g659: پرستار is a noun meaning 'attendant/caretaker' (here the server). The phrase با جام زرین is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase 'with a golden cup' that qualifies the attendant. دو شست follows as the direct object phrase: a quantity 'two shast/thumbs/handfuls' — a poetic measure of what was taken.
segments.s165-g660: گرفته is a perfect/past participle form meaning 'having taken' or 'seized' and governs the following object-complement. ازان جام 'from that cup' gives the source; هر یک به دست is an idiomatic distributional phrase meaning 'one to each hand' (هر یک = 'each one', به + دست = 'into the hand'). The whole clause is a concise past-action description.
segments.s165-g661: همان is a demonstrative 'the same' linking this phrase to something preceding. صد طبق مشک و صد زعفران is a coordinated noun phrase listing quantities and goods ('one hundred trays of musk and one hundred of saffron'); طبق is the counted noun 'tray/plate' followed by the item (مشک, زعفران).
segments.s165-g662: سپردند is a past tense verb 'they entrusted/handed over.' یکسر here is an adverb meaning 'entirely/at once' modifying سپردند. به introduces the beneficiary (فرمانبران), so the clause reads 'they handed over entirely to the obedient (servants).' فرمانبران is a plural agent/recipient noun.
segments.s165-g663: به marks direction or target 'to/into.' زرین عماری is a poetic nominal phrase where زرین ('golden') modifies عماری (a building/dwelling—here 'halls' or 'palace'). و connects that to دیبا جلیل, a noun-adjective pair 'brocade majestic' (i.e., 'noble/august brocades' or fine cloths of honor).
segments.s165-g664: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/departed.' با + خواسته sets up accompaniment: با 'with' and خواسته here 'requests/petitions' (object) so 'with their petitions.' خیل خیل is a repetitive, emphatic plural noun 'crowd upon crowd' meaning 'in great crowds' — an intensifying adverbial phrase describing how they went.
segments.s165-g185: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they departed/went'. The preposition با 'with' introduces the accompaniment; رود و رامشگران is a coordinated object ('the lute/string-instrument and the entertainers/comforters'). Word order here places the verb before the prepositional phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s165-g186: بباده is a prepositional/manner phrase 'with wine' modifying the following verb. نشستند is the past plural 'they sat down' (the provided lemma senses don't include 'sit', so the correct sense is not listed). یکسر is an adverb 'at once/altogether' placed before سران 'the chiefs/leaders' to indicate that all the chiefs acted together.
segments.s165-g187: بدو is the indirect-object clitic/phrase 'to him'. داد is the past verb 'gave'. جان و دل are the direct objects 'life and heart'. افراسیاب appears after the verb as subject (poetic inversion): 'Afrasiab gave him life and heart.'
segments.s165-g188: همی is an adverb meaning 'still/continually' (here stressing the ongoing state). بی سیاوش is a prepositional phrase 'without Siavash'. نیامدش is the negative past 'did not come to him' with the enclitic pronoun -ش referring back to the person; خواب is the noun 'sleep' (i.e. sleep/dream/rest). The clause means 'sleep did not come to him without Siavash.'
segments.s165-g189: همی is an iterative adverb 'kept on/continually'. خورد (here used for consuming) functions as a past verb 'they drank/consumed'. می‌تا introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'until' (می‌تا + clause). جهان تیره شد is the clause 'the world became dark' serving as the limit of the drinking.
segments.s165-g190: سرمیگساران is the plural noun 'the chief revellers' or 'lead drinkers'. ز is the causal/prepositional 'because of/by'. می‌خیره (with شد) describes a change of state 'became amazed/was transfixed' and شد is the auxiliary/past 'became'. Together the phrase means the leading drinkers were stunned by the wine.
segments.s165-g191: سیاوش is the subject. به ایوان is a prepositional phrase 'to the iwan/porch/portico' indicating direction. خرامید is a past tense verb 'he strode/sauntered proudly'. شاد is a predicative adjective 'glad/merry' following the verb, describing his state.
segments.s165-g192: به مستی is an adverbial phrase 'in/through drunkenness' (به used to mark the state). ز ایران is the genitive/prepositional 'of/from Iran' indicating the content of the recollection. نیامدش is the negative past 'did not come to him' with enclitic -ش; یاد is 'memory/remembrance'. The clause says that, in his drunkenness, remembrance of Iran did not come to him.
segments.s165-g1001: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. از پشت اسپان is a prepositional phrase 'from the backs of the horses' (از + noun), with پشت 'back' describing the part of the horse; اسپان (poetic plural) = 'horses'. فرود آمدند is the past tense verb (compound: فرود 'descent/alighting' + آمدند 'they came') meaning 'they dismounted/alighted'. Word order here places the time/conjunctive word and the location phrase before the verb, a common poetic/inverted order.
segments.s165-g1002: ز here is the poetic preposition 'at/on/from (the occasion of)'. گفتار 'utterance/speech' names what provoked the reaction. یکباره is an adverb 'suddenly/at once' modifying the verb. دم 'breath' = a brief sound/cry here (poetic use). برزدند is the past plural verb meaning 'they struck/uttered' — in this collocation دم برزدند = 'they gave a cry/uttered a shout'.
segments.s165-g1003: یکی here means 'one/a certain' and combines with خوان 'feast/banquet' to mean 'a banquet'. زرین is the adjective 'golden' (in post-nominal position, common in poetic word order). بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned/arranged' (بی- + آراستن), so the clause means 'they prepared/arrayed a golden feast.'
segments.s165-g1004: This clause lists objects before the verb, a frequent Persian order: می‌و 'mey-o' (wine/fruit in poetic diction) and رود (here a musical sense - a melodic/strumental reference) joined by و 'and'. رامشگران (plural) are those who bring comfort/pleasure — i.e., entertainers or musicians. خواستند is the past plural 'they desired/asked for', tying the listed items as what they requested.
segments.s165-g1005: ببودند is an archaic/poetic past plural form meaning 'they were' (variant of بودند). یک هفته is a time phrase 'for one week'. زین‌گونه 'thus/in this way' modifies شاد 'merry/happy'. The order places duration and manner before the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s165-g1006: ز here means 'of/from' marking the source or object of remembrance. شاهان گیتی is a genitival phrase 'the kings of the world' (شاهان = kings; گیتی = world). گرفتند یاد is a light-verb construction: گرفتند 'they took' + یاد 'remembrance' = 'they remembered' or 'they commemorated.' The verb is past plural, matching the subject of the previous clause.
segments.s165-g1007: به هشتم is a temporal phrase 'on the eighth (day)' where به introduces the time. یکی نامه 'a letter' is the noun phrase and آمد is the past singular 'came/arrived'. ز شاه 'from the king' is the source phrase following the verb, a normal order in classical Persian.
segments.s165-g1008: به introduces the indirect object 'to' (to whom the letter was sent). نزدیک here functions as part of the noun phrase and can be read as 'to/for the chief' (literally 'near the chief') — together the phrase denotes the recipient: سالار توران سپاه 'the commander (سالار) of Turan's host (سپاه)'. Word order places modifiers after the head in poetic style.
segments.s165-g801: کجا is a rhetorical interrogative 'where' (here roughly 'who/where, who ever told him'). گفته بودند is a past perfect/pluperfect: گفته (past participle 'said') + بودند (auxiliary 'had') → 'had said'. با او is the indirect object 'to him'. ز پیش is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'from before/previously' (ز = 'from', پیش = 'before'). The whole segment sets up 'Where/Who had told him beforehand'.
segments.s165-g802: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/that when'. چون here functions temporally 'when/as'. بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to pass' (classical style used for a general/temporal sense: 'when it passes'). چرخ in poetic usage = 'the Wheel' i.e. fate/fortune. بر is the preposition 'upon/on'; کار خویش is 'his own affairs' composed of کار ('affair/work') + خوی ('own/disposition') + enclitic ش (his). Together the clause means 'that when the Wheel passes over his affairs'.
segments.s165-g803: سرانجام is an adverb 'at last/in the end'. چون is the temporal 'when'. گرددت is an archaic/classical verbal form: گردد (to turn/happen) with the enclitic ت marking 'to/for you' → 'when it/when the times turn for you' (i.e. 'when your fortunes change'). روزگار is 'time/fortune/the course of events'.
segments.s165-g804: به زشتی is an adverbial phrase 'toward ugliness/into ill' meaning 'turning bad'. شود is the present/subjunctive 'becomes/happens'. بخت ('fortune' or 'luck') is the subject; آموزگار ('teacher') is a predicative noun: 'fortune becomes a teacher (i.e. teaches harshly)'. Poetic inversion places the adverbial before the verb: 'to ugliness becomes fortune a teacher' → 'fortune becomes a harsh teacher.'
segments.s165-g805: عنان تگاور is the possessive/agentive order common in classical Persian: 'the reins of the rider' (object + possessor). همی داشت is the progressive construction (همی + داشت) meaning 'was keeping/was holding' (همی = continuous particle, داشت = auxiliary indicating continuous aspect). نرم modifies the action: 'gently/softly'.
segments.s165-g806: همی ریخت is another continuous description: همی (continuous) + ریخت (simple past of ریختن used here with habitual/continuous force) → 'was pouring/kept pouring'. از دیدگان آب گرم is a prepositional phrase 'warm water from (his) eyes' — in Persian tears are described as 'water from the eyes'; the possessor is implicit in context. Together: 'warm water (tears) was pouring from his eyes.'
segments.s165-g807: بدو گفت پیران is inverted word order: بدو 'to him' (indirect object), گفت 'said' (verb), پیران 'the elders' (subject) — literal 'to him said the elders'. که here introduces the following speech/content; ای شهریار is the vocative 'O king'. The string functions as the elders addressing him.
segments.s165-g808: چه بودت is an idiomatic classical question: چه 'what' + بودت ('was to you' — بود + enclitic ت 'to you') → 'what was to you/what has happened to you'. که introduces the explanatory clause 'that' (why). گشتی is the past form of گشتن 'you became/you came to be' (here 'you have become'). چنین سوگوار is the predicate 'so sorrowful/mournful'. Altogether: 'What has happened to you that you have become so mourning/sorrowful?'
segments.s165-g665: بیورد is a past-tense verb 'brought' (verb before subject here, a poetic word order). بانو is the subject 'the lady'. ز بهر forms a small prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / as', where ز is an archaic preposition and بهر means 'for' and نثار is the noun 'offering' — together 'for an offering'.
segments.s165-g666: ز دینار marks the thing/count in currency (ز used poetically before the currency), with دینار 'dinar(s)'. با خویشتن 'with herself / with her own person' (با + emphatic reflexive خویشتن) indicates accompaniment/possession. سی‌هزار is the numeral 'thirty thousand' specifying the amount.
segments.s165-g667: به نزد فرنگیس is a destination phrase 'to the presence of Farangis' (به + نزد = 'to/in the presence of'). بردند is past plural 'they carried/took'. چیز is the direct object 'the thing' (object follows the verb in this poetic line).
segments.s165-g668: روانشان is 'their souls/spirits' (روان + 3pl possessive suffix). پر از is a fixed expression 'full of' (پر = full, followed by از + noun). آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation'. بود is the past copula 'was', and نیز 'also' adds that detail (often placed at the end in poetry).
segments.s165-g669: وزان is a fused/poetic connective (و + ز آن) meaning 'and from that / at that (sight)'. روی پیران و افراسیاب is 'the faces of the elders and Afrasiab' (روی = face; پیران = elders; و connects Afrasiab). The whole phrase points to what was seen or observed.
segments.s165-g670: ز بهر is an idiomatic prepositional cluster 'for the sake of / toward' (ز + بهر). سیاوش is the person toward whom action is directed. همه is the subject 'all / everyone' and پرشتاب is an adjective/adverb 'hurried, in haste' describing their manner — together 'everyone hurried toward Siavash.'
segments.s165-g671: به یک هفته sets the duration 'for one week'. بر مرغ و ماهی literally 'on bird and fish' is a poetic way to indicate feasting on fowl and fish (بر + food items). نخفت is a past-tense negative form 'did not sleep' (here implying they stayed awake while feasting).
segments.s165-g672: نیامد is 'did not come' used idiomatically; سر یک تن اندر نهفت is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'the head of a single person was not put/hid inside' — i.e., 'not one person went to bed/retired.' سر = 'head', یک تن = 'one person', اندر = 'in/into', نهفت = 'was hidden/laid to rest' (past participial/poetic usage). The line as a whole emphasizes that nobody went to sleep.
segments.s165-g1057: بیاراست is a past/perfective verb meaning 'adorned' (third-person singular); it precedes its object here in the older/poetic word order. شهری is the direct object (a city) in an indefinite/unspecified sense. بسان is a comparative preposition 'like/as', and بهشت is the noun 'paradise' — together they form the simile 'a city like paradise'.
segments.s165-g1058: به is a locative/goal preposition here ('into/on/at' the plain); هامون is the place noun 'plain'. The sequence گل و سنبل و لاله is a simple coordinated list of flowers (flower, hyacinth, tulip) linked by و. کشت is the verbal noun/verb 'planting' or 'he planted' (here functioning as the action performed on the plain); the whole clause states what was planted there.
segments.s165-g1059: بر + ایوان gives a locative phrase 'on the iwan/porch'. نگارید is a poetic form referring to depictions/paintings (here functioning as 'were painted' or 'they depicted'); چندی modifies نگار and means 'several' or 'a number of'. نگار is the noun 'picture/painting' and is the head of the noun phrase.
segments.s165-g1060: ز is the classical/genitive preposition 'of' (used poetically instead of از). شاهان is the plural 'kings'. The repeated token وز here is an old fused form equivalent to و + ز (and of) introducing further genitive items; the lexicon entry given does not match this usage (see senses). بزم is 'banquet/feast', and the final کارزار is 'battle' or 'battlefield' — a coordinated list of subjects depicted.
segments.s165-g1061: نگار is the noun 'picture/depiction' heading the list. The sequence سر و تاج و کاووس شاه is a coordinated list of nouns: 'head, crown, and Kavus the king' — کاووس شاه places the title شاه after the proper name in apposition (name + title). Word order is a simple enumeration of what is depicted.
segments.s165-g1062: نگارید here reiterates the verbal/noun notion of 'depicted/painted'. با is the comitative preposition 'with'. یاره means 'companion/attendant' (here a person shown alongside), گرز is the instrument 'mace' (an object shown), and گاه ('occasion' / 'at times') functions adverbially; the phrase lists accessories or accompanying elements in the depictions.
segments.s165-g1063: بر تخت او is a locative phrase 'on his throne' (بر + تخت + the pronominal او 'his'). The following names 'رستم پیلتن' are placed after the locative phrase — a common epic word order where the subject (the named hero) follows the location; پیلتن is an epithet/place-name attached to رستم.
segments.s165-g1064: همان here functions as a demonstrative/linking word meaning 'also/there' or 'that same', introducing further figures. زال و گودرز is a simple coordination ('Zāl and Goudarz'), و is the conjunction 'and', آن is the demonstrative 'that', and انجمن is 'assembly' or 'council' — together naming the people who were present.
segments.s165-g1025: یکی نامه = 'a certain letter' (یکی here = 'a certain/someone'); نامه is the direct noun. سوی سیاوش = 'toward/to Siavash' (سوی + proper name). به مهر is an adverbial phrase 'with love/affection' (به + مهر); the preposition به marks the manner/attitude toward which the letter is sent.
segments.s165-g1026: نوشته is used adjectivally/predicatively ('written' / 'composed'). به کردار = 'in the manner of' (به marking manner + کردار 'deed/way'). گردان سپهر is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the turning/revolving heaven(s)' (گردان 'revolving' qualifies سپهر). Together they qualify how the letter was written: 'written in the manner of the turning heavens.'
segments.s165-g1027: که تا introduces a subordinate clause ('that / until'); تا here = 'until' and governs the verb برفتی 'you left/went' (past 2nd-person). نیم before شادمان is a modifier meaning 'half/somewhat' → 'somewhat happy.' Word order follows poetic/archaic Persian with modifiers preceding adjectives.
segments.s165-g1028: از marks separation/source ('from / free of') and governs اندیشه 'thought' — together with بی‌غم 'carefree' it describes state of mind. نیم یک زمان = 'for half a time / for a while' (نیم modifies زمان). The whole phrase gives a duration/quality: 'for a while your mind was half free from care.'
segments.s165-g1029: ولیکن = 'but/yet' introduces contrast. من is the subject. اندر خور رای تو is a figurative locative/genitive chain: خور 'sun' + رای 'counsel/opinion' + تو 'your' → 'in the sun (light) of your counsel' = 'by the guidance of your counsel' (خور used metaphorically).
segments.s165-g1030: به توران = 'to Turan' (direction with به). بجستم is an archaic/obscure verb form meaning roughly 'I set myself / I went / I took (a position)'—its exact lexical sense is uncertain in the supplied list. همی is an enclitic/adverb giving continuity or emphasis ('indeed/still'), and جای تو = 'your place' (object/role taken). The verb governs the following object phrase.
segments.s165-g1031: گر = 'if' introduces a conditional. آنجا که هستی = 'the place where you are' (که is a relative marker); here هستی functions as 'you are' (2nd-person present), an archaic/poetic use not reflected in the supplied noun senses. خوش و خرم است = 'is pleasant and joyful/prosperous' with the copula است at the end.
segments.s165-g1032: چنان چون = a paired emphatic 'just so / in that way.' بباید is a modal verb 'ought/should' (present form) and combines with the following nominal phrase دلت بی‌غم است ('your heart is without sorrow'); note دلت = دل + enclitic -ت 'your'. The clause states that, as it should be, your heart is carefree.
segments.s165-g465: بدو گفت is a tight phrase: بدو (to him) + گفت (said). Here گفت is the past tense 'he said' and بدو is an enclitic prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; word order (prepositional pronoun + verb) is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s165-g466: This clause is an adverbial/imperative continuation: به فر سیاووش خسرو به ناز. به + فر = 'for/by the farr (royal radiance) of'; سیاووش names whose farr is invoked; خسرو is a title/name ('the king') addressed; به ناز functions adverbially 'with nâz' = 'with pride/pomp'. The sequence is poetic (shortened and emphatic), linking the order (prepare) to its honouring purpose.
segments.s165-g467: چگونه introduces a rhetorical question 'how (can it be)...'. نباشیم is 1st-person-plural negative subjunctive/optative ('we not be' / 'we could not be'); امروز شاد completes the clause 'today (be) joyful'. The negative subjunctive here produces the idiomatic 'How can we not be ...'.
segments.s165-g468: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause 'for/since'. داماد باشد is a present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-person clause used in narration to state a fact ('is a groom'); نبیره قباد names the groom as 'the scion/offspring of Qobad'. Note the verblessness in English is rendered here by باشَد ('is').
segments.s165-g469: بیاورد is past tense 'he brought' (narrative past); گلشهر is the proper name (subject or agent in context) and دخترش را is the definite object with را marking 'his daughter' as what was brought. This follows the normal SOV object marking with را after the object.
segments.s165-g470: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put' (here transitive, past). از بر تارک is an archaic/prepositional phrase meaning 'upon the crown/crest (of the head)'; افسرش را is the object marked with را — افسر here 'helmet' — so the whole means 'placed (her) upon the crest of his helmet'. The somewhat inverted order (prepositional phrase before the object) is a poetic construction.
segments.s165-g471: This is a poetic list introduced by repeated به ('with/with the use of'); دیبا, دینار, در, درم list luxury items (brocade, dinars, pearls/jewels (poetic در), dirhams). The repeated و connects nouns in a cataloguing rhythm typical of epic poetry.
segments.s165-g472: Another linked list with the adverbial به: 'with scent and with colour and with every (thing), more and less.' هر بیش و کم is an idiomatic pair 'every (one), more and less' i.e. 'of every kind, whether greater or lesser'. The parallel repetitions of به and و create emphatic accumulation.
segments.s165-g201: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the army' (ز = poetic 'from/of'); همی here is the older adverbial marker indicating ongoing or habitual action ('would/was'); هر کسی = 'each person'; با نثار = 'with an offering' (با + noun). The clause lacks an explicit subject pronoun (omitted 'he/they') as is common in Persian.
segments.s165-g202: ز دینار وز گوهر شاهوار is a coordinated noun phrase giving the content of the offerings: ز here marks the material/source 'of/dinared', دینار = 'dinar(s)', وز is an archaic contracted conjunction ('و ز' = 'and of') joining the next item, گوهر = 'gems/jewels', شاهوار is an adjective 'regal/royal' modifying گوهر. The whole phrase is the object of نثار above.
segments.s165-g203: ازین‌گونه = 'in this manner'; پیش here means 'in the presence of/before' when used with a person (پیش سیاوش = before Siavash); روند is the third-person plural present/past indicative 'they go/proceed' (here narrative present/continuous in style). Word order places the manner phrase before the verb phrase.
segments.s165-g204: هشیوار و بیدار و خامش are three coordinated predicative adjectives/adverbs ('alert, awake, and silent') describing the manner of their going; روند repeats the verb to apply to all three coordinated descriptors ('they went alert and awake and silent'). This repetition of the verb for emphasis is common in poetic diction.
segments.s165-g205: فراوان functions as an adjective/adverb 'many/plentiful' modifying سپهبد ('many spahbeds'); فرستاد is simple past 'he sent' (3rd singular); چیز here is the direct object 'things/supplies'. Poetic word order allows the modifier, noun, verb, and object to appear in this sequence.
segments.s165-g206: بدین گونه = 'in this manner' (poetic compound); یک هفته = 'one week' marking duration; بگذشت is the past impersonal/third-person singular 'passed' (i.e., a week passed); نیز = 'also/too', indicating that another week went by as well. The clause reports elapsed time following the actions described.
segments.s165-g207: شبی = 'one night' (temporal adverbial); با سیاوش = 'with Siavash' or 'to Siavash' (marks the addressee); چنین = 'thus/so' introducing the quoted speech; گفت here is the reporting verb 'said' (poetic inversion places گفت before شاه); شاه = 'the king' (the speaker). Word order (temporal phrase + indirect object + manner + verb + subject) reflects poetic flexibility.
segments.s165-g208: که introduces the content of the king's speech 'that...'; فردا = 'tomorrow'; بسازیم is the present/subjunctive 1st person plural or hortative/intentional 'we will prepare/make ready' (here 'we two will prepare'); هر دو = 'both of us' (lit. 'each two' = 'the two'); پگاه = 'dawn/early morning' giving the time. Together they form the quoted plan: 'Tomorrow at dawn the two of us will prepare.'
segments.s165-g793: Literal word order is inverted: the prepositional phrase 'از اخترشناسان' ('from / of the astrologers') comes first, then the verb 'بپرسید' (past tense 'asked'), and finally the subject 'شاه' ('the king'). This is a terse, poetic past narration: 'the king asked (of) the astrologers.'
segments.s165-g794: 'که‌گر' is the contracted poetic form of 'که اگر' meaning 'if / whether'. 'سازم' is first person singular (subjunctive/optative sense used here) of 'ساختن' — 'I should/might make or set up.' 'ایدر' is an archaic form of 'اینجا' ('here'), and 'یکی جایگاه' is 'one / a single place/seat'; together they express 'whether I should establish here one place.'
segments.s165-g795: 'ازو' here functions like a possessive 'of him / his' (classical usage). 'فر' specifically denotes the royal divine glory; 'بختم' (بخت + ـم) means 'my/ his fate / luck' in this construction; 'به سامان بود' is a fixed expression meaning 'was in order' — 'بود' is past tense 'was.' The whole segment states that his glory and fortune were settled.
segments.s165-g796: The particle 'وگر' (here written together as 'وگرکار') functions adversatively ('but / however') linking the second clause; 'کار با جنگ سازان بود' literally 'his work was with war-makers' — 'با' = 'with', 'جنگ' = 'war', 'سازان' = 'makers/doers' (here the sense 'makers' is intended), and 'بود' is past tense 'was.' The clause contrasts his settled fortune with the fact that his activity concerned war.
segments.s165-g797: 'بگفتند' is a past plural 'they said.' 'یکسر' here means 'at once / altogether,' and 'به شاه' marks the addressee 'to the king.' 'گزین' is an imperative or hortative 'choose / select' directed to the king; the clause reports their immediate advice.
segments.s165-g798: The subordinating 'که' introduces the content of their speech ('that / for'); 'بس نیست' is a negative predication 'is not enough'; 'فرخنده بنیاد این' is a noun phrase with adjective before noun in classical order meaning 'this auspicious foundation' (lit. 'auspicious foundation this'). The whole gives their reason: that the foundation, though auspicious, is insufficient.
segments.s165-g799: The prepositional 'از اخترشناسان' ('from the astrologers') indicates source; 'برآورد' in classical narrative can mean 'they estimated/predicted/declared' (here 'forecasted'), and 'خشم' is the object 'anger / wrath.' So this clause reports what was learned from the astrologers: a prediction of anger.
segments.s165-g800: 'دلش' = 'his heart' (دل + enclitic -ش 'his'). 'گشت' is past 'became' (intransitive 'turned into'), so 'دلش گشت پردرد' = 'his heart became full of pain / sorrowful.' The coordinate 'و پرآب چشم' literally 'and (his) eye became full of water' is an idiomatic way to say his eyes filled with tears; adjective phrases follow the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s165-g913: مرا (mara) is a dative/experiencer marker meaning 'to me' (classical Persian 'I had / to me there was'); the syntactic subject is فرِ نیکی‌دهش ('the glory that gives goodness'), followed by یار بود where بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole phrase literally says 'to me a goodness‑giving far was a companion' → 'I had a goodness‑bestowing glory as a companion.'
segments.s165-g914: خردمندی and بخت are coordinated by و 'and'; both are nominative predicates linked to the copula بود ('was'). Here بود is past/stative ('was'), so the clause means 'wisdom and (an) alert fortune were (with me).' بخت بیدار is a noun + adjective ('fortune, alert').
segments.s165-g915: برین سان is a frozen adverbial phrase 'بر این سان' = 'in this manner / thus' (بر + این + سان); یکی شارستان is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain city / one city', and ساختند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they built'. Word order places the manner first, then the object, then the verb, typical of epic narrative.
segments.s165-g916: سرش را is 'its/head‑his' (سر + pronominal clitic ش + object marker را) marking the direct object; به introduces a recipient/target (here a locus or ornament 'to/with Parvin'); پرداختند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they set/paid/placed/adorned' (classical use here 'they set/assigned/adorned'). The clause means 'they set/adorned its head with Parvin (the Pleiades)'.
segments.s165-g917: کنون 'now' begins the clause; اندرین 'in these / of these' is an adverbial phrase (classical fused form of بر این / اندر این); هم 'also' reinforces inclusion; به کار آوریم is a causative/phrasing meaning 'to put into use / to employ' with آوریم as 1st‑person singular present/future in classical style ('I will bring / I will employ'). The verb is final, as typical.
segments.s165-g918: بدو 'to it / to him' is a dative clitic + pronoun ('to it/him'); در فراوان is an intra‑phrase 'in abundant (measure)' modifying نگار آوریم, where نگار 'ornament / picture' is the object and آوریم again is 'I will bring'. The clause reads 'to it/him I will bring abundant adornments.'
segments.s165-g919: چه introduces a rhetorical question/exclamation 'what/why'; بندی is a verbal form (subjunctive/optative) from بستن 'to bind' and here functions as 'would you bind / bind'; دل is the object 'the heart'; اندر سرای سپنج is a prepositional phrase 'within the house of the sponge' (اندر = in, سرای = house/abode, سپنج = sponge) giving the location. The clause asks rhetorically why one would fix the heart in such a place.
segments.s165-g920: This is a pair of parallel rhetorical questions: چه + noun + به + noun. چه یازی به رنج = 'what (rejoicing/play) is there in suffering' and چه نازی به گنج = 'what coquettishness/delight in treasure'. چه introduces each question, یازی and نازی are nouns (poetic) indicating 'rejoicing / display' and 'coquettishness/delicacy' respectively, each governed by به + a noun (رنج, گنج). The construction contrasts suffering and wealth.
segments.s165-g753: بیامد is a past-tense, third-person singular verb ('came'); Persian often omits the explicit subject here. سوی + noun is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward/to the ...'). پادشاهی خویش is a possessed noun phrase: پادشاهی 'kingdom' + خویش (here split into خوی + ش) as the enclitic possessive 'his/own'. Word order is verb → direction phrase → possessed noun phrase.
segments.s165-g754: سپاه is the noun 'the army/troops' (subject or coordinated subject). از پسِ پشت is a stacked, somewhat emphatic locative: از 'from' + پس (here 'after/behind') + پشت 'rear' meaning 'from far behind'. و links the second phrase; پیران ز پیش means 'the elders (pīrān) from the front', where ز is the poetic short form of از. The clause lists two contingents and their directions.
segments.s165-g755: بران is an archaic/poetic adverb/demonstrative meaning roughly 'thereupon' or 'on that account'. مرز و بوم 'border and land' is a paired noun phrase ('frontiers and homeland'). اندر آگه شدند is a passive/anticausative construction: اندر 'in/throughout', آگه 'aware', شدند 'they became' — literally 'became aware throughout the land.'
segments.s165-g756: بزرگان 'the great ones' (nobles) is the subject. به راه شهنشه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/on the road to the shahanshah (king of kings)', where شهنشه is a title. شدند is the past plural verb 'they went/they set out' or 'they became [on the way]' — here meaning they departed for the king. The structure is subject → direction phrase → verb.
segments.s165-g757: به شادی دل is an instrument/cause phrase: به 'with/with regard to' + شادی 'joy' + دل 'heart' — literally 'with hearts full of joy'. از جای برخاستند is a fixed phrase 'they rose from their places' (برخاستند past plural 'they rose/stood up'), indicating people stood up from where they were seated.
segments.s165-g758: جهانی here means 'the whole/worldwide' (adverbial or collective 'all around'). به آیین is 'according to/with (ceremonial) custom' (آیین 'ceremony/rite'), and بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned/they arrayed' (بی‑ prefix + آراستن). The clause says they ceremonially dressed/arranged the entire realm.
segments.s165-g759: ازان پادشاهی is a poetic locative 'from that kingdom' (ازان = از + آن). خروشی is a deverbal/noun form 'a clamor/uproar' (‑ی noun suffix), and بخاست is the past singular intransitive 'rose up / arose' — together 'a clamor arose from that kingdom.' Word order places the source phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s165-g760: تو is the pronoun 'you' (addressing someone). گفتی here would normally be the past of گفتن 'you said', but the provided sense list does not include the speech-verb sense, so its sense is marked as not listed (see senses). زمین گشت is 'the earth turned/became' (گشت = turned/became). با چرخ راست is a poetic phrase 'with the wheel [of fate/heaven] right/straight' — چرخ often denotes the sky or the wheel of fortune in poetry, and راست 'right/true/straight' qualifies it. The clause therefore reproduces a quoted or asserted statement.
segments.s165-g1009: کزانجا is an adverbial deictic 'from there/thence' placed before the imperative. برو is an imperative 'go'. تا + به + noun phrase marks the extent/destination ('as far as/to the Sea of China'); تا here means 'as far as' and به introduces the goal دریای چین.
segments.s165-g1010: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that/thereupon'. گذر کن is an imperative compound where گذر (pass/passage) takes the auxiliary/imperative کن 'do/make' to mean 'pass on'. به introduces the destination 'مکران زمین' (the land/region of Makran).
segments.s165-g1011: همی here marks continued or habitual action ('go on/keep'), and رو is an archaic verbal form 'go' (imperative/imperative-like) following همی; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. تا + سر مرز هند gives the limit 'as far as the edge/top of the Indian frontier', with سر meaning 'edge/top'.
segments.s165-g1012: وزانجا is a conjunctive adverb 'and/from there' linking to the previous clause. گذر کن is again the imperative 'pass (on)'. به + دریای سند marks the destination 'to the Sea of Sind (Indus)'.
segments.s165-g1013: همه ... سراسر forms an intensifying frame 'throughout the whole/entire country'. باژ 'cry' is the thing to be proclaimed, and بخواه is an imperative 'demand/ask/bid' (here 'proclaim' or 'send out'). Word order places the scope words (همه ... سراسر) around کشور.
segments.s165-g1014: بگستر is an imperative 'expand/spread'. به introduces the target 'to the border of the Caspian (مرز خزر)'. در سپاه is a locative phrase 'in/within the army' modifying how or where it should be spread.
segments.s165-g1015: برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'. خروش is the subject 'a roar/cry'. از + در پهلوان is a source expression: از marks origin and در here means 'door/gate' (not the preposition), so 'from the hero's door/gate'.
segments.s165-g1016: ز بانگ is another source phrase 'from the shout/cry'. تبیره is a noun whose precise literary sense is uncertain here (the provided sense-list doesn't supply the specialized meaning), so it functions as the particular shout or cry-name; زمین شد نوان is a predicative clause 'the land became new/renewed' with شد as the past/auxiliary 'became' and نوان meaning 'new/renewed'.
segments.s165-g1121: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing list items. یاقوت (noun) is 'ruby' and is coordinated with the following item by و 'and'. زگوهر repeats the preposition in contraction (ز + گوهر) meaning 'of gem(s)', and شاهوار is an adjective after the noun giving a lofty/regal quality ('fit for a king'); in classical Persian adjectives commonly follow or stand after the noun they qualify.
segments.s165-g1122: The initial ز again marks 'of/from' and introduces دینار ('dinar, coin' — here a mass/collective sense: money). وز is a fused form representing vieux poetic وَ + ز ('and of'), an archaic/poetic sequence; the supplied sense list has no direct match, so its specific lexical entry is irregular here. تاج گوهرنگار is a noun + postpositive adjective: 'a crown (تاج) that is گوهرنگار (jewel‑studded)'.
segments.s165-g1123: ز introduces another list item 'of/besides'. دیبا and اسپان are coordinated ('brocade and horses/steeds'). به here introduces the target or purpose ('for/for use on') and precedes زین پلنگ, a genitive phrase 'saddle (زین) of/covered with leopard (پلنگ)' — note classical Persian often uses به + noun phrase to indicate the object/recipient of an item.
segments.s165-g1124: به again introduces additional items being listed (here 'a golden ...'). زرین is an adjective 'golden' placed before ستام; the exact literary noun ستام is rare/ambiguous in the supplied senses (the catalogue gives 'praised/praise'), but in context it functions as a noun meaning a piece of armor or precious fitting (treated here as 'cuirass/ornament'). و links the next noun phrase جناغ خدنگ, a noun + qualifier construction where جناغ ('breast/sternum') qualifies خدنگ ('dagger'), i.e. a dagger associated with the breast — idiomatically 'a dagger at the chest'.
segments.s165-g1125: فرنگیس را uses را as the definite object/recipient marker (here marking the person receiving the gifts). افسر and گوشوار are two direct objects (helmet and earring) coordinated by و. In narrative Persian the recipient marked by را can stand before the objects that are given.
segments.s165-g1126: همان is a demonstrative/adverb meaning 'the same' or 'likewise' and here links a second pair of gifts to the first. یاره in the supplied senses can mean 'beloved/companion' or 'follower/attendant'; in this passage it functions as a noun meaning an attendant/companion given with the gifts (the lexical entry is somewhat ambiguous). و links طوق ('necklace/collar') which is qualified by گوهرنگار, a postpositive adjective meaning 'jewel‑studded' (goher‑ + نگار).
segments.s165-g1127: بداد here is a classical past tense verb meaning 'he gave' (داد with a prefixed ب- is narrative), but the supplied sense list only contained 'oppression', so no exact sense entry matched (marked below as -1). بیامد is the past 'came' (he came), and بسوی is the preposition 'toward'. ختن in this context is a toponym (Khotan) — the annotator's sense list supplied only the verb 'circumcise', so the place‑name sense is not listed and is marked -1 below.
segments.s165-g1128: همی is the archaic/progressive particle often used in epic narrative to mark continuous or emphatic action ('then/thereupon' or progressive 'was/would'). رای here means 'plan/counsel' and forms the object of زد; the compound رای زد is an idiom 'to give counsel / to deliberate' — زد is a light verb here (the supplied senses are mainly 'strike/attack/sound' and do not match this idiomatic use, so it is marked -1). شاد is an adjective/adverb 'joyful/merry' modifying the action, and با + انجمن 'with the assembly' indicates accompaniment (با = with; انجمن = assembly/meeting).
segments.s165-g713: گرفتند is a past-tense 3rd-person plural verb meaning “they took/seized.” هر دو follows the verb and emphasizes that the action was done by both persons (“both of them”). برو آفرین is an interjected exclamation: برو is an imperative “go” and آفرین is a laudatory interjection “bravo.” Together they report what the two cried out as they seized him.
segments.s165-g714: که‌ای here functions as the poetic/vocative particle (originally که ای) addressing someone: “O …”. مهتر is an honorific ‘chief/headman,’ و is the coordinating conjunction “and,” شهریار is “king/sovereign,” and زمین is used as “land/earth.” The whole phrase is a vocative: “O chief and sovereign of the land.”
segments.s165-g715: همیشه is an adverb “always/ever.” ت + را together represent the object pronoun ‘to you’ (ت is the 2nd-person element; را here is the object marker written separately), so ترا = “to you.” جاودان باد is an optative formula: باد is the optative particle “may it be,” with جاودان “everlasting” modifying روز “day.” The phrase literally reads “Always may your day be everlasting.”
segments.s165-g716: به شادی is a prepositional phrase “for/to joy,” here functioning as a benediction (“may joy be yours”). و links the two wishes. بدخواه را has the object-marker را marking the definite ‘ill‑wisher’; پشت کوز is a locative phrase “(at) the back of the jar/with his back to the jar.” The clause is elliptical: an implied optative verb (باد) governs both parts, so it means roughly “may joy be yours, and may the ill-wisher be at the back of the water-jar” (i.e. put to shame/kept away).
segments.s165-g717: وزان = و + زان, a connective meaning “and/from that” linking to the prior scene; جایگه is “place.” بازگشتند is past 3rd-person plural “they returned.” شاد follows as a predicate/adjectival complement describing their state “(they were) glad.” The sequence gives: “And from that place they returned, glad.”
segments.s165-g718: بسی is an adverb “much/very.” از جهاندار is a prepositional phrase “of/from the sovereign” (i.e. concerning the king). کردند یاد is a common classical Persian idiom where کردن + یاد = “made remembrance” → “they remembered” or “they commemorated.” Word order places the quantity and object before the verbal idiom: “they remembered the sovereign greatly.”
segments.s165-g719: چنین likewise/thus and نیز “also” open the statement. یک سال is the time expression “one year” (i.e. another year). گردان is a participial adjective “turning/revolving” modifying سپهر “sky/heaven” — a poetic epithet (literally “the revolving heaven”); together they situate the year under the motion of the heavens.
segments.s165-g720: همی + گشت is a classical imperfective/past-continuative construction: همی “continually” with گشت (past of گشتن) gives “kept turning / passed on.” بیدار is adjectival “awake/alert.” بر before the nouns is a prepositional/relational marker meaning “toward/on behalf of,” so بر داد و مهر means “toward justice and kindness.” Together: “(the heavens) kept turning, alert toward justice and kindness.”
segments.s165-g969: بسی is a quantifier placed before the noun phrase meaning 'much' or 'great' (here 'many/great'). غارت is the noun 'plunder'. The coordination و links that noun to the following verbal idea بردن خواسته, where بردن is the infinitive 'to take/carry off' and خواسته (literally 'desired') functions as a nominal or participial complement ('what was desired' or 'desired things').
segments.s165-g970: پراگندن is the verbal infinitive 'to scatter' (here describing the action done to the treasure). گنج is the direct object 'treasure', and آراسته is a past-participial/adjective 'adorned' placed after the noun (typical Persian adjective order). The phrase therefore reads 'scattering the adorned treasure.'
segments.s165-g971: بسا is a frequent/quantifying word 'many; often' placed before the noun کشورا ('lands/countries'). کان here means 'as if' (a comparative/likening particle). به پای is a locative expression 'at the feet of' and ستور is 'steed/horse' (here plural idea 'under horses' hooves'). The whole segment gives a simile: 'many lands as though at the horses' feet.'
segments.s165-g972: بکوبند is third-person plural present (subjunctive/indicative mood in classical style) of کوبیدن 'to strike/trample' — 'they trample/beat'. The conjunction و links to گردَد, here 'گردد' is a third-person singular present of گشتن/'to become' meaning 'it becomes/turns'. The prepositional phrase به جوی آب شور 'into a stream of salty (briny) water' gives the result state; شور is an adjectival noun 'saltiness/brine' postpositive to آب.
segments.s165-g973: از introduces the source 'from Iran and Turan' (ایران و توران). برآید is a classic third-person singular present/imperf. form meaning 'arises' or 'comes up'. خروش (the noun 'cry/uproar') appears after the verb by stylistic inversion common in epic verse: 'from Iran and Turan a cry arises.'
segments.s165-g974: جهانی here qualifies the scope of the uproar as 'worldwide' or 'universal'. ز (poetic variant of از) marks origin 'from', خون من is the genitive 'my blood'. آید به جوش is an idiomatic combination: آید 'comes' + به جوش 'to a boil/surge', so the phrase means '(a) worldwide (uproar) comes surging from my blood.'
segments.s165-g975: جهاندار 'the world-ruler/sovereign' is the subject. بر چرخ 'upon the wheel/sky' is a locative phrase — in poetic Persian 'چرخ' often means heaven or fate. چونین 'thus' modifies نوشت; نوشت is past tense 'wrote/inscribed'. The clause reads 'the sovereign inscribed thus upon the heavens.'
segments.s165-g976: به فرمان او 'by his command' is an instrumental/agent phrase. بردهد is a present/subjunctive third-person form meaning 'he gives/bestows' (classical epic usage). هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever/whichever', and کشت here is a nominal sense 'that which is sown/cultivation' (not 'killing' in this context). Together: 'by his command he bestows whatever is sown.'
segments.s165-g985: 'چنین گفت' is a formulaic, archaic word-order meaning 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus' precedes the verb). 'کز' introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'that which'). 'من' is the subject of the embedded clause and 'بد آمد به من' is a light-verb construction meaning 'an ill/bad thing happened to me' (آمد here = 'befell/happened', and 'به من' is the dative 'to me').
segments.s165-g986: گر ('if') begins a conditional clause. او راست گوید همی = 'if he speaks truly' — گوید is a present/optative-form verb (classical Persian present) meaning 'says / speaks' and همی is an archaic durative/emphatic particle marking continuity or insistence. این سخن ('these words' / 'this speech') is the object/topic of 'گوید' or the thing being referred to by the condition.
segments.s165-g987: ورا is an older/poetic pronoun meaning 'him/it' or 'thither', functioning as the direct object here. 'من' is the speaker/agent and 'کشیده' is a past/perfect participle or perfective verb form meaning 'have drawn/taken/dragged'. 'به توران زمین' is the goal phrase ('into the land of Turan'), with به marking direction and زمین = 'land, country'. Word order (object–subject–verb–prepositional goal) is typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s165-g988: پراگندم is first-person perfect/past ('I scattered' / 'I spread'). اندر جهان is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('in the world'). تخم کین is a genitive/nominal phrase 'seed of hatred' (تخم = 'seed, origin'; کین = 'hatred, vengeance'), serving as the direct object of 'پراگندم'. The clause says the agent spread the seed of enmity through the world.
segments.s165-g989: شمردم is a first-person past verb 'I counted/considered'. همه modifies the following noun phrase. باد here is literal 'wind' used metaphorically: شمردم ... باد = 'I regarded ... as wind' (i.e., as worthless or empty). گفتار شاه is a genitive phrase 'the king's speech/words' functioning as the thing being regarded.
segments.s165-g990: چنین هم همی گفت is a stacked set of adverbial/emphatic particles plus the verb: چنین = 'thus', هم = 'also/even', همی = an archaic durative/emphatic particle, and گفت (verb) = 'he said/spoke'. با من پگاه = 'with me at dawn' (با = 'with', من = 'me', پگاه = 'dawn/early morning'), an adverbial phrase specifying time and interlocutor.
segments.s165-g991: وزان پس is a temporal/discourse connective: وزان = 'and from that' / 'thereupon', پس = 'then/after'. چنین گفت = 'he thus said' (چنین + verb). با دل به مهر is a prepositional phrase describing manner: با دل = 'with (his) heart' and به مهر = 'toward/with affection/love' (مهر = 'love/affection'), so 'with a loving heart' or 'affectionately'.
segments.s165-g992: که introduces the content clause ('that' / 'for'), followed by an ablative/causal phrase از جنبش و راز گردان سپهر. از = 'from' / 'because of', جنبش = 'movement, motion', و راز = 'and secret/mystery', گردان سپهر = 'the revolving/turning sky/heavens' (گردان here adjectival/participial = 'revolving'). The whole phrase gives the purported cause/source (the motions and hidden revolutions of the heavens).
segments.s165-g1041: هزار is the cardinal numeral 'a thousand' preceding the noun اشتر (here contextually 'camels'); the following words بختی سرخ موی form a post‑nominal descriptive phrase: بختی (an adjective or name) and the compound adjective سرخ‌موی 'red‑haired / reddish'. In Classical Persian modifiers like سرخ‌موی typically follow the head noun to describe its appearance. Note: the supplied lemma list labels اشتر as a personal name, but in this line it functions as the common noun 'camel'.
segments.s165-g1042: بنه is a noun 'bundle / bale' (the thing being loaded). بر نهادند is the compound of preposition بر 'upon' + نهادند (3rd pl. past of نهادن) 'they placed / they set on', so the predicate means 'they placed upon (the camels) bales'. با introduces an instrumental/qualitative phrase 'with', and رنگ و بوی is a coordinated noun phrase 'color and scent' describing the bales. Word order: object (بنه) + particle (بر) + verb (نهادند), then adjunct (با ...).
segments.s165-g1043: از ... ایران و توران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran and Turan' (از + place names). گزیده is a past‑participle/adjective 'chosen / select' modifying سوار 'rider / horseman'; Persian allows the participial adjective before or after the noun, here it directly qualifies سوار to mean 'elite horsemen.'
segments.s165-g1044: برفتند is the 3rd person plural past of رفتن with the archaic prefix بر- (here meaning 'they set out / rode off'); it is the finite verb of the clause. شمشیرزن is a noun 'swordsman' functioning appositionally with the numeral phrase ده هزار (ten thousand) which gives the number of those who went; numerals often appear after or before the counted noun in poetic prose.
segments.s165-g1045: به پیش سپاه is a prepositional locative phrase 'before the army / in the army's presence' (به + پیش + سپاه). اندرون is 'the inner place / harem / inner apartments' and خواسته is the past participle of خواستن used here to mean 'having requested / summoned' (literally 'asked for the inner [people]'); together the phrase conveys 'they had the inner (people) brought before the army.'
segments.s165-g1046: عماری is a plural/collective noun meaning 'dwellings / buildings' (here 'arranged/constructed dwellings'); و links the next noun خوبان 'the fair / beautiful ones' (i.e. beloveds or beautiful people). آراسته is a past participle/adjective 'adorned / made handsome' describing both things enumerated. The sequence shows result: dwellings arranged and beauties adorned.
segments.s165-g1047: ز ... و ز ... is a poetic repetition of the preposition 'from / of' (ز) introducing a list of valuable materials: یاقوت 'ruby' and گوهر 'gem / jewel'. شاهوار is an adjectival epithet 'regal / lofty' applied to گوهر (or the whole list), placed after the noun as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s165-g1048: چه introduces an exclamatory enumerative clause 'what of / how about' and is followed by a series of items introduced with از or the fused وز- form: از طوق 'of necklaces', و ز تاج 'and of crowns', وزگوشوار 'and of earrings' (وزگوشوار is a fused poetic form combining و + ز + گوشوار). The phrase functions to amplify the list of luxuries with an exclamatory tone.
segments.s165-g617: دو تا = a numeral/adverbial phrase meaning “two times” or “twice.” گشت is the simple past of گشتن (here “turned” / “moved”); the verb precedes the subject in this narrative line. پیران is the subject (Piran). و links the first action to the next clause. بردش is برد + enclitic -ش (him/it) and here functions as a transitive past (“took/led him”); نماز is the object “prayer.” The clause therefore strings two actions: Piran made two turns/movements and took him to prayer.
segments.s165-g618: بسی is an adverb meaning “very / greatly.” آفرین here is an exclamation/noun “praise, acclaim.” کرد is the simple past auxiliary/verb “did / made” so آفرین کرد = “praised” or “gave acclaim.” و connects this clause to the next. برگشت is past “returned” and باز is an adverb “again / back,” giving “returned again” or simply “returned.”
segments.s165-g619: به نزد = a prepositional phrase meaning “to / in the presence of.” سیاوش is the addressee (Siavash). خرامید is the simple past of خرامیدن (“strode/proudly advanced”); the action is modified by the adverb زود “quickly/at once,” which follows the verb for emphasis. The subject is understood from the preceding lines (Piran).
segments.s165-g620: برو here functions as a motion verb “went / went off” in the narrative (not an imperative). بر is the verbal prefix combined with شمرد to form برشمرد (“recounted / enumerated / reported”); the prefix is fused to the verb. آن کجا رفته بود is a reported-content clause: آن = “that/the,” کجا = “where,” رفته بود is the past perfect (had gone) formed with رفته + بود (auxiliary), so the whole means “(he) reported where (he) had gone.”
segments.s165-g621: نشستند is the past plural of نشستن “they sat (down)”—in narrative it comes first followed by adverbial/nominal complements. شادان دل = “with joyful heart(s)” (شادان modifies دل). آن شب is the time adverbial “that night.” بهم = “together / with one another,” indicating mutual company. The whole clause: they sat together that night, joyful in heart.
segments.s165-g622: به باده = instrumental phrase “by/with wine” (به + noun used to mark means). بشستند is the past plural of شستن “they washed” and takes جان را as its definite object (را marks the object): جان را = “the soul / life.” ز غم = “from grief/sorrow,” marking the source being washed away. So literally: “with wine they washed the soul from sorrow.”
segments.s165-g623: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” خورشید is the subject “the sun.” از چرخ گردنده is a prepositional phrase “from the revolving wheel/sky,” where گردنده is a participle “revolving” modifying چرخ. سر is the noun “head,” the object of the following verb (i.e., “lifted its head”). The word order places the temporal clause before the verbs that follow.
segments.s165-g624: برآورد is a past verbal form from برآوردن meaning “raised / produced” (here, the sun ‘raised’ its head). برسان here is a verb meaning “bring / send” (it puts the action of sending/bringing that follows); together the verbs describe the sun’s action and its effect. زرین is an adjective “golden,” modifying سپر “shield.” The image is poetic: the sun, lifting its head, brings forth a golden shield of light.
segments.s165-g585: کنون is an adverb meaning 'now' placed at the sentence start. باورم شد is a light Verbal Phrase: باور (my belief) with the possessive -م plus شد 'became', literally 'my belief became', an idiom meaning 'I came to believe / I was convinced' (شد marks the change of state in the past). که introduces the reported clause that follows; او این بگفت is the subordinate reported speech (او = 'he', این = 'this', بگفت = archaic past 'said').
segments.s165-g586: که begins a subordinate (here indirectly interrogative/relative) clause: 'what ...'. گردون گردان is a compound description where گردان ('revolving') modifies گردون ('the sky/heaven'). چه introduces the interrogative 'what'. دارد is the present/past-state verb 'has' (classical narrative present), and نهفت is an adjective/past participle 'hidden, concealed' — together دارد نهفت = 'has hidden / conceals'.
segments.s165-g587: چرا is the interrogative/rhetorical 'why'. کشت here is a deverbal noun meaning 'planting/cultivation' (classical order places it before the modal). باید is the modal 'must/should', so کشت باید = 'should one plant'. درختی is an indefinite object 'a tree' (the -ی gives indefiniteness). به دست is an adverbial phrase 'by hand' with به as the instrumental/agent marker (here 'by') and دست 'hand'.
segments.s165-g588: که here is a relative/explanatory 'which/that' linking back to 'a tree': 'a tree that ...'. بارش is بار + pronominal -ش 'its produce/fruit/burden' (here 'its fruit'), بود is the past copula 'was', so بارش بود زهر = 'its fruit was poison'. و = 'and'. برگش is برگ + -ش 'its leaf/leafage' (classical form), followed by کبست, an obscure/archaic noun/adjective describing the state of the leaves; the line gives two bad qualities of the tree (poisonous fruit and something bad about its leaves).
segments.s165-g589: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کاووس is the proper name 'Kavus'. وز here should be read as the classical juxtaposition 'و ز' (= 'and from'), so it functions as a coordinated prepositional element introducing the second source; the printed single token reflects that contraction. تخم is 'seed' and افراسیاب is the name 'Afrasiab' (genitive relation: 'the seed of Afrasiab').
segments.s165-g590: چو is the comparative/simile particle 'as/like'. آتش بود تیز is a clause 'it was (as) fire, sharp' with بود as the copula and تیز as the predicate adjective 'sharp/keen'. یا introduces an alternative 'or', followed by موج آب 'a wave of water' (موج = 'wave', آب = 'water'), completing the simile: 'as fire, sharp, or as a wave of water.'
