segments.s165-g1197: همان is a demonstrative 'the same/that' applied to the following list. The nouns are coordinated with و: گوهر (jewel), تخت (throne), and دیبای چین (diبای = brocade/silk; چین = fold/pleat — i.e., brocaded/pleated silk). Word order is determiner + noun + conjunction + noun, a straightforward noun list.
segments.s165-g1198: The repeated همان again marks 'the same' before a second coordinated list: یاره (companion/attendant), گرز (mace/club), تیغ (sword), نگین (gem or signet). Each noun is joined by و. یاره here refers to a follower/attendant rather than a romantic 'beloved' given the martial list.
segments.s165-g1199: ز هنا is the preposition 'from/of' introducing the domain to inspect: گستردنیها (the spread-out things/displays — plural formed with -ها from the participial/adjectival stem گستردنی) و از بوی و رنگ 'and from scent and color' — i.e. by appearance and smell. The sequence invites inspection of visual and olfactory qualities.
segments.s165-g1200: ببین is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'see/look.' تا here introduces the purpose/result 'so that/to' (classical use). ز گنجت 'from your treasure' locates the source; چه آید 'what comes' — چه = 'what', آید = present 'comes'; به چنگ is an idiom 'into the grasp' (به + چنگ), so the clause literally asks you to 'see what from your treasure comes into your grasp.'
segments.s165-g393: This is a nominal clause with the subject name 'سیاوش' followed by a place/name 'هیمدون' and the predicative phrase 'به نخچیر بود' (literally ‘was in/for hunting’). The verb بود is the past copula (here sense 'was') placed at the end as in classical Persian; 'به' marks the activity/purpose ('on a hunt') and 'نخچیر' is the object/semantic complement 'game, prey'. 'هیمدون' functions as a locative/proper-name adjunct to the subject.
segments.s165-g394: همی is an adverbial marker common in epic Persian indicating a continuing or habitual action ('kept on / was in the act of'). تاخت is a past tense verb 'galloped/rushed'; the conjunction و links the next verb افگند 'threw/caused to fall'. در introduces the locative phrase 'دشت گور' ('in the Plain of Gūr'), where گور here names the plain (likely 'onager/plain' sense).
segments.s165-g395: A serial list of destinations introduced by repeated preposition به ('into/toward'): 'به غار و به کوه و به هامون'. The final verb بتاخت is a past tense/preterite of 'to gallop/charge' (third person); the prefixed ب- is the classical verbal form seen in epic Persian. Each 'به' marks direction or motion into those places.
segments.s165-g396: This is an instrumental/listing phrase: 'بشمشیر و تیر و بنیزه' = 'with sword and arrow and spear'. The final verb بیاخت (here a past-form verb) indicates engagement/meeting in combat with those implements; in epic usage it functions like 'engaged/fought with'. The instruments precede the verb in typical Persian order.
segments.s165-g397: به هر جایگه is a locative phrase 'at every place/spot'. بر یکی توده کرد is a causative/result clause: بر یکی (onto one) توده (a heap) کرد ('he made/piled into one heap'). کرد is the past tense of 'to do/make' taking توده as the object/goal of the action.
segments.s165-g398: سپه را is the definite object 'the army' marked by را. ز introduces the source 'from the prey/spoils' (ز نخچیر), and آسوده کرد is a causative phrase 'made (them) at ease / relieved (them)'. Together it reads 'he relieved the army from the (problem or burden of) prey/booty.'
segments.s165-g399: وزان جایگه is a connective locative expression 'and/from that place'. سوی introduces the direction 'toward' and 'ایوان شاه' is a possessive noun phrase 'the king's iwan/hall' (the possessor follows the noun in Persian). The whole phrase specifies movement toward the king's porch.
segments.s165-g400: همه شاد دل is an adverbial circumstantial phrase 'all, happy in heart' (شاد modifies دل). برگرفتند is the past plural verb 'they took up / set out (took up)'; راه is the object 'the road/way'. In Persian the circumstantial phrase precedes the verb, so 'they, all joyful in heart, took the road.'
segments.s165-g1065: 'ز دیگر سو' is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'from/on the other side' with ز as an older/poetic preposition. 'افراسیاب و سپاه' is a coordinated noun phrase ('Afrasiab and his host'); no finite verb appears here, so this phrase functions as the topical/subject part of the clause.
segments.s165-g1066: چو is used here in the comparative sense 'like/as'. پیران is the name Pīrān (a person) rather than the common noun 'elders' (sense list does not give a proper-name sense, so index is -1). The phrase 'پیران و گرسیوز کینه‌خواه' describes those figures as 'kineh-khah' (revenge-seeking/vengeful), with کینه‌خواه functioning as an adjectival/epithet.
segments.s165-g1067: بهر (بِه + ر) here means 'for / at / in' and introduces the locative 'گوشه‌ای' ('a corner'). 'گنبدی ساخته' is 'built a domed (structure)': گنبدی is an adjective/descriptor 'domed' and ساخته is the past participle/verb 'made/built' used predicatively (classical Persian often omits an explicit subject).
segments.s165-g1068: سرش را is the object phrase: سر 'summit/top' + ـش (possessive clitic 'his/its') and را marks it as definite/direct object. به ابراندر is an adverbial phrase 'into/among the clouds' (ابراندر = 'in the clouds'), and افراخته is the past participle 'raised/erected' describing the action performed on the object. The construction means 'he raised its summit into the clouds.'
segments.s165-g1069: نشسته is a participle/adjective 'seated' placed before the noun as a descriptive clause. 'سراینده رامشگران' is a genitive construction: سراینده 'singer/poet' with رامشگران ('of the peacemakers') functioning as the possessor/qualifier; classical word order often places the modifier after the head.
segments.s165-g1070: This compact phrase uses nominal compounds and repetition for emphasis. سر here means 'chief/leader'; اندر = 'among/in'; ستاره is used in the sense of 'luminary/famous person' (a metaphorical 'stars'); the repeated سران سران is a piled genitive meaning 'head of heads' or 'leader of leaders' (intensifying the rank).
segments.s165-g1071: The order is somewhat inverted: 'سیاووش گردش نهادند نام' = '(They) set (نهادند, 3rd pl. past) the name of Siyâvash (نام سیاووش) in گردش (circulation/turn)'. گردش here is used metaphorically for 'circulation/spread' and نهادند is the past tense verb 'they placed/set'.
segments.s165-g1072: همه شهر = 'the whole city / all the townspeople' (همه as 'all/everyone' modifying شهر). زان = 'from that / because of that'; شارستان here means 'that citadel/city-quarter' and شادکام is an adjective 'fortunate/joyful'. The phrase states that the entire city was joyful because of that place/event.
segments.s165-g705: This is a noun series followed by the past‑tense verb یافتی (2nd person singular). The coordinated list می‌و خوان و خوالیگران (noun + وَ + noun) comes before the verb, a common Persian word order (objects before verb). یافتی is the simple past 'you found/obtained' and ties the whole coordinated direct object to the subject implied in the previous line.
segments.s165-g706: بخوردی is a past (2nd‑singular) verb meaning 'you ate/drank/partook'. The clause continues with و 'and', then هرچند 'although/however much' introducing a concessive subordinate idea, and برتافتی (past 2sg) 'you vaunted/exulted' (an intransitive sense). Note the poetic terse linking: the concessive has no explicit main clause following here — it connects to what follows in the narrative.
segments.s165-g707: ببردی is a past (2nd‑singular) verb meaning 'you carried off / you won / you took away'. It is followed by و 'and' and رفتی 'you went'. سوی is a preposition 'toward' placed before the noun phrase خان خویش 'your khan/your lord'; خویش is the reflexive/possessive element (here realized as خوی + ش in the segment list), forming 'his/your own'. The whole clause is a sequence of actions by the same subject (you).
segments.s165-g708: This short nominal/clausal sequence states a result or state after arrival. بدی here is an archaic/poetic form functioning as a past copula ('was' / 'became' / 'you made him'), linking to the predicate شاد 'glad'. The following یک هفته مهمان خویش is a time phrase + noun phrase 'a guest of yours for one week' where خویش + ش again mark possession. The clause is elliptical and characteristic of poetic compression.
segments.s165-g709: در بسته زندانها is a genitival/locative sequence 'the closed doors of the prisons' (در + بسته + زندانها), with بسته as an adjective/participial modifier of در. برگشاد is a perfective/past participle 'opened' (used here as the predicate). The order places the descriptive noun phrase before the predicate, a frequent poetic arrangement.
segments.s165-g710: ازو is the archaic contraction of از + او 'because of him / on his account'. شادمان بخت is a compact clause where بخت 'fortune' is understood as subject and شادمان 'rejoiced / was glad' as predicate. The conjunction و links that to او نیز شاد 'and he also was happy' — a straightforward coordination contrasting the abstract 'fortune' with the person.
segments.s165-g711: به هشتم is a temporal prepositional phrase 'on the eighth (day)'; Persian often omits 'روز' (day) in such phrases. سیاوش is the subject proper name and بیامد is the past tense 'came'. پگاه 'at dawn' is an adverbial time expression placed after the verb. The sequence follows a normal temporal + subject + verb + adverbial ordering.
segments.s165-g712: This is a short motion clause: the place/name compound اباگرد پیران functions as a name phrase (Pîrân of Abâgard) and به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near the king' expressing approach. In Persian به + نزدیک + noun indicates movement or approach toward that noun.
segments.s165-g881: درازی (درازی) = 'length' (here definite/possessive implied by the following پهناش). و is the coordinating 'and' linking the two dimensions. پهناش is پهنا + -ش ('its breadth'), so the phrase names 'its length and its breadth.' سی بار سی is a numeric phrase 'thirty by thirty' (lit. 'thirty times thirty') describing those dimensions.
segments.s165-g882: بود‌گر is a condensed conditional: بود (would be) + گر (if), so 'it would be if…'. بپیمایدش is the poetic/preverbal b- + پیماید (subjunctive/optative 3sg of پیمودن) + -ش (object 'it') meaning 'were to traverse/measure it.' پارسی here is a person from Pars (a Persian) and is the agent of the conditional action.
segments.s165-g883: یک و نیم is the numeral 'one and a half' where و links whole and fraction. فرسنگ is the unit 'farsang/parasang.' بالای کوه is a locative phrase 'above the mountain' (بالای = 'above/on top of', followed by the noun کوه).
segments.s165-g884: که introduces a relative/result clause 'such that' or 'which.' از رفتنش = از + رفتنش ('from its/his going') where -ش on رفتنش refers back to the action/place; it means 'from going there.' مرد is the generic 'man' (one). گردد is present subjunctive/poetic form of 'become' (گردیدن/گشتن used as 'become'), and ستوه is the predicative adjective 'weary.' Together: '…such that a man becomes weary from going (there).'
segments.s165-g885: وزان is a compound connective (و + زان) meaning 'and from that' or 'and from it.' روی هامونی is a noun phrase 'the face/side of the Hamun (the Hamun plain)' with هامونی (هامون + -ی) as identifying adjective/name. آید پدید is a poetic word order for 'presents itself/appears' (آید = 'comes', پدید = 'forth/visible'), i.e., 'appears.'
segments.s165-g886: کزان = از + آن (contracted) 'from which.' خوبتر جایها = comparative adjective خوبتر ('better/more beautiful') modifying جایها ('places'); note adjective precedes noun. کس ندید is a negative existential phrasing 'no one has seen' (کس here reads with the negated verb ندید to mean 'no one'). Together: 'from which no one has seen better places.'
segments.s165-g887: همه is the quantifier 'all/everything.' The nouns گلشن و باغ و ایوان are coordinated by و and list the prominent features ('flower-gardens, orchards, and porticoes'). بود at the end is the past copula 'was,' predicating the whole list.
segments.s165-g888: کش here is a poetic/linking particle introducing the extent clause (lit. 'so/so much that'); ایوانها is the plural 'the porticoes.' سر به کیوان بود is a figurative locative: سر ('top') + به ('to') + کیوان (Kayvan/Saturn, used poetically for the heavens) + بود ('was'), i.e. 'their tops reached up to Kayvan/the sky.'
segments.s165-g857: چو is the temporal/conditional conjunction 'when.' فرسنگ صد is a poetic inversion (noun before numeral) meaning 'a hundred farsangs.' گرد ... بر گرد کوه uses گرد ('around/about') with بر + گرد + کوه to express 'around the mountain' (the repetition / word-order is a poetic emphasis: 'around upon the mountain').
segments.s165-g858: ز بالای او = 'from its/top of it' (ز = 'from'). چشم is the subject meaning 'the eye' or 'sight.' گردد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of گشتن used here as 'become' (i.e., 'grows, becomes'), and ستوه is the predicate adjective 'weary.' The phrase literally: 'from its top the eye becomes weary.'
segments.s165-g859: ز هر سو که introduces a relative/conditional clause 'from every side that (one) goes toward it.' پویی here is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'you go' / 'one goes' (from پوییدن); the printed senses list does not include this verbal sense. بدو = 'to it.' راه نیست is the main clause 'there is no road/way.'
segments.s165-g860: همه گرد بر گرد او = 'all around him/it' (repetition of گرد emphasizes encirclement). در یکیست is a copular clause 'is in one' meaning 'is the same / is one and the same everywhere.' The whole line restates that every side around the mountain is identical (no passage).
segments.s165-g861: بدین = به این ('at/with this') in compressed poetic form (the segment is split in the word list). کوه = 'mountain.' بینی is the present 2nd/impersonal 'you see' / 'one sees' (classical usage). دو فرسنگ تنگ = a narrow width of two farsangs (تنگ 'narrow'). The phrase describes the visible narrow pass at that mountain.
segments.s165-g862: ازین روی = از این روی 'from this side'; زان روی = ز آن روی 'and from that side.' دیوار سنگ is a noun + noun (stone wall) meaning 'a wall of stone' (سنگ postmodifies دیوار). The conjunction و links the two sides.
segments.s165-g863: بدین چند فرسنگ = 'in/within these few farsangs' (بدین = به این; چند فرسنگ = 'a few/several farsangs'). اگر introduces the condition 'if,' and پنج مرد 'five men' is the conditional subject. The phrase sets up a hypothetical small party present in that span.
segments.s165-g864: بباشد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of بودن used here in a conditional sense ('would be' / 'could be'). به راه = 'into / to a road' (i.e., to make or be on a road). از پی کارکرد is an idiomatic phrase 'by following work' = 'by exerting effort / by working at it' (کارکرد here = 'action/effort, performance'). Together: 'they could, by working, make/open a road.'
segments.s165-g529: بدو is the clitic phrase 'به + او' fronted here meaning 'to him'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (presented here as reported speech); پیران is the subject 'the elders'. که introduces the following subordinate clause ('that ...'). با روزگار is a prepositional phrase 'with/the times' that links forward to the verb in the next segment.
segments.s165-g530: نسازد is a 3rd-person singular present (poetic/archaic) copular/verbal predicate meaning 'does not fit/accord' (negative stem). خرد‌یافته is a compound participial adjective 'one who has acquired wisdom' (خرد + یافته) that functions as the affected person, and کارزار is the thing at issue 'battle/warfare'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: predicate (نسازد) precedes the noun phrase that it scopes over.
segments.s165-g531: نیابی is a present/future-oriented negative form 'you will not find/attain' (2nd person); گذر is the noun 'passage/way' and functions as the object; تو is the explicit subject 'you'. ز introduces the source 'from', and گردان سپهر 'turning/revolving heaven(s)' is the genitive/compound source phrase — 'passage from/through the revolving sky/heavens'.
segments.s165-g532: کزویست is a fused, archaic/explanatory particle phrase (کز + ویست) meaning roughly 'for/that from it is' or 'from which is'; it introduces what issues from the heavens. آرام و پرخاش و مهر is a coordinated list of nouns 'calm (آرام), assault/aggression (پرخاش), and love/affection (مهر)' — the conjunction و simply links the items. The whole segment explains what the revolving heavens yield.
segments.s165-g533: به ایران is a locative/goal phrase ('to/at/in Iran' — here meaning 'in Iran' or 'among the Iranians'); اگر introduces the conditional 'if'; دوستان is the direct object 'friends'; داشتی is the past/imperfect 2nd-person form 'you had' (used here in a counterfactual/conditional sense: 'if you had ...').
segments.s165-g534: به یزدان is the prepositional phrase 'to God' (the indirect object/recipient). سپردی is the simple past 'you entrusted/handed over'. و is the coordinating 'and'. بگذاشتی is a past form of گذاشتن used here in the sense 'you would have left/put (it down/left it behind)' — together the clause means you would have entrusted (the matter or friends) to God and departed/left it to Him.
segments.s165-g535: نشست is the past tense 'he sat down' (or 'he sat and settled'). و simply connects the next phrase. نشانت here is the bound form نِشان + ت (nishān + -t) and functions as 'a sign (to you)' or as a verb 'showed you' depending on reading; in poetry such forms can be compressed and ambiguous. کنون means 'now'. ایدرست is an archaic/exclamatory word — it can be read as a vocative 'O Just/Right one' addressing someone, or as an emphatic 'indeed/truly' depending on context; both readings are attested in the Shahnameh.
segments.s165-g536: سر تخت ایران is a genitive string 'the head/top of the throne of Iran' (سر = head/top; تخت = throne; ایران links as possessor). به دست اندرست is a poetic locative/state construction: به + دست (hand/power) + اندرست (archaic 'is within/among') meaning 'lies in the hand' or idiomatically 'is in (someone's) power/control.' The clause therefore states that the top of the Iranian throne is in (his) control.
segments.s165-g841: ز من (z-e man) is a poetic short form of از من, meaning “from me” and marks the source/speaker; بشنو is the 2nd-person singular imperative of شنیدن “listen/heed.” از here introduces the topic (about) and governs the following noun phrase گنگ دژ داستان “the story of the mute fortress.” Word order is source + imperative + topic phrase (poetic syntax).
segments.s165-g842: بدین is a prepositional/adverbial phrase (ب + این in older spelling) meaning “with this / to this”; داستان repeats the referent “story/tale.” باش is the 2nd-person singular imperative of بودن “be,” and همداستان is an adjective/noun meaning “like‑minded/in agreement (with the tale).” The clause is an exhortation: “be of one mind with this story.”
segments.s165-g843: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause (“for/since/that”). چون here means “like/as” comparing what follows to something else; گنگ دژ is the noun phrase “the mute (or silent) fortress.” در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase “in the world.” جای نیست is an existential negation: جای “place” + نیست “does not exist/is not,” giving “there is no place.”
segments.s165-g844: بدان سان is an adverbial expression meaning “in that manner/so” (i.e. “such as that”); زمینی means “on earth / in this world” (adverbial use); دلارای is an adjective meaning “charming / beloved” describing the thing just mentioned; نیست negates the existence (“does not exist”). Together: “There is no such charming thing on earth.”
segments.s165-g845: که again introduces a relative/explanatory clause (“that/which/because”). آن را is a pronominal object with the accusative/definiteness marker را (“that/it” as the object). سیاوش is the subject name. برآورده is the past participle/verb meaning “brought about / raised / set up,” and بود here is the auxiliary in the past (forming a pluperfect/remote past sense: “had raised”).
segments.s165-g846: بسی is a classical adverb meaning “much / many / very” (here “many”); اندرو = اندر + او (classical adverb) means “in it/within it”; رنجها is the plural noun “sufferings/hardships” (رنج + ها); برده بود is the compound past (برده as the past participle of بردن ‘to bear/experience’ plus بود) giving “had borne/experienced.” The clause: “he had borne many hardships in it.”
segments.s165-g847: به یک ماه is a temporal/directional phrase “for/over one month” or “a month’s (distance).” زان (زاN) means “from that/thereof” and روی here means “side/direction” (literally “from that side”). دریای چین is the place-name “the sea of China.” The whole phrase sets a month-long movement toward the Chinese sea (ellipsis continues in the next clause).
segments.s165-g848: که again introduces a clause (“for/when/that”). بی‌نام is an adjective “nameless / without a name.” بود is the past copula “was.” آن زمان و زمین literally “that time and the earth” — a poetic way to say “at that time both time and the land/earth were nameless.” Word order is standard classical Persian: predicate after the relative particle.
segments.s165-g1177: پس آنگه are successive temporal adverbs ('then/afterwards'), here reinforcing sequence; به گرسیوز marks the indirect object ('to Garsivaz') with به + name; آهسته is an adverb modifying the verb, 'softly/gently'; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (3rd sg.), with the subject omitted and understood from context.
segments.s165-g1178: نهفته همه is a noun phrase 'all that was hidden' where نهفته (participle/adjective) modifies همه; برگشاد is a perfect/passive participle 'opened/revealed' (literary/poetic past) functioning as the main predication; از نهفت is an ablative/source phrase ('from hiding'/'out of concealment') clarifying what was revealed.
segments.s165-g1179: بدو is the indirect-object clitic/phrase 'to him' (to that person); گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past) and ends the clause; رو here is an imperative/verb meaning 'go' or 'turn' (archaic short form of برو), and تا marks the goal/direction; سیاووش is the destination name, and گرد is an adverb/postpositional element 'about/around' indicating vicinity.
segments.s165-g1180: ببین is an imperative 'look/see'; تا introduces purpose/subordinate content ('so that' / 'in order that'); چه جایست is a fused interrogative clause 'what place/position it is' where جایست (جای + است) functions as 'it is a place / its position is'; بر گرد گرد is an idiomatic, repetitive adverbial phrase 'on every side / all around' (بر + گرد 'around', doubled for emphasis).
segments.s165-g1181: سیاوش is the subject; به + توران زمین is a directional/locative phrase 'to/onto the land of Turan' (توران modifies زمین); دل نهاد is a frozen verbal phrase 'placed/settled his heart' (نهاد past of نهادن 'put/placed'), meaning 'he gave his heart to / devoted himself to'—a literary idiom.
segments.s165-g1182: از ایران is the oblique object 'of/from Iran' (what he no longer remembers or cares for); نگیرد is a negative present/future-like form ('will not take' / 'does not take') used here narratively to mean 'he will take no (more)'; دگر هیچ یاد = 'any further remembrance' with دگر 'again/any more', هیچ 'no/none', and یاد 'memory/remembrance'. Together: 'he would take no further remembrance of Iran.'
segments.s165-g1183: مگر here has an adversative/exceptional sense 'except/only'; کرد is the past verb 'did / made'; پدرود تخت و کلاه is the direct object phrase 'farewell to the throne and to the helmet/crown' (پدرود = farewell, تخت = throne, کلاه in epic diction can mean helmet/crown).
segments.s165-g1184: چو is a comparative/linking particle 'as/like'; the sequence گودرز و بهرام و کاووس شاه simply lists the names 'Goudarz and Bahram and King Kavus' who did the same (elliptical: 'as Goudarz, Bahram, and King Kavus [had done]').
segments.s165-g825: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. خاکیم is a noun (خاک) plus the 1st-person-plural copular ending -یم, literally 'we are earth/dust' (a common Persian expression for being mortal). و 'and' links the clauses. باید is the modal 'must/ought'; شدن is the infinitive 'to become' used as the complement of باید; زیر خاک is a prepositional phrase ('under/beneath the earth') giving the result/place of becoming.
segments.s165-g826: همه جای is a noun phrase 'every place' or 'everywhere' (همه + جای). ترسست is written in the fused classical form (ترس + است) functioning as a predicate 'it is fear' / 'there is fear here' rather than a 2nd-person past; the following و coordinates the next nouns تیمار and باک ('care/solicitude' and 'fear/dread'), so the clause says that fear, solicitude and dread are everywhere.
segments.s165-g827: تو رفتی is simple past 'you went/you left' (2nd-person singular). و links clauses. گیتی is the subject 'the world'; بماند is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive-form meaning 'remains/continues' (in poetry it functions as a neutral present 'remains'); دراز is an adverb/adjective 'long' qualifying بماند (i.e., 'remains long' — the world endures).
segments.s165-g828: کسی ... نداند is a negative indefinite construction meaning 'no one knows' (کسی here functions as 'no one' with the negative verb). آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/evidently' modifying نداند. نداند is the classical present/optative negative form 'does not know' (3rd-person). ز introduces the object 'راز' — 'about/from the secret' (i.e., 'no one knows the secret openly').
segments.s165-g829: جهان is the subject 'the world'. سر به سر is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'throughout' or 'utterly' (a fixed poetic phrase). عبرت و حکمت‌ست is the predicate: عبرت 'lesson/moral' coordinated with حکمت (wisdom) and followed by the archaic predicate-ending -ست ('it is'), so the clause says 'the world is entirely lesson and wisdom.'
segments.s165-g830: چرا 'why' begins a question. ز + و here represent the classical contracted 'زو' (= از او / 'from it, concerning it'): ز is the preposition 'from/of' and و is the bound pronoun 'it/him/her' (orthographically written as و). همه بهر من is 'all/entirety for me' (بهر = for/toward). غفلت‌ست is the fused form غفلت + است, 'it is negligence/heedlessness' — so the clause asks why, concerning it, everything for me amounts to neglect.
segments.s165-g831: چو شد is a temporal clause 'when ... became/reached' (چو = 'when'). سال 'year/years' is the counted time. بر before a number here indicates 'upon/reaching' a given age; شست و شش is the numeral (classical spelling for sixty‑six or 'sixty and six' in the text). چاره جوی is an exhortation: چاره 'remedy' + جوی (from جستن, 'seek') — 'seek a remedy' (جوی functions as a verbal directive 'seek').
segments.s165-g832: ز بیشی means 'against/from excess' (ز = 'from/of', بیشی = 'excess/surplus'). و connects the next phrase. از رنج 'from hardship/suffering' uses از as the preposition 'from'. برتاب (imperative/archaic verbal form of بر تابیدن) means 'bear/put up with' (an exhortation), and روی 'face' is the noun object — together 'bear (it) in the face of suffering' or more idiomatically 'endure hardship.'
segments.s165-g385: به ... گرانتر نبود is a comparative negative: به + measure (یک جو) + ز + دیگر + adjective + نبود. Literally: 'was not dearer by one jo (barley) than the other.' ز is the poetic 'from/than' used in comparisons; نبود is the past negative 'was not.'
segments.s165-g386: نظاره شد uses the noun نظاره ('sight, view') plus شد ('became') to mean 'came into view' or 'was seen.' آن لشکر شاه is the subject (that army of the king) and زود 'soon' modifies the verb phrase; word order (verb after subject) is common in poetic prose.
segments.s165-g387: بگفتند is the past plural 'they said'; یکسر همه انجمن ('the whole assembly, all at once') is an emphatic noun phrase placed after the verb for poetic effect. یکسر here has the sense 'all at once/entirely.'
segments.s165-g388: که introduces a reported clause ('that'); اینت is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative 'this one'; سرافراز is an adjective 'high, honored, triumphant' describing the person; و شمشیرزن links the second predicate 'and a swordsman.' The sequence is a predicate phrase describing 'this one.'
segments.s165-g389: به آواز functions adverbially ('in a voice,' i.e. aloud). گفتند is the speech verb 'they said' and یک با دگر ('one with another') means 'to one another' — so the whole phrase: 'they said aloud to one another.' Word order places the adverbial first for emphasis.
segments.s165-g390: که introduces reported content: ما را (us as object, with را the object marker) بد آمد is an impersonal/periphrastic expression 'it went ill (for us)' or 'it boded ill for us'; ز ایران 'from Iran' qualifies the source; به سر is literal 'to the head' but here idiomatic (part of the expression indicating harm coming upon them). آمد is past 'came/happened.'
segments.s165-g391: سر سروران is a genitive/construct phrase 'the head(s) of the chiefs' or idiomatically 'the chiefs themselves'; اندر آمد به تنگ is an idiom meaning 'came into constraint' → 'were distressed/pressed.' The verb آمد is past 'came' and تنگ ('narrow/close') is used idiomatically.
segments.s165-g392: سزد‌گر is a conditional/ concessive particle 'if it be fitting' (literally 'if fitting'); بسازیم is the first-person plural subjunctive/optative 'let us make/let us set up' (here 'let us wage/make war'); با شاه جنگ is the object phrase 'war with the king.' Together it yields a proposal: 'If it is fitting, let us make war with the king.'
segments.s165-g1: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. خورشید تابنده is a noun phrase (noun + adjective) 'the radiant/shining sun'. بنمود is the past tense (3rd sg.) of نمودن/بنمودن (poetic), here 'showed/turned (its)'; پشت is the direct object meaning 'back' (i.e. the sun 'turned its back'). Word order places the verb before its object in this poetic line.
segments.s165-g2: This is two coordinated nominal-predicate clauses. هوا (subject) + شد (past of شدن 'become') + سیاه (predicate adjective) = 'the air became dark'. و links the clauses, then زمین شد درشت uses the same pattern for 'the earth became coarse/rough.' The adjectives are predicative following the auxiliary شد.
segments.s165-g3: سیاووش is the subject (proper name). لشکر is the direct object ('an army') without a preposition; به جیحون is a prepositional phrase marking direction/goal 'to the Jeyhun'. کشید is the past tense 3rd sg. of کشیدن, here meaning 'drew/moved/led' (poetic use 'marshaled/led'). The verb appears at the end in typical Persian order.
segments.s165-g4: به مژگان is an instrumental/locative phrase 'by/with (his) eyelashes' (به + noun used instrumentally). همی is an archaic/narrative particle indicating ongoing or habitual action (here it colours the verb as continuous/poetic). از جگر خون کشید is an idiomatic object: از + جگر ('from the heart/seat of feeling') + خون ('blood'); کشید (same verb) here means 'drew out' or 'shed' — together 'he drew blood from his heart' (a poetic expression of deep grief).
segments.s165-g5: چو آمد introduces a temporal clause 'when he came'. به ترمذ marks the destination 'to Termez'. درون بام و کوی ('within the roofs and alleys') gives the locale; note the noun phrases follow the verb in this poetic line but syntactically indicate the place being described in the next clause.
segments.s165-g6: بسان بهاران is a simile 'like spring'. پر از is an adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of'), governing the nouns رنگ و بوی 'color and scent' as the things the place is full of. Word order here is typical: simile phrase then predicate 'full of X'.
segments.s165-g7: چنان (so/thus) + بد here functions idiomatically to mean 'so (much)' or 'thus' (an intensifying phrase); همه شهرها is the topic 'all the towns'. تا ... به چاچ gives the extent 'as far as Chach' (تا ... به = 'up to / as far as'). The clause sets the scope/location of the description.
segments.s165-g8: تو is a rhetorical/generic 'you' (used impersonally). گفتی here functions as a modal/hypothetical past ('you would say' or 'one would say') rather than a literal report of speech. عروسی‌ست is a nominal predicate 'it is a wedding' (ست = copula). با طوق و تاج is a comitative/instrumental phrase 'with necklace(s) and crown(s)', describing the trappings that make one call it a wedding.
segments.s165-g873: This is a nominal list (enumeration) of bird names: تذروان, طاووس, کبک, with the adjective/qualifier دری placed after them and understood to attach to the last item (a common poetic placement). The conjunction و simply links the items; the whole phrase functions as the direct object of the following verb (بیابی). Note that تذروان is an archaic/bird-name item.
segments.s165-g874: بیابی is the verb 'you find' in a subjunctive/optative form (here translated as a general future/you-will-find statement). The subordinate temporal particle چو 'when' (sense = 'when') begins the clause چو از کوه‌ها بگذری, where از marks source 'from/out of' and بگذری is the present/subjunctive of گذر 'to pass' ('you pass'/'you cross'). Word order places the temporal clause after the verb, which is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s165-g875: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. گرماش and سرماش are nouns with the possessive/ending -ش 'its' (referring to the place) — 'its warmth/summer' and 'its cold/winter' — followed by the adjectives گرم and سرد. The clause states the absence of extreme heat or cold.
segments.s165-g876: همه جای is a locative phrase meaning 'everywhere' ('all places'); شادی و آرام و خورد is a nominal list functioning as the predicate (the copula 'is/are' is omitted in Persian). خورد here is a nominalized sense related to eating/food (what is eaten), so the list reads 'joy, calm, and food/provisions.'
segments.s165-g877: نبینی is the negative present/imperative-like form 'you do not / will not see' (here a general statement). بدان is the fused prepositional phrase 'بَ + آن' = 'in/at that' (the input split it as ب + دان; دان functions as the remainder of the demonstrative), شهر 'city' is the locative target, and بیمار کس literally 'sick person' with کس 'person/anyone'. Together: 'you will not see any sick person in that city.'
segments.s165-g878: یکی here functions as the indefinite/article-like 'a'/'one' (a single example), followed by بوستان 'garden'. بهشتست is the fused copula construction 'is paradise' (بهشت + است), and و بس 'and that's all' / 'and enough' closes the sentence. The line asserts that the place is essentially a garden of paradise.
segments.s165-g879: همه آبها is 'all the waters' (plural); روشن و خوشگوار are coordinating adjectives 'clear/bright and pleasant'. This nominal phrase is a predicate describing the waters; the copula is omitted, typical of Persian descriptive lines.
segments.s165-g880: همیشه is the adverb 'always'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and بوم 'land, dwelling' forms the phrase بر و بوم او 'upon its land/realm' (و here is the coordinating particle inside the compound). چون بهار is a simile 'like spring'. The whole adverbial says the place is perpetually like spring.
segments.s165-g105: مرا (topic/accusative) is fronted to mark the speaker as the one affected; نیز = "also"; خویشی‌ست is a predicative compound (خویشی + -ست, literary copula) meaning "is kin/related". با او = "with him" and به خون = "by blood" (expressing blood-relation). The whole clause reads as a topicalized equational sentence: 'As for me, I am also kin with him by blood.'
segments.s165-g106: The line contains two parallel nominal phrases for emphasis. همش (here = "entirely/wholly") modifies each following noun phrase. پهلوانم is پهلوان + the first-person possessive/clitic -م ("my champion"), and رهنمون is a noun "guide/guidance" completing the parallelism; both phrases present the person as the speaker's champion and guide. The repetition of همش intensifies the claim.
segments.s165-g107: همانا is an emphatic particle "verily/indeed." برین بوم is the literary combination بر + ین (here functioning like 'on this') + بوم = "on this land/realm." و بر صد هزار repeats بر to govern the following numeral phrase "over a hundred thousand." The phrase sets up the domain ('on this land and over a hundred thousand') for what follows.
segments.s165-g108: به فرمان من = "by/at my command" (به + فرمان with من as possessor). بیش is used quantitatively here as "more/more than" and باشد is the literary third-person subjunctive/existential copula "there may be / there are." سوار (rider/horseman) is the noun governed by بَیش و باشد: "there are more riders under my command."
segments.s165-g109: همم here functions as an additive/topic marker introducing possessions (poetic spelling/variant); بوم و بر = "on land and on/by (the other side)" or more generally 'in this realm and beyond.' هست is the copula 'is/are' and the following هم + گوسفند lists one class of possessions ('and also sheep'). The clause enumerates what the speaker possesses across domains.
segments.s165-g110: This is a simple enumerative phrase: هم = 'also/too' introducing a series of items: اسپ (horse), سلیح (weapons/armaments), کمان (bow), کمند (lasso/snare). The coordinating و links the list; the whole functions as continuation of the possessions named in the previous segment.
segments.s165-g111: مرا (topic/accusative) fronted marks the speaker as the one concerned; بی‌نیازیست is a nominal predicate (بی‌نیازی + -ست) meaning 'is without need / is self-sufficient.' از هر کسی = 'from anyone' (i.e. 'of anyone' or 'toward anyone'), so the clause states 'I am without need of anyone.'
segments.s165-g112: نهفته جزین نیز هستم بسی is compact: نهفته = 'hidden/kept in reserve', جزین = 'except/aside from this', نیز = 'also/besides', هستم بسی = 'I am (many/ample)' or better read as 'I have many (things)'. Together the line means 'besides this, I also have many things hidden/kept in reserve.' Note the poetic economy: nouns imply 'treasures/resources' after نهفته.
segments.s165-g817: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. سر تاج is a poetic compound: سر here (used figuratively) = 'head/chief' and تاج = 'crown/sovereignty', placed together as a single epithet. شاهنشهان is a genitival/attributive noun 'of the shahanshahs (emperors)'; older Persian often omits an ezafe, so the phrase reads 'that head/crown of the emperors.'
segments.s165-g818: Another parallel interrogative: کجا 'where', آن 'that'. دلاور is used as a noun 'hero/champion'. گرامی is an adjective 'honored/esteemed' modifying the following noun مهان. مهان in classical usage means 'guests' or 'noble/important persons' (here 'notable persons' or 'distinguished champions'), placed after its modifiers in typical Persian order.
segments.s165-g819: کجا 'where'; آن 'those/that'. بتان is the plural 'idols' used poetically for 'beautiful ones' or 'beloveds'. پر از is the idiom 'full of (something)' with پر (adjective) followed by the preposition از. ناز و شرم are coordinated nouns 'coquetry/affectation and modesty/shame', the qualities filling the betan.
segments.s165-g820: This is a pair of nominal descriptions: سخن گفتن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'speaking' or 'speech' (سخن + گفتن) and is described by the adjective خوب 'good/pleasant'. The second coordinated phrase آوای نرم uses the ezafe (-ی) on آوا to form 'the voice of ...' and نرم 'soft/gentle' describes that voice. Together: 'pleasant speech and a soft voice.'
segments.s165-g821: کجا 'where'; آنک is the relative 'that one who/which'. بر کوه means 'on the mountain' (بر = on). بودش is the past tense of بودن with the enclitic ش = 'his/it to him', here functioning syntactically as 'his abode was' (archaic ordering). کنام is a poetic noun 'nest/abode' (literally 'bosom/lap') and completes the predicate: 'his abode was on the mountain.'
segments.s165-g822: رمیده is a past participle 'having fled' or 'fled/one who fled'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. آرام here means 'rest/peace/comfort' (i.e., worldly ease). وز in classical poetry is a fused form (و + ز) meaning 'and from' linking the next items; this is a poetic connective rather than the modern lexical senses listed. کام 'desire/pleasure' and نام 'name/fame' follow in a series: 'fled from peace, and from desire and fame.'
segments.s165-g823: چو is the temporal connector 'when'. گیتی 'the world'. تهی 'empty' and ماند (past of ماندن) 'remained/was left' form the clause 'the world remained empty'. از راستان is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the righteous' — راستان is the plural/collective 'the righteous/upright ones' and is the source from which the world is empty.
segments.s165-g824: تو 'you' marks the addressee. ایدر 'here'. ببودن is a poetic/archaic infinitival/verbal noun form meaning 'to be / existence' (ب- prefixed). مزن is a prohibitive/imperative 'do not (make/tell)' — in the idiom داستان زدن it means 'spin/tell a tale', so مزن داستان = 'do not tell a story'. Grammatically the phrase treats 'تو ایدر ببودن' ('your being here') as the topic/object and issues the prohibition: 'do not tell tales of your being here.'
segments.s165-g73: نگه کرد is a compound past (literally “made a gaze”) used here as a simple past verb meaning “looked” or “fixed their gaze.” پیران follows the verb as the subject (“the elders”). به دیدار او is a prepositional phrase “toward/for seeing him” (به + دیدار + pronoun او) specifying the object of the look.
segments.s165-g74: نشست is a past form meaning “they sat” or “took a seat.” The following list بر و یال و گفتار او is linked elliptically to the earlier verb (i.e., “[they sat and looked] upon his …”); بر here functions as a preposition “upon/on” governing یال and گفتار, and او is the possessive pronoun “his.” The conjunction و simply joins the three items.
segments.s165-g75: بدو is the prepositional pronoun “to him/toward him.” در دو چشمش literally “into his two eyes” marks the locus of the staring. همی is the classical iterative/continuous particle (“continually/still”), and خیره ماند is a compound meaning “remained transfixed/staring” (خیره = staring, ماند = remained).
segments.s165-g76: همی هر زمان is a habitual/time phrase “continually/at every moment.” نام یزدان is the direct object “the name of God.” بخواند is the classical imperfective/present-form used here to indicate repeated action — “he would call/recite (the name).”
segments.s165-g77: بدو گفت is an act of speaking directed to him: بدو = “to him/unto him” and گفت here functions as ‘said’ (classical past). کای is the vocative particle “O,” and نامور شهریار is a nominal phrase “renowned king/sovereign.” Note: گفت is used here as the verb ‘said’ despite the lemma senses given as nouns.
segments.s165-g78: ز شاهان گیتی توی یادگار is a genitival/locative phrase describing him: ز شاهان گیتی = “of/from the kings of the world” (ز = poetic ‘of/from’), توی = classical/prepositional “in/among,” and یادگار = “a memorial/memento.” Together they mean “a memorial among the world’s kings.”
segments.s165-g79: سه چیزست is an existential/classical construction: “there are three things.” بر تو means “concerning/about you” (lit. ‘on you’). که اندر جهان introduces the relative clause “that in the world …” with اندر جهان = “in the world.”
segments.s165-g80: کسی را نباشد is a negative existential clause: کسی را = “(for) anyone/no one” with را used for emphasis/definiteness, and نباشد is the classical negative of ‘to be’ meaning “is not/does not exist.” ز تخم مهان literally “from the seed/stock of the great (ones)” = “of noble lineage”; the phrase says that no one of that stock has these things. (Note: some lemma senses in the provided list are not exact matches for these classical usages.)
segments.s165-g361: This is a coordinate noun phrase: 'پرستار بسیار' = 'many attendants' (noun followed by quantity word), linked by 'و' to 'چندی غلام' = 'several slaves/servants'. Word order (noun + modifier) and the simple coordination are typical of older/poetic Persian; 'پرستار' here means attendants/keepers.
segments.s165-g362: 'یکی' (= one/a certain) introduces the following noun 'جام' (cup), but adjectives/qualifiers intervene: 'پر ز یاقوت' is an adjectival phrase 'full of rubies' formed with poetic preposition 'ز' (= از) and 'پر' used adjectivally 'full (of)'; 'رخشنده' ('radiant') qualifies the cup as well. Final word-order places the head noun 'جام' at the end, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s165-g363: 'بفرمود' is the honorific/past verb 'he commanded/ordered'. 'تا' introduces a purpose/result clause taking the subjunctive; here it governs 'بشمرند' (subjunctive/plural of 'شمردن') meaning 'that they should count'. 'خواسته' functions as the object 'what was requested' or 'the requested things' which they are to count.
segments.s165-g364: 'همه' ('all') is the quantified object/subject to be moved; 'سوی' is the preposition 'towards'; 'کاخ سیاوش' is a possessive noun phrase 'Siavash's palace' with the possessor following the possessed (typical in poetry); 'برند' is the plural subjunctive/present used in coordination with the earlier subjunctive 'بشمرند' and means 'they should carry/take'.
segments.s165-g365: This clause means 'each person had his own land in Turan.' 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/of' used here in an idiomatic sense 'from each' = 'each'; 'هر کش' = 'every person' (archaic spelling/word); 'به توران' gives the location 'in Turan'; 'زمین خویش' = 'his own land' (خوی + ش = 'one's own'); 'بود' is the past copula 'was' used existentially to mean 'there was/each had'.
segments.s165-g366: This sentence continues with 'ورا' ('to him') marking the beneficiary, 'مهربانی' = 'kindness' as the theme, and 'بیش بود' = 'there was more' (بیش = comparative/greater; بود = past 'was'). The compound 'مهربانی برو' functions idiomatically as 'kindness that went to him / shown to him' (the verb 'برو' here expresses direction or distribution—'went to'), so the whole clause means 'and still more kindness was shown to him.'
segments.s165-g367: 'به خویشان' is the indirect object 'to the relatives/kinsmen'; 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' (چنین + گفت). 'کاو را' is an archaic/emphatic element (here functioning to mark the addressee/emphasis) and 'همه' addresses the whole group 'all' — the clause sets up the reported speech that follows. Note: some listed lemma senses do not include the exact pragmatic/archaic pronoun sense used here.
segments.s165-g368: 'شما' (you, plural) is the addressee; 'خیل' is the predicative noun 'a host/company/crowd' and 'باشید' is the present-subjunctive/imperative 'be' addressing 'you' (polite/plural). 'هم چون' is a fixed comparative phrase 'like/as' and 'رمه' is the simile 'a flock' — together 'هم چون رمه' = 'like a flock.' The clause is an injunction: 'You all be a company like a flock.'
segments.s165-g441: ولیکن is an adversative conjunction meaning 'but' and opens the sentence. ترا را is the stressed/accusative second‑person object (ت as the clitic + را as the definite/object marker) marking the person addressed. آن سزاوارتر is a comparative adjective phrase: آن 'that' + سزاوارتر (comparative of سزاوار, 'more deserving' or 'more fitting'), which prepares to be completed by the relative clause introduced by که.
segments.s165-g442: که is the relative/completive particle 'that/which' linking the comparative to its content. از + دامن شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's hem/robe' (از governs دامن, which takes the genitive شاه). جویی is a present/optative form meaning 'seek / you seek' (classical/poetic present-subjunctive sense) and governs its object گهر 'a jewel'. In short: 'that (you) seek a jewel from the king's robe.'
segments.s165-g443: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/so/therefore'. پرده من is the noun phrase 'my veil/curtain'. چهارند is an archaic predicative form meaning 'are four / is fourfold' (the -ند ending here marks the predication). خرد follows as a vocative 'O Wise One' (خرد used to address someone as 'wise' or 'sage').
segments.s165-g444: چو here means 'when / since'. باید is the impersonal modal 'it is necessary / one ought'. ترا (ت + را) is the object 'you' with the object marker. بنده is the predicate noun 'servant'. The second باید + شمرد forms the periphrastic verbal idea 'ought to count / should regard' (شمرد = 'to count/consider'); together the clause means 'when it is fitting one should regard you as a servant.' The repetition of باید is rhetorical/emphatic in classical style.
segments.s165-g445: ازیشان (از ایشان) is a partitive/locative 'of/from them'. جریرست is the proper name with the contracted copula (جریر + است → جریرست), 'is Jarir'. مهتر is a predicate 'chief/elder' and بسال 'by a year / a year older' qualifies his seniority; together: 'Of them, Jarir is the chief, (older) by a year.'
segments.s165-g446: که introduces a relative clause modifying مهتر: 'who'. از خوبرویان is a partitive 'of the beautiful (ones / fair‑faced)'. ندارد is the negative possessive 'does not have'. همال here functions archaically as 'match/peer' (i.e. 'equal'); the clause means 'who among the beautiful has no equal.'
segments.s165-g447: یکی is the indefinite determiner 'a certain / one'. دختری is 'a maiden / girl' (the -ی gives indefiniteness). هست is the copula/existential 'is/there is', and آراسته is a predicative adjective 'adorned / dressed up'. Together: 'There is a certain adorned maiden.'
segments.s165-g448: چو is the simile particle 'like / as'. ماه is 'the moon' (a common simile for beauty). درخشنده is an attributive/participial adjective 'shining / radiant'. با + خواسته here yields 'with desire' or better 'much desired' — خواسته is a participle/adjectival noun meaning 'desired / sought after'. The phrase describes her as 'shining like the moon and much desired.'
segments.s165-g809: چنین داد پاسخ که = چِنین + داد + پاسخ + که. چِنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the verb; داد (past of دادن) with پاسخ (noun) forms the verbal phrase 'gave an answer'. که here introduces the subordinate clause ('that/so that'). چرخ بلند follows as the agent of the coming verb: چرخ = 'the sky/heaven' (used metaphorically for the cosmic power/fate) and بلند is a postpositive adjective 'high/lofty'.
segments.s165-g810: دلم کرد پردرد و جانم نژند = دل + ـم (possessive 'my') + کرد + پردرد; و + جان + ـم + نژند. دلم کرد پردرد is a causative construction: کرد ('made') takes دلِـم ('my heart') as object and پردرد 'full of pain/sorrow' as predicative complement. جانم نژند has an omitted copula (poetic ellipsis of شد/شدن): 'my life/soul became base/despicable.'
segments.s165-g811: که هر چند گرد آورم خواسته = که introduces a concessive clause here ('that/although'); هر چند is an idiom meaning 'however much' or 'though'. گرد آورم is the verb phrase 'I gather/bring together' (گرد in combination with آوردن = to gather), and خواسته is a noun 'that which is wished/desired' (object of گرد آورم).
segments.s165-g812: هم از گنج و هم تاج آراسته = a correlative هم ... هم meaning 'both ... and'. از introduces the source/kind ('from/of' treasure), گنج = 'treasure/riches'. تاج آراسته = 'an adorned crown', with آراسته as a postpositive adjective meaning 'adorned/ornate.'
segments.s165-g813: به فرجام یکسر به دشمن رسد = a temporal/result phrase followed by the verb. به فرجام 'to the end/outcome' and یکسر 'entirely/altogether' emphasize total loss; به دشمن رسد literally 'it reaches the enemy' (رسد here = 'attain/reach' or 'fall into the hands of'). Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase + adverb + prepositional phrase + verb.
segments.s165-g814: بدی بد بود مرگ بر تن رسد = an exclamatory/conditional remark. بدی (nominal 'evil') + بد بود ('would be bad' / 'it is bad') expresses regret or judgment; مرگ بر تن رسد = 'when/if death comes upon the body' with رسد used in the sense 'befall/happen'. The clause uses بود in past/conditional sense and omits a conjunction like 'اگر' ('if')—a rhetorical construction.
segments.s165-g815: کجا آن حکیمان و دانندگان = کجا 'where' begins a lament/question; آن is the demonstrative 'those/the'; حکیمان 'wise men/sages' (classical plural); و connects to دانندگان 'the knowers/learned ones'. The phrase asks for the whereabouts of these previously-mentioned figures.
segments.s165-g816: همان رنج‌بردار خوانندگان = همان 'the same/that very' points back to the people just named; رنج‌بردار is a compound adjective/noun 'sufferer/one who bears hardship' (used attributively here), and خوانندگان (literally 'readers' or 'reciters') is the plural noun describing them. Together: 'those same readers who endured suffering.'
segments.s165-g497: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. چند فرزند من is a noun phrase 'several / some children of mine' (چند here = 'several/some'); خویش functions as 'one's own / kin' and ش is the enclitic possessive 'his/her/its' attached to خویش. تست here appears at the end of the clause as an older poetic/copular element 'is/are' (classical Persian -ست), so the clause reads roughly 'If several of my children are (your) own.' Word order follows Subject (چند فرزند من) + predicate (خویش ش تست) typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s165-g498: مرا غم is an idiomatic combination: مرا here functions as the dative/possessive 'to me / for me' (often written as مـَرا in classical texts) and غم = 'grief / sorrow', so 'my sorrow'. ز بهر = 'because of / on account of'. کم و بیش = 'less and more' i.e. 'few or many / small or great in number'. تست again is the poetic/copular ending ('is'), so the clause means 'my sorrow (to me) is because of their being few or many.' Note the older clitic/copula -ست attaching to the clause-final word.
segments.s165-g499: This is a nominal clause with the name first: 'فرنگیس' (Farangis) followed by the predicate 'مهتر ز خوبان اوی'. مهتر = 'chief/foremost' and ز introduces comparison 'of / among' (here 'among the beautiful'). اوی is an archaic form meaning 'is she' (او + ی), so the whole fragment means 'Farangis is foremost of the beautiful.' Word order: Topic/subject (name) + predicate (superlative/comparative phrase) + archaic copular clitic.
segments.s165-g500: نبینی is the negative present/future 2nd/3rd-person form 'you/one do not see' (classical negative). به گیتی literally 'in/within the world' (به functioning as a locative/prepositional marker with گیتی 'world'). چنان = 'such' modifying موی و روی 'hair and face'. So the clause means 'in the world you will not see hair and face such as (hers)'. Note the negation is prefix نـ and the verb is clause-final as in Persian.
segments.s165-g501: به بالا ز سرو سهی برترست is a comparative-predicative phrase: به بالا 'up/high' + ز سرو 'than the cypress' (ز introduces the standard of comparison) + سهی 'the dark/black (poetic epithet, here describing the person as dark-haired or dusky)' + برترست = برتر + ست 'is superior / is higher'. The copula -ست is the classical third-person marker attached to the adjective. Word order places the comparative phrase before the verb-like predicate.
segments.s165-g502: ز مشک سیه بر سرش افسرست: ز مشک سیه = 'of (/) from black musk' (a poetic image), بر سرش = 'upon her head' (بر + سر + enclitic ش = 'her'), and افسرست is 'is (an) afsar/crown/helmet' where افسر (afsar) means a head-piece (helmet/crown) and -ست is the copula 'is'. So the phrase reads 'a (dark/musky) crown rests upon her head.' Note the classical syntactic placement of the copula as suffix.
segments.s165-g503: هنرها و دانش 'arts and knowledge' is a coordinated noun phrase. ز اندازه بیش literally 'beyond measure' (ز here marks source/reason or 'from' in a poetic comparative sense, and اندازه بیش = 'more than measure / excessive' = 'exceeding measure'). Together it means 'her arts and learning are beyond measure' or 'exceed measure.'
segments.s165-g504: خرد را پرستار دارد به پیش: خرد 'wisdom' with را marking the direct object; پرستار here is 'guardian / keeper' (one who tends or cherishes), دارد = 'has/keeps' (present tense auxiliary/lexical verb), and به پیش = 'before (her) / in front'. The clause means 'she sets/keeps wisdom as a guardian before her' i.e. she holds wisdom foremost. Note را marks the definite object and دارد is the modern-present 'has/holds.'
segments.s165-g401: سپهبد (vocative title) opens the line addressing the commander. چه ... چه is an exclamative correlative meaning “how ... and how ...,” linking two adjectives. بودی is the 2nd-person singular past of بودن (“you were”), so شادان چه بودی دژم = “how joyful you were, (and) how grim.” Word order places the adjectives before the copula for poetic emphasis.
segments.s165-g402: بجز با = “except with.” نبودی is the past negative 2nd‑person (ن + بودی) meaning “you were not.” به هم here functions with هم to mean “together/joined,” so the whole clause says you were not together with anyone, except with Siyavash; the prepositional phrase بجز با سیاوش specifies the sole exception.
segments.s165-g403: ز here is the poetic form of از (“from/of”), used in a list: “from Jahan and from Garsivaz and whoever (else) was.” هرک = “whoever” (indefinite), and بود is the past copula “was.” The clause enumerates possible people while keeping the verb in the past.
segments.s165-g404: به کس = “to anyone/someone” (here governed by the negative that follows). نگشاد is a poetic past form (ن + گشاد) meaning “did not open/reveal” (negative past of گشودن). Thus به کس راز نگشاد = “he revealed his secret to no one.” و شادان نبود closes with another past negative copula: نبود = “he was not (joyful).”
segments.s165-g405: مگر = “except.” با سیاوش marks the exception (“with Siyavash”). روز و شب (“day and night”) is an adverbial time phrase; the verb that governs it is implied as the past copula/being together. The word بدی in the text here is irregular in the provided senses (likely a poetic or scribal variant of بودی “you were”), so the clause means “except with Siyavash you were (together) day and night.”
segments.s165-g406: ازو = “from him / to him” (here “to him”). برگشادی is the 2nd‑person past singular of برگشاد(ن)/برگشودن meaning “you opened up / revealed (to).” به خنده دو لب is an instrumental/temporal phrase: به خنده = “with a smile,” دو لب = “(on) both lips” (a poetic way to say ‘with a smile on your lips’). The line says he opened up (to Siyavash) with a smile on his lips.
segments.s165-g407: برین گونه is a fixed poetic phrase meaning “in this manner / thus” (بر + این گونه). یک سال بگذاشتند uses بگذاشتند (past, 3rd‑person plural) to mean “they spent/passed” a year; the verb takes سال as its object and places the action in the past.
segments.s165-g408: غم و شادمانی = “sorrow and joy.” بهم داشتند is a compound: بهم (“to one another / together”) + داشتند (3rd‑person plural past of داشتن = “they had/possessed”), so the clause means “they had sorrow and joy together” — i.e., they shared both grief and rejoicing.
segments.s165-g321: بکوشید is a past, 3rd-singular classical form meaning 'he strove/attempted.' تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that/in order that'). The small clause that follows has an inverted word order: بر زه (onto the string) is the adverbial/oblique phrase, آرد is the verb in a classical/present-subjunctive sense ('may put/bring'), and کمان (the subject/object 'bow') comes after the verb for emphasis—together: 'he strove that he might put the bow onto the string.'
segments.s165-g322: نیامد is the simple past-negative 'did not come / would not fit.' In this narrative position بُرو is functioning as a short connective/vernacular particle (archaic/poetic usage) linking the failure to what follows. خیره شد is a compound of the participle خیره ('staring/fixed') + شد ('became') = 'became fixed/stared,' and بدگمان is an adjective 'suspicious/doubtful' describing his state.
segments.s165-g323: ازو = 'from him/of him' marks source/possession. The verb بستد is a past-form used here transitively ('took/seized' in context); word order places the agent (شاه) adjacent to the verb: 'the king took it from him.' به زانو نشست is an adverbial phrase + past verb: 'sat (down) on (his) knee' (به زانو = on the knee; نشست = sat).
segments.s165-g324: بمالید is past 'he rubbed' (here a physical rubbing/anointing). کمان را is the definite object (را marks the direct object: 'the bow'). خانه here follows the verb as an adverbial/element attached to the action (contextually 'over/at the place of the bow' — an idiomatic/poetic use; the exact technical sense is dialectal). به دست uses به as an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by/with' (به دست = with the hand) and دست = 'hand.'
segments.s165-g325: به زه کرد is a light-verb construction: به زه (to/onto the string) + کرد (made/did) meaning 'he put it on the string' or 'he strung it.' و خندان چنین is a participial phrase ('and, smiling, thus') modifying the speaker, and گفت شاه shows the verb گفت ('said') followed by the subject شاه in inverted order ('the king said').
segments.s165-g326: که introduces reported speech ('that/that is'). اینت is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative roughly 'this' (classical form of این). کمانی is an adjective/noun related to 'kaman' ('bow-shaped' or 'of the bow'). چو = 'as/like' sets up the comparison, باید = 'should/ought' expresses necessity, and به راه ('for the road' or 'for the march/use') is an adverbial phrase indicating purpose or occasion.
segments.s165-g327: مرا (here written as م + را in the tokenization) functions in an existential/possessive pattern ('to me' / 'I had'); thus مرا نیز گاه جوانی کمان = 'to me also at the time of youth (there was) a bow' i.e. 'I, too, once had a bow.' نیز = 'also', گاه جوانی = 'the time of youth' (i.e. 'in my youth'), and کمان = 'bow' (the possessed item).
segments.s165-g328: چنین بود = 'it was thus/so' (چنین 'so/thus' + بود 'was'). و اکنون دگر شد زمان = 'and now the time has become different': اکنون = 'now', دگر = 'other/different', شد = past 'became', زمان = 'time/era.' The clause states a contrast between past possession and present changed circumstances.
segments.s165-g121: سیاووش is the subject (the proper name Siavash). بدان گفت‌ها is a parenthetical impersonal formula meaning 'it is said about that' (بدان = به + آن, 'regarding that'; گفت‌ها is the archaic impersonal/past reportive 'it is said' / 'they say'). رام شد is the main past predicate 'became tame' (شد = past 'became'). The clause order is Subject — parenthetical reportive — predicate.
segments.s165-g122: برافروخت is a past intransitive verb 'he flushed / was kindled / was roused'. و is the simple connective 'and'. اندر is the preposition 'in/within'. خور here is verbal/agentive in the eating/drinking sense ('to eat/drink' stem) and with جام ('cup' or 'draught') forms the idea 'to take/drink from the cup'. شد is the past inchoative 'became' used to mark entrance into that state (i.e. 'he took to drinking').
segments.s165-g123: به خوردن نشستند is a standard periphrastic verb of purpose: به + infinitive (خوردن 'to eat') expresses purpose/intent and نشستند is the past plural 'they sat down / began (to do so)'. یک با دگر is an idiomatic phrase 'one with another' (= together, mutually).
segments.s165-g124: سیاوش پسر گشت is Subject + predicate-nominal + past 'became' (گشت = 'became'), i.e. 'Siavash became a son / like a son.' و links the next clause. پیران پدر mirrors the same pattern: the name (پیران) followed by predicate پدر and the verb is omitted but understood from the previous گشت (parataxis), giving 'and Piran (became) a father.'
segments.s165-g125: برفتند is the simple past plural 'they went / departed'. با خنده و شادمان is an adverbial phrase 'with laughter and joy' modifying their manner of departure (با + nouns indicating accompaniment/state). شادمان functions as noun/adjective 'joyful' describing them.
segments.s165-g126: به ره = 'onto/along the road' (به + ره). بر نجستند here contains the verb جَستند (past of 'to leap/seek') with the negative prefix ن- (i.e. 'did not leap/seek'); together the phrase means they did not pause/linger on the road. جایی زمان 'any place or time' are the objects of that negated verb (they did not stop for any particular place or time).
segments.s165-g127: چنین تا رسیدند is a temporal/sequence frame 'thus until they arrived' (چنین = 'thus', تا introducing the extent until arrival). در شهر گنگ = 'in/into the muffled (or indistinct/quiet) city' where گنگ modifies شهر as an adjective.
segments.s165-g128: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which' (ک + از + آن fused) introducing what issues from that city. بود is the past copula 'was'. خرم سرای درنگ is a noun phrase: خرم 'merry/joyful' modifying سرای (here 'saray' = abode/hall — the exact lexical sense in the provided lemma list does not match, so the item is treated as the building/place) and درنگ 'pause/rest' qualifying the place (i.e. 'the joyful hall/house of rest'). Grammatically, the relative phrase 'کزان بود ...' means 'from which there was / came ...'.
segments.s165-g681: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'. The clause follows typical Persian order with the subject (بانوی) before the verb (بشنید); پیغام اوی places the possessed noun (پیغام) before the enclitic possessive (اوی = 'his/her'). The overall literal order is 'when the lady heard his message'.
segments.s165-g682: به سوی is a prepositional phrase 'toward' (به + سوی) and takes the namesake target (فرنگیس). بنهاد is the simple past 3rd-person singular of بنهادن 'placed/put' used here idiomatically with روی 'face' to mean 'turned (her) face'. The object روی follows the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s165-g683: زمین را ببوسید is 'kissed the earth', with را marking the definite direct object زمین; the verb ببوسید is past 3rd-person. The subject گلشهر follows the verb (a common poetic inversion). و گفت connects this clause to the reported speech that follows.
segments.s165-g684: که introduces reported content ('that ...'). خورشید را is topicalized with the object-marker را. گشت is the past of شدن 'became', and the predicate ناهید جفت states the pairing: literally 'the sun became (with) Nahid pair' = 'the sun became paired with Nahid.'
segments.s165-g685: هم امشب is an adverbial phrase 'also/this very night'. بباید is a modal 'must/ought' and is followed by the infinitive شدن (here functioning like 'to go/visit' in context) so the phrase means 'must go/be present'. نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the king's presence'. The subject is omitted and understood from context (3rd person).
segments.s165-g686: بیاراستن is the infinitive 'to adorn/array'. گاه here means 'time' or 'occasion' and pairs with the rest of the clause; او را is the definite object pronoun 'him/her' (او with object marker را). به ماه is a prepositional/comparative phrase 'like the moon' or 'with the moon' describing how he should be adorned.
segments.s165-g687: بیامد is the past 3rd-person singular 'came'. The proper name فرنگیس follows the verb (poetic word order). چون ماه نو is a simile 'like the new moon' modifying her arrival.
segments.s165-g688: به نزدیک is a locative phrase 'to/near the proximity of'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying تاجور. تاجور and شاه نو form a nominal group in apposition (poetically ordered), so the phrase reads 'that tajur, the new king' (تاجور used as a descriptor here).
segments.s165-g425: نبینمت is a negated present-tense verb with the enclitic object -مت ('I do not see you'); in poetry the verb comes first. پیوسته is an adverb 'constantly' modifying the verb phrase. خون کسی is a noun phrase 'someone's blood' (خون + کسی), placed after the adverb; the whole segment implies 'I don't see you continuously with/covered by anyone's blood' (i.e. no one is constantly shedding blood for you).
segments.s165-g426: کجا is the interrogative 'where/how (then)'; داردی is the 2nd‑person present form 'you have' (literary ending -ی). مهر بر تو is a dative/benefactive phrase 'affection toward you' (مهر + preposition بر + تو). بسی is a postpositive quantifier 'much/enough' modifying مهر; together the clause asks rhetorically where you can claim to have much love directed at you.
segments.s165-g427: برادر نداری is a negatived possession clause: noun + نداری (here 'lack'/'you do not have') implying 'you have no brother'. The following نه خواهر نه زن is the correlative 'neither... nor...' adding sister and wife. The personal subject (تو) is omitted as typical in Persian.
segments.s165-g428: چو is the simile 'like/as'. شاخ گلی is a genitive/attributive sequence 'a branch of a flower' (گلی qualifying شاخ). بر کنار چمن is a locative phrase 'at/on the edge of the meadow'; the whole simile compares the addressee's solitary state to a delicate flower‑branch at the meadow's edge.
segments.s165-g429: یکی 'one/a single' specifies the number. زن نگه کن is an imperative phrase: نگه (keeping) + کن (do) → 'keep/maintain a wife' or 'take a wife'. سزاوار خویش is an adjectival phrase 'worthy/fitting for yourself' (سزاوار = fitting; خویش = 'one's own'/'of yourself', here functioning to say 'befitting you').
segments.s165-g430: از ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran'. منه here functions as a prohibitive/negative (poetic) 'do not bring/take' (an older/literary imperative form). درد و تیمار are coordinated nouns 'pain and solicitude/care'. پیش at the end means 'before/in the presence of' or 'forthcoming' — together the clause advises not to bring sorrow and care from Iran into view/into the court.
segments.s165-g431: پس از مرگ کاووس is a temporal prepositional phrase 'after the death of Kavus' (پس از = after). ایران تراست inverts normal word order for emphasis: the subject ایران is followed by تراست ('is yours' — تراست is a literary form meaning 'it is yours'), so the clause declares that Iran will belong to you after Kavus's death.
segments.s165-g432: همان is the demonstrative 'that/same' emphasizing identity. تاج و تخت is a coordinated noun phrase 'crown and throne' (symbolizing sovereignty). دلیران is a plural attributive 'of the brave' and تراست repeats the predicate 'is yours' applied to the whole noun phrase; the result is 'that very crown and throne of the brave will be yours.'
segments.s165-g737: فراوان is an adverb/adjectival quantifier meaning 'plentifully' or 'in great number' that modifies عماری; عماری here is a noun 'a dwelling/building' used in the plural sense in the phrase; بیاراستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural classic/formal verb meaning 'they adorned/they furnished' (بی- prefix plus آراستن). Word order is typical Persian: adverb/quantifier + noun + verb (subject implied in the verb ending).
segments.s165-g738: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/so' linking to what follows; پرده is a noun 'curtain/veil'; خوبان is the plural noun 'the lovely/beautiful ones' or 'beloveds' (here referring to beautiful people, i.e. the ladies); بپیراستند is the past 3rd-plural of پیراستن with the b- prefix, here meaning 'they adorned' or 'they arranged' (the b- prefix is a classical narrative form). Note the object/goal (پرده خوبان) precedes the verb as is normal in Persian.
segments.s165-g739: فرنگیس را: فرنگیس is the proper name and را is the definite/direct-object marker marking her as the patient of the verb; در عماری places location ('in a pavilion/dwelling'); نشاند is an archaic/transitive past form related to نشاندن (causative/put or 'seated'), 3rd-person singular past — here 'he seated (her)'. The clause literally follows object/location + verb order typical in classical Persian.
segments.s165-g740: بنه is a concrete noun here (literally 'a small bundle/heap' in the lexicon provided) and برنهاد is the classical past 3rd-person singular of برنهادن meaning 'placed' or 'set down'; و is the coordinating 'and'; سپه را uses را to mark سپه ('the host/army') as the object; براند is an older-form past/present of راندن meaning 'to drive' or 'to lead/drive away' (here 'led/drave the host'). Note the sequence: he seated Farangis, then put down the bundle and (he) drove/led the host.
segments.s165-g741: ازو is the prepositional/clitic pronoun 'from him/of him'; بازنگسست is an archaic past form composed of باز + نگسست meaning essentially 'broke apart/parted' (classical usage can imply separation or its negation depending on context); پیران is the plural noun 'elders' and گرد here functions as a postpositional/relational element meaning 'around' or indicating the group around someone. Word order here is somewhat inverted in the classical style: the prepositional pronoun + verb is followed by the agent/group phrase.
segments.s165-g742: بنه again is 'a bundle/heap' and برنهاد is past 'placed/put down' (3rd-singular classical form); و is 'and'; سپه را marks 'the host/army' as object with را; ببرد is a classical past/perfect form of بردن used in narrative to mean 'took/carried away' (3rd-person singular). The clause strings actions: they set the bundle down and took/carry away the host.
segments.s165-g743: به شادی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with joy' or 'joyfully' (به + noun شادی); برفتند is the classical past 3rd-person plural of رفتن 'they went' (the b- prefix is frequent in classical narrative); سوی is a directional preposition 'toward(s)'; ختن here names the destination (the city Khotan in the narrative).
segments.s165-g744: همه is the universal determiner 'all/everyone'; نامداران is a plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones' (famed men/heroes); شدند is the past 3rd-person plural of شدن 'became' and here functions to mean 'were/assembled as' or 'came to be'; انجمن is a noun 'assembly/meeting' and sits after the verb in classical order — together the clause means 'all the famed men formed an assembly' or 'gathered into a council.'
segments.s165-g9: به هر منزلی is a locative prepositional phrase 'at/for every dwelling'. ساخته functions as a participial adjective 'made/prepared' modifying the coming noun, and خوردنی is an adjective 'edible/fit to eat' that also modifies that noun; together they describe the dishes that follow. Word order is slightly poetic: modifiers (ساخته خوردنی) appear before the noun which is completed in the next segment.
segments.s165-g10: خورش‌های is the plural noun 'dishes/stews' (خورش + plural suffix). زیبا and گستردنی are adjectives joined by و: 'beautiful' and a verbal-adjective from گستردن meaning 'laid out/abundantly spread' (gostardani). Together they complete the picture of the prepared food.
segments.s165-g11: چنین 'thus/so' links to the preceding action. تا introduces a temporal clause 'until', and به قچقار باشی = 'until he was/arrived at Qachqar' where باشی is an older/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'be/remain' used for the temporal state. براند is the main verb 'they drove' (i.e., drove him on/along) governing that temporal clause.
segments.s165-g12: فرود آمد is a compound intransitive 'descended/ alighted' (فرود + آمد). آنجا 'there' is an adverbial place. و links to the next clause; چندی 'for a while' modifies بماند, and بماند (here a narrative-form of 'to remain') means 'he stayed/lingered' for a short time.
segments.s165-g13: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آگاهی آمد = 'news/information arrived' (a common formula for 'when word came'). پذیره شدند uses the noun پذیره 'reception' with the auxiliary/verb شدند to mean 'a reception was made' or 'they were received' (passive/impersonal sense).
segments.s165-g14: همه 'all' is the subject. سرکشان here refers to the rebellious/proud ones (contextually), and با + تبیره 'with explanation' indicates the means or manner; شدند is the past auxiliary meaning 'they were (so) / they became', so the whole phrase means 'all the rebellious were dealt with/pacified by explanation.'
segments.s165-g15: ز خویشان 'from among the relatives' is a prepositional phrase. گزین کرد is a compound verb 'chose' (گزین + کرد), and پیران هزار 'a thousand elders' is the object; the numeral follows the noun here in a poetic inversion (përān هزار = 'a thousand elders').
segments.s165-g16: پذیره شدن is a verbal noun phrase 'the being-received' / 'the reception' (پذیره + شدن). را is the direct-object/definiteness marker attaching to that phrase. برآراست is a past narrative verb 'he arranged/set in order' (from برآراستن), and کار 'work/task' names what he arranged — 'he arranged the work of reception.'
segments.s165-g409: This is an adverbial/time phrase plus subject phrase: 'سیاوش یکی روز' = 'Siavash, one day' (literary order: proper name + 'one day'). 'و پیران' joins the elders with 'and'. 'بهم' is an adverb meaning 'together' and modifies the forthcoming verb 'نشستند'.
segments.s165-g410: 'نشستند' is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of نشستن ('they sat'). The conjunction 'و' links two coordinated verbs; 'گفتند' is past 3pl ('they spoke/said') but in context functions like 'they discussed'. 'هر بیش و کم' is an idiomatic phrase 'every more and less' = 'everything, large and small' — 'هر'='every', 'بیش'='more/greater', 'کم'='less/smaller'.
segments.s165-g411: 'بدو گفت پیران' = 'the elders said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). 'کزین' is the literary fused preposition 'کز + این' meaning 'from this'. 'بوم و بر' is a fixed pair meaning 'land and region/realm' (two nouns joined by و). The verb completing the clause comes after.
segments.s165-g412: 'چنانی که' introduces a concessive/relative sense 'in whatever way/whatever sort'. 'باشد' here is a present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-singular of بودن ('may be / is'), used inside the relative clause. 'کسی' = 'anyone/someone'. 'برگذر' is an imperative/hortatory form meaning 'let (him) pass / allow to pass / overlook' (بر-گذر: let pass). Together: 'whatever kind he may be, let someone pass.'
segments.s165-g413: 'بدین مهربانی' is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this kindness' (بدین = 'by this'). 'که' introduces the clause that follows (result or explanation). The sequence 'بر تست شاه' is archaic/literary; 'بر' functions as a prepositional element and 'شاه' = 'the king'. 'تست' here is an old/contracted form not represented in the provided sense list (see note) — in older Persian shapes like 'تست' can function as a possessive/copular unit; the whole phrase frames what the kindness achieves regarding the king.
segments.s165-g414: 'به نام تو' = 'in/under your name' (به = 'to/for', نام = 'name', تو = 'you'). 'خسپد' is an archaic/poetic verb here meaning roughly 'be laid/placed/entombed' or 'be set' (the provided sense entries do not capture this sense). 'به آرامگاه' = 'into the tomb' (به = to/into, آرامگاه = tomb). Grammatically this is a result clause: by this kindness, (so that) your name is placed / you are laid to rest in the tomb.
segments.s165-g415: 'چنان دان' is an imperative/hortative: 'consider/know it so' (چنان = 'thus', دان = imperative of دانستن/'know' or 'consider'). 'که' introduces the explanatory content. 'خرم بهارش' = 'its joyful spring' (خرم = joyful/blissful, بهارش = 'its spring' where the -ش is the possessive suffix 'its'). 'توی' at the end of this segment is the locative/adverb 'in/within' (here functioning like 'is in you' — i.e. 'its spring is in you').
segments.s165-g416: This line uses poetic inversion: 'نگارش تویی' = 'you are its inscription' and 'غمگسارش تویی' = 'you are its consoler'. 'نگارش' = 'its writing/inscription/record'; 'تویی' is the 2nd-person singular present copula 'thou art / you are'; 'غمگسارش' = 'its consoler/comforter'. Word order (noun + تویی) is typical in classical poetry to emphasize the predicate.
segments.s165-g313: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. گفتار مهتر is the object phrase (گفتار 'speech' followed by the title/epithet مهتر 'the elder/chief'), placed before the verb in typical Persian SOV order. شنید is a simple past-tense verb 'he heard'.
segments.s165-g314: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking origin or source. قربان is a noun here (literally 'sacrifice' or sacrificial place/occasion) used with ز; کمان کیی is a noun + adjectival epithet construction: کمان 'bow' followed by کیی 'Kayanian/royal' (attribute after the noun, typical of classical Persian). برکشید is the past tense 'he drew/raised' (here specifically 'drew' a bow). The whole phrase therefore indicates he drew the (Kayanian) bow from/at the (sacrificial) place or on the sacrificial occasion (poetic usage).
