segments.s165-g315: سپهبد is the subject/title ('spahbed, general'). کمان is the direct object 'the bow'. خواست is past 'he wanted'. تا introduces a purposive/subordinate clause here ('so that/in order that') and is followed by بنگرد, the subjunctive/present-jussive verb 'he may look/examine' (ب + نگر + د): after خواست + تا the verb is in the subjunctive sense of intended action.
segments.s165-g316: یکی is an indefinite 'one/someone'. برگراید is an older/poetic 3rd-person form meaning 'should turn / incline / move' (jussive/hypothetical flavor in this context). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/if', and فرمان برد is a tight noun+verb phrase; فرمان 'order/command' is the object/nucleus, and برد here is an older use meaning 'takes/receives' (i.e. 'takes the order' or 'obeys the command' in context). Grammatically, the clause expresses the contingency the spahbed wanted to check: whether someone would turn/act who would take the order.
segments.s165-g317: کمان را: کمان 'bow' plus the definite/object marker را marks it as the direct object. نگه کرد is the compound 'to hold/keep' (نگه داشتن) in past tense 'he held/kept (it)'. و links clauses. خیره بماند uses خیره 'staring/astonished' adjectivally/adverbially and بماند (past narrative/presentive form) 'he remained/stood', so together 'he stood/ remained staring'.
segments.s165-g318: بسی is an intensive adverb 'much/very/many' (here modifying آفرین). آفرین is a noun 'acclamation/praise' (not the verb 'to create' in this context). کیانی is the adjectival epithet 'Kayanian/royal' that follows the noun it qualifies (classical order). بخواند is the past narrative form of خواندن 'he recited/uttered/sang' — here 'he uttered many Kayanian praises.'
segments.s165-g319: به marks the indirect object 'to/for'; گرسیوز is the proper name 'Garsivaz' (indirect object). تیغ‌زن is the noun 'swordsman/attacker' (the thing given). داد is the past verb 'gave'. مه is a short, archaic/vocative or epithetic particle here placed after the verb; in classical Persian texts مه can appear as an epithet or interjection (its precise pragmatic shade is poetic/epistolary), so grammatically it's a postpositive element attached to the clause.
segments.s165-g320: که introduces reported content of the order ('that /:'). خانه بمال is an imperative clause: خانه (house) + بمال (imperative of مالیدن 'rub/press/— in older usage also 'search, cleanse, beat' depending on context), i.e. a command to act on the house. و connects the commands. در آور is a compound imperative (در 'door' + آور imperative 'bring') meaning 'bring out to the door' (در here = door/threshold). به زه is a prepositional phrase 'to the string' (زه = bowstring) attached to the verb, specifying destination/aim (poetic phrasing, likely meaning 'to the bowstring' as fate/execution point). Overall this segment is an imperative sequence reported after داد / he gave (i.e. he ordered these actions).
segments.s165-g281: ازان is an adverbial 'thereupon/from that' pointing back to the preceding event; گوی is the noun 'ball' (the polo/ball object). خندان شد is a predicative construction where خندان (an adjective 'smiling/laughing') is made verbal by the past auxiliary شد ('became'/'was'), and the proper name افراسیاب follows as the subject in poetic word order (predicate before subject).
segments.s165-g282: سر نامداران is a genitive/construct phrase meaning 'the head/foremost of the renowned (men)' with سر = 'chief/leader'. برآمد is a past verb 'arose/rose up'. ز خواب is an ablative phrase 'from sleep' giving the source of the rising; overall: 'the chief of the famed arose from sleep'.
segments.s165-g283: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice / aloud' (به used adverbially). گفتند is the past plural verb 'they said' (quotative reporting), and هرگز precedes the nominal سوار to mark negation ('never ... rider'); the negative sense is completed by the verb ندیدیم in the next segment, so this segment sets up an utterance: 'they said aloud, never a rider...'.
segments.s165-g284: ندیدیم is the past first-person-plural 'we did not see' (negation with هرگز). بر + زین is a locative/prepositional phrase 'on the saddle' (بر = on, زین = saddle). چنین نامدار is an adjectival phrase 'so/such renowned' modifying سوار; together it yields 'we have never seen such a renowned rider on a saddle.'
segments.s165-g285: ز میدان is an ablative 'from the field/arena'. به یکسو is the directional phrase 'to one side'. نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed/moved' (here 'they withdrew/moved aside'). گاه at the end is a temporal adverb 'after a while' or 'then', marking the time reference for what follows.
segments.s165-g286: بیامد is the past 'he came'. نشست here is 'he sat' / 'took a seat' (a second verb describing the action that follows coming). از برگاه شاه is a locative/poetic expression 'in/at the king's court' (از used idiomatically to indicate location in literary Persian). The two verbs form a short sequence 'he came and sat (in the royal court)'.
segments.s165-g287: سیاووش is the subject (proper name). بنشست is the past intransitive 'sat down' (he took a seat). با او 'with him' marks accompaniment and به تخت 'on the throne' marks the location; poetic word order places the verb near the front and the subject/name in its normal position.
segments.s165-g288: به دیدار او is a causal/temporal phrase 'at his sight / upon seeing him' (literally 'to the meeting of him' used to mean 'on meeting/seeing him'). شاد شد is 'became happy' (past of شدن with predicative adjective شاد). شاه (the king) follows as the subject in this poetic order, and سخت is an adverbial intensifier 'very/strongly', so the clause means 'the king became very glad to see him.'
segments.s165-g505: "از افراسیاب ار بخواهی رواست" is conditional: از ... بخواهی = 'if you ask (from) Afrasiab' where از introduces the source and ار (archaic اگر) = 'if'. بخواهی is second-person present/subjunctive (you ask/request). رواست is روا + ست, a predicative phrase meaning 'it is permissible/fitting' (ست = copula; here the whole clause states the result of the condition).
segments.s165-g506: "چنو بت به کشمیر و کابل کجاست" is a rhetorical question. چنو (= چنان/چنو) = 'such'; بت is the poetic 'idol/beautiful one' used here as 'a beauty/idol'; به کشمیر و کابل gives the locale ('in/to Kashmir and Kabul'—به used idiomatically to introduce the place); کجاست is the interrogative copula 'where is it?'. Grammatically the possessor/qualifier ordering is poetic — 'چنو بت ... کجاست' = 'where is there such an idol?'.
segments.s165-g507: "شود شاه پرمایه پیوند تو" uses شود (optative/subjunctive) as 'may ... become'. شاه پرمایه is a noun + adjective phrase ('the king rich in resources' or 'the wealthy king'), and پیوندِ تو = 'your bond/ally' (possessive تو follows the noun). Word order is poetic: the verb شود appears before its subject and complement, expressing a wish/blessing.
segments.s165-g508: "درفشان شود فر و اورند تو" parallels the previous clause: درفشان (here adjective 'shining, glittering') with شود gives 'may ... become shining'. فر و اورند تو = 'your glory and Orvand' (تو as possessor applies to both nouns). The coordinate structure links فر and اورند as two things wished to be made radiant; the verb is again placed before the nouns for poetic emphasis.
segments.s165-g509: "چو فرمان دهی من بگویم بدوی" begins with چو = 'when'. فرمان دهی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you give the command'. من بگویم is 1st-person ('I will say' / 'I will speak') with the personal pronoun kept for emphasis. بدوی is an archaic/contracted form meaning 'to him' (equivalent to به وی); it is attached after the verb phrase and indicates the addressee of what the speaker will say.
segments.s165-g510: "بجویم بدین نزد او آبروی" states the speaker's purpose/result: ب + جویم = بَجویم (1st-person-subjunctive) 'I will seek/search for'. بدین is a compound adverbial 'by this/with this' (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation), and نزد او آبروی = 'reputation/honour before him' (نزد = 'in the presence of/with, before'; آبروی = 'reputation'). The whole clause means 'I will, by this, seek your honour before him.'
segments.s165-g511: "سیاوش به پیران نگه کرد و گفت" is simple narrative past: سیاوش (subject) + به پیران (toward/at the elders = target of the gaze) + نگه کرد ('looked' — compound with کردن) + و گفت ('and said'). نگه کرد here is the past of نگاه کردن; word order is subject — (prepositional object) — verb, typical in narrative sequences.
segments.s165-g512: "که فرمان یزدان نشاید نهفت" is a reported-content clause introduced by که = 'that'. فرمان یزدان = 'the command of God' (یزدان = God). نشاید is an impersonal modal meaning 'it is not fit/ought not', and نهفت (intransitive/passive infinitive/participial form of نهفتن) = 'to be hidden'/'be concealed'. Together: 'that God's command ought not to be concealed.'
segments.s165-g1089: سیاوش فرود آمد is a straightforward past action: subject (سیاوش) + compound verbal phrase فرود آمد (فرود 'descent/alighting' + آمد 'came/arrived') meaning 'alighted' or 'dismounted'. از نیل رنگ is a prepositional/qualifying phrase where از + noun (نیل 'indigo') + رنگ 'color' describes appearance — poetically saying 'of Nile‑color' (i.e. dark/indigo‑tinted) and modifying the preceding noun (Siyavash).
segments.s165-g1090: مر او را گرفت has گرفت (past 3sg of گرفتن) as the main verb 'seized/took'. را is the definite/object marker attached to او ('him'). مر is an archaic/dialectal object/dative form meaning 'me / to me' placed before the object phrase for emphasis; the prepositional phrase اندر آغوش تنگ ('in(to) a tight/close embrace') follows, with اندر = 'in/within', آغوش = 'embrace/bosom', and the adjective تنگ after the noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s165-g1091: بگشتند is past 3pl 'they returned/turned back'. هر دو = 'both' (each of the two). بدان is a fossilized combination here (ب + دان) functioning as 'to that' (i.e. 'to that place'); شارستان = 'city, citadel'. Word order is verb + subject phrase + destination (typical poetic order).
segments.s165-g1092: ز هر در زدند literally 'from every gate they struck' — ز (poetic 'from'), هر در 'every gate', زدند (past 3pl of زدن) here used idiomatically to mean 'issued forth/streamed out' or 'sounded out'. از هنر داستان is a genitive/qualifying phrase with از indicating source/about, هنر 'skill/valour/feat' specifying the kind of tale, and داستان 'tale/legend' as the noun being proclaimed.
segments.s165-g1093: سراسر همه is an emphatic adverbial 'throughout, all around'; it modifies the following list of locations. The coordinate list باغ و میدان و کاخ places gardens, public squares and palaces side by side (noun + و + noun + و + noun). This is a typical cataloging of the urban landscape.
segments.s165-g1094: همی دید is a poetic past imperfective/continuous: همی (an old verbal particle often marking continuous or frequent action) + دید (saw) = 'he kept seeing' / 'he saw'. هرسو 'on every side / everywhere'. بنای 'building' (construct) followed by فراخ 'spacious' (the adjective after the noun, as common in poetic word order) gives 'spacious buildings'.
segments.s165-g1095: سپهدار پیران is a titling phrase: سپهدار = 'commander/spahbed' followed by پیران (the name). ز هر سو براند: ز هر سو = 'from every side/direction' (ز = poetic 'from', هر سو = each side), and براند is the present/past poetic form of راندن 'to drive/dispatch' (here past or narrative present 'he drove/sent out/put into motion').
segments.s165-g1096: بسی آفرین بر سیاوش بخواند: بسی is an adverb 'much/many' (intensifier), آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'praise/bravo', بر is the preposition 'upon/for/toward' marking the addressee (سیاوش). بخواند is past/narrative form of خواندن 'to recite/sing/read' — here 'he uttered/recited praises'. The verb follows the object phrases, as is common in Persian narrative style.
segments.s165-g1073: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. پیران is the plural noun 'elders' (subject). بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' used with that clause; ز + noun (ز هند و ز چین) is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from India and from China' with the preposition repeated for rhythm and emphasis.
segments.s165-g1074: سخن رفت is an impersonal idiom: 'word/rumour was spoken' or 'word spread' (سخن is the abstract 'word/news', رفت is 'went/spread'). زان is the contraction of از آن 'from that' referring back to the city, and با آفرین is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with acclamation/praise' (با + noun).
segments.s165-g1075: خنیده is a past/pp form meaning 'sung/was sung' (here passive or stative). به توران marks the locality/target ('in/to Turan') with به used poetically; سیاووش is the proper name; گرد follows the name and here functions as a poetic noun/particle indicating the sphere or 'circling/about' of the subject (i.e. the fame/round associated with Siyavash).
segments.s165-g1076: کز is the emphatic/relative 'that/for which' introducing a causal or temporal subordinate clause. اختر بنش کرده شد is a compacted archaic construction: اختر 'star' + بنش 'seat' + کرده شد ('had been made / had taken') = 'when the star had taken its seat' (کرده here is the perfect participle used with شد). روز ارد is a named temporal phrase ('the day of Ard') functioning as the main temporal result; the exact cultural name is a proper noun.
segments.s165-g1077: A series of place-nouns introduced by از/ز ('from') and coordinated with و ('and'). The alternating use of از and ز is a poetic stylistic device but both mark origin/source ('from the iwan and the palace, and from the orchard and garden').
segments.s165-g1078: Another coordinated list introduced by ز 'from' (ز کوه و در و رود ...) with وز at the cadence — وز here is an archaic fusion of و + ز used for metrical balance meaning 'and from'. The final noun راغ ('longing/yearning') is the predicate noun implying 'there arose/was' (the verb is omitted in such poetic lines).
segments.s165-g1079: شتاب is a noun used adverbially ('haste' / 'in haste'); آمدش is the past tense verb 'he came' with the enclitic -ش attached (classical style). تا + subjunctive (ببیند) marks purpose: 'in order to see'. که begins the subordinate indirect question/nominal clause that follows, and شاه is the subject of that indirect question.
segments.s165-g1080: چه کرد is the indirect question 'what did (he) do' (چه = 'what', کرد = past 'did'). اندران is the locative/relational adverb 'in/therein' (an older form of 'در آن'), followed by نامور جایگاه 'renowned place' as the locative phrase 'in that famous place'. The subject pronoun is omitted as normal in Persian.
segments.s165-g169: یکی (numeral 'one') modifies تخت (throne) and comes before it; تخت زرین is a noun + adjective (golden throne). نهادند is the 3rd‑person plural past of نهادن 'to place, set', so the clause means 'they placed/set'. پیش here is a preposition 'before/in front of' and attaches to the whole verb phrase (object 'it' is implied).
segments.s165-g170: همه پایها is a nominal phrase 'all the feet/feet‑bases' (پایها = the feet/legs/foot‑bases of the throne). چون is the simile marker 'like/as'. سر here means the anatomical 'head' and گاومیش is 'water‑buffalo' — the clause describes the throne's feet as being shaped like buffalo heads.
segments.s165-g171: به + noun phrase (به دیبای چینی) marks the material/attire 'in/with Chinese brocade' (چینی postmodifies دیبا). بیاراستند is the 3rd‑person plural past of آراستن with the archaic/preverbal b‑ prefix (بی‑آراستـ), meaning 'they adorned/arrayed (it)'; the direct object is implied (the throne).
segments.s165-g172: فراوان here functions adverbially/quantitatively 'many/abundant' modifying پرستندگان 'worshippers/devotees' (subject). خواستند is 3rd‑person plural past of خواستن 'to want, to desire, to ask', so the clause reads 'many worshippers desired/asked (for it)'.
segments.s165-g173: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past form often translated 'he commanded/said'. پس = 'then/afterwards'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'), and رود here is the 3rd‑person singular verb (subjunctive/optative sense) of رفتن 'to go' used with تا to express what was ordered. سوی کاخ is a directional phrase 'toward the palace'.
segments.s165-g174: بباشد is a jussive/optative form ('may he be' / 'let him be') expressing desired/resultant state. به کام is an idiom 'to/for (his) desire' = 'satisfied'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. نشیند is the 3rd‑person singular present/jussive of نشستن 'to sit, to be seated' (here 'may he sit'), and فراخ is an adjective/adverb 'spacious/at ease' describing how he should sit.
segments.s165-g175: سیاوش is the proper name and subject. چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. در پیش ایوان is a locative phrase 'at/in front of the iwan' (در + پیش + noun), and رسید is the 3rd‑person past of رسیدن 'to arrive/reach' — 'when Siavash arrived before the iwan.'
segments.s165-g176: سر طاق ایوان is a genitive/possession chain 'the head/top of the iwan's arch' (سر = summit/top; طاق = arch). به marks direction/extent 'to/up to' and کیوان (literally the planet/sphere Saturn, used poetically for the sky/heavens) is the endpoint; رسید is past 'reached'. The clause says the arch's crown reached up to Kiwan.
segments.s165-g1153: سروش is the subject (the angel/messenger). آوریدش is an archaic/polite past form meaning “brought” (verb) with the enclitic object -ش “him/it”; here it functions as “brought him/it.” همانا is an emphatic adverb “verily/truly” modifying the clause; خبر is the noun “news” (the thing brought). The clause order is subject + verb(+clitic) + emphatic + object.
segments.s165-g1154: که introduces a subordinate reported clause (“that / of how …”). چونان here means “in such a way / how” (introducing the manner). نگاریدش is an older past form of نگاریدن “to adorn/beautify,” with the enclitic -ش marking a 3rd‑person object (“it/him”); the verb describes the action performed on آن بوم و بر. آن is the demonstrative “that,” and بوم و بر is a coordinated phrase “land and region/plain.”
segments.s165-g1155: همانا is the emphatic adverb “verily/truly.” ندانند is the negative present/past-form of دانستن here meaning “they do not (know / are not able to)”; in context it conveys inability or absence of return. ازان شهر means “from that city” (ازان = from that; شهر = city). باز is the adverb “back/again,” so the clause expresses that people do not come back from that city.
segments.s165-g1156: نه is the coordinating negative “nor.” خورشید is used literally/metaphorically “the sun.” ازان means “of/from that.” مهتر here is the noun “esteemed elder/chief” (a distinguished man). سرافراز is an adjective “exalted/lofty/triumphant,” describing that chief. The clause states there is no sun (i.e., nothing) more exalted than that elder.
segments.s165-g1157: یکی here means “a certain” (introducing a single notable item). شهر is the noun “city.” دیدم is the 1st-person past “I saw.” که begins the relative/subordinate clause that qualifies the city. اندر زمین is the locative phrase “in the world / on the earth” (اندر = in; زمین = earth/land).
segments.s165-g1158: نبیند is an older negative form of دیدن meaning “does not/has not seen” (here used impersonally: nobody sees it). دگر کس = “any other person / no other person” (دگر = other; کس = person). به توران و چین is a locative phrase “in Turan and China” (به = in/among; توران and چین are place names). The whole segment says no one in Turan or China has seen such a city.
segments.s165-g1159: ز بس is a fixed idiom meaning “from so much / because of so many.” The following nouns are a list: باغ (garden), ایوان (iwan/porch/portico), and آب روان (running/flowing water), with و as the coordinating conjunction. آب روان is a compound where روان qualifies آب as “flowing/runing water.”
segments.s165-g1160: برآمیخت is the past (or perfect) verb “was mingled/was blended.” گفتی here functions idiomatically as “one would say / so to speak” (a commentative particle derived from گفتن). خرد با روان is the phrase “wisdom with the spirit/soul” (خرد = wisdom; با = with; روان = soul/spirit), together meaning that wisdom seemed to mingle with the soul.
segments.s165-g241: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). بدو = به + او, a prepositional phrase meaning “to him” placed after the subject. گفت is the past-tense verb “said.” فرمان تراست is the reported clause: فرمان (noun, “the command”) followed by تراست, an older/poetic copular/verbal predicate meaning “is yours” (تَراست = ‘to/ra-ast’ = ‘belongs to you’).
segments.s165-g242: This is a nominal list (سواران و میدان و چوگان) followed by the same predicate تراست; the list is the logical subject of تراست, so the meaning is “the horsemen and the field and the polo‑mallet are yours.” چوگان here denotes the polo‑mallet (an item), and میدان means field/arena.
segments.s165-g243: سپهبد is the agent/title (spahbed). گزین کرد is a compound verb: گزین (choose/select) + کرد (past auxiliary) = ‘chose/selected.’ کلباد را is the direct object marked by را (definite object marker), so the clause reads “the spahbed chose Kalbad.”
segments.s165-g244: چو here introduces the following items as additional examples/like‑wise (literally “such as”): گرسیوز و جهن و پولاد را. The final را marks the definite object(s); in poetic prose the را can scope over the whole series. This is a simple coordination of proper names.
segments.s165-g245: چو introduces a comparison/simile: “like …”. پیران and نستیهن are coordinated names (here referring to persons), and جنگجوی follows نستیهن as an appositive/epithet ‘the warrior’ (post‑nominal descriptive phrase).
segments.s165-g246: Another simile introduced by چو: هومان is the person being described. که introduces a relative clause “who …”; بردارد is a 3rd‑person present/presentive verb meaning “lifts/takes up,” and از آب گوی is a prepositional phrase “from the water the orb” (آب = from the water, گوی = the orb/sphere). The relative clause characterizes هومان by his feat.
segments.s165-g247: به نزد سیاووش is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/into the presence of Siyâvash.” فرستاد is the past‑tense verb “sent.” یار is the direct object ‘a companion/ally’ (word order: verb before object is common in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s165-g248: چو introduces descriptive similes of that companion: رویین is an adjective meaning ‘iron/steel‑like’ (i.e., armored or hard), و چون (another ‘like/as’) links to شیده نامدار — here شیده is a name or epithet and نامدار is a postpositive adjective ‘renowned/famed.’ Together they describe the yâr as ‘steel‑clad and famed (like Shideh).’
segments.s165-g897: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that' / 'so that'). آن را is the demonstrative + object marker (آن = 'that', را marks it as definite object). کسی تا نبیند uses تا + negative subjunctive (نبیند) to express 'until/unless anyone sees' — in this context it means 'so that no one may see it.' به چشم is a prepositional phrase 'with the eye' (به + noun).
segments.s165-g898: تو گویی is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'it is as if' / 'you would say' (here not a direct address but a formula introducing an imagined judgment). ز takes the source/causal sense 'from' and گوینده is 'the speaker'; گیرند is a present/plural verb 'they take/seize' (here 'they take' in the sense 'they take offense'), and خشم is the object 'anger/resentment.'
segments.s165-g899: نیاید is an archaic/poetic negative-form verb meaning 'does not come / will not come'. برو here is a contracted poetic form of بر او ('upon it' / 'to it') rather than the verb 'go' (an old fused prepositional form). منجنیق و نه تیر lists two instruments ('catapult and not arrow' = 'neither catapult nor arrow'), with و linking them and نه negating the second member.
segments.s165-g900: بباید is the impersonal/old poetic form 'it is necessary' / 'must'; the following ت is the 2nd-person suffix (ترا = 'you' in oblique form). را is the object marker attached after the clitic; دیدن is the infinitive 'to see' (here the content of the necessity). آن is the demonstrative 'that', and ناگزیر is a predicative adjective 'inevitable/compelled' — the whole says essentially 'you must see that; it is inevitable.'
segments.s165-g901: ز marks origin/source ('from its blade'). تیغش is the possessive 'its blade/sword' (تیغ + -ش). دو فرسنگ تا is a distance expression: 'two parasangs as far as...' where تا functions as 'as far as.' بوم خاک is a genitival phrase 'the bōm (native dwelling) of the soil' = 'the native soil.' Word order is compact and poetic: source + measure + limit + place.
segments.s165-g902: همه گرد بر گرد is a tautological/poetic piling of adverbial particles meaning 'all around' (همه = 'all/entirely'; گرد ... گرد = 'around about'). خاکش is 'its soil' (خاک + -ش), and مغاک is the noun 'abyss/gulf' placed in predicate position — 'all around its soil [there is] an abyss.'
segments.s165-g903: نبیند begins with the negative/optative form 'does not/cannot see.' ز بن ('from the base') gives the vantage point; دیده is 'the eye' (subject or experiencer), بر تیغ کوه is a compound 'on the blade of the mountain' (بر = on/upon, تیغ = edge/blade, کوه = mountain). Word order is compact: 'the eye does not see from the base the mountain's blade.'
segments.s165-g904: هم starts 'also/even'. از introduces the cause ('from' / 'because of') and بر شدن here conveys the idea 'of going up / of mounting' or 'of being upon' (an infinitival/noun phrase formed with شدن). مرد is the generic 'man'; گردد ستوه is a compound verb phrase where گردد = 'becomes' and ستوه = 'weary' — 'even from attempting to get on it a man becomes weary.'
segments.s165-g289: Word order: the prepositional phrase به لشگر ('to the army') marks the addressee, سپس/پس ('then') is temporal, and چنین ('thus') is an adverb signaling reported speech; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (placed before the speaker's name). نامجوی is the speaker's proper name placed at the end for emphasis. Note: گفت is a past-tense verb here, even though its listed senses did not include the verbal 'to say.'
segments.s165-g290: که introduces reported content ('that' / 'that which he said'). میدان شما را is a definite noun phrase 'your field/arena' with شما 'you' and را as the definite/object marker; here را attaches to the definite list that follows. و ... و links the items (چوگان = polo mallet here) and گوی = the ball. The whole segment is a nominal list that functions as the content of گفت.
segments.s165-g291: همی is a habitual/progressive adverb here ('kept, continued to'). ساختند is the past plural of ساختن 'to make/arrange' — together ساختند آن دو لشکر نبرد = 'they arrayed the two armies for battle' (نبرد = 'battle' as the purpose/goal). The phrase places the verbal action first (poetic inversion) followed by the noun phrase 'the two armies'.
segments.s165-g292: برآمد is a past-tense verb 'arose/issued forth' (here, 'fighting broke out'). همی again gives continuity. تا introduces a temporal limit 'until'; به خورشید گرد is a poetic locution meaning 'around the sun' — i.e. an adverbial phrase indicating time/extent (commonly read as 'until the sun's turning / until sunset').
segments.s165-g293: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. ترکان is the plural subject 'the Turks'. به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with speed / quickly' (به + noun used adverbially). بیاراستند is past plural and here means 'they arrayed/put themselves in order / readied themselves' (literal sense 'they adorned/arranged themselves'), so the clause means 'when the Turks quickly prepared themselves.'
segments.s165-g294: همی gives a continuous or immediate sense ('then/thereupon'). بردن is the infinitival/nominal form 'to take/win' and here functions as the action they attempted; گوی را places 'the ball' as its definite object (را). خواستند is the past plural of خواستن 'they wanted/sought' — together 'they sought to take the ball.'
segments.s165-g295: ربودند is the past plural 'they seized/took' (here a neutral verb for taking possession). ایرانیان is the agent 'the Iranians' (placed after the verb in this poetic inversion). گوی is the object 'the ball', and پیش is an adverb 'before/ahead' indicating position ('kept the ball in front / held it forward').
segments.s165-g296: بماندند is past plural 'they remained / they were left / they stayed' and here means the Turks 'held back' or 'abstained'. ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'. ز is the poetic form of از 'from', introducing the cause or separation 'from their conduct' (i.e. they refrained). کردار خویش is 'their own conduct/behavior' — خوی + the possessive suffix ش = 'their own'. The whole means 'the Turks refrained from their own action/behavior.'
segments.s165-g625: سپهدار پیران is a nominal phrase: 'سپهدار' (commander) with 'پیران' after it (here treated as a genitive/appositive). The verb ببست is past-tense 3rd-person singular 'closed/blocked'. میان is the direct object and is followed by the object marker را, so literal order is Subject – Object – Verb (SOV): 'The commander (of Piran/elders) closed the middle.'
segments.s165-g626: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in one instance / suddenly' (one + occasion). تیزرو is a compound adjective/noun 'swift-runner' describing the rider/horse, and برنشست is the past 3rd-person singular of بنشستن with the prefix بر- ('mount/up upon, sit on'), here 'sprang up / mounted'. Word order is adverb – noun – verb.
segments.s165-g627: به + noun phrase (به کاخ سیاووش) is a prepositional phrase 'toward/to Siyâvash’s palace.' بنهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'he placed/put' used in the idiom روی نهادن (بنهادِ روی) meaning 'to turn one's face toward.' روی is the direct noun 'face' receiving the action.
segments.s165-g628: بسی here functions adverbially ('much' / 'many'); آفرین is 'acclamation/praise.' خواند is past 'he uttered/recited' (here 'he said/cry out praises'). بر introduces the target 'upon/over' and فر is the abstract noun 'farr' (royal glory). اوی is an old third‑person enclitic pointing back to the person whose glory is praised ('his').
segments.s165-g629: بدو is an indirect-object clitic phrase 'to him.' گفت is the reporting verb 'he said.' کامروز is a temporal adverb 'today.' برساز is the 2nd-person singular imperative of ساختن/سازیدن with the prefix بر- 'set up/arrange' (reported imperative: he told him to do this). کار is the noun 'affair/matter' that is to be arranged.
segments.s165-g630: به مهمانی indicates purpose/direction 'for the banquet.' دختر شهریار is a possessive/genitive construction 'the daughter of the shah/king' (noun + modifier), so the whole phrase specifies the occasion for which the affair should be prepared.
segments.s165-g631: چو here means 'if/when' introducing a conditional clause. فرمان دهی is a subordinate clause with دهی (2nd-person present/subjunctive form) 'you give the command.' من سزاوار او is a verbless predicate: 'I am worthy/fit for him' (سزاوار as predicate adjective taking او as its complement).
segments.s165-g632: میان را is again the definite object 'the middle' + object marker را. ببندم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive (or jussive) form 'that I close' used here to express the intended/future action ('I will close'). پی means 'after/in pursuit of' and links to کار او 'his affair' as the motive/purpose of the action. The clause thus states the speaker's pledge to act on behalf of that affair.
segments.s165-g257: مرا یار باشند بر زخم گوی is an optative clause. مرا is the object pronoun (م + را) placed before the verb for emphasis ('to me'); یار باشند is a third-person plural subjunctive/optative ('may there be companions' / 'let there be allies'); بر زخم گوی is a prepositional phrase where بر = 'against' and زخم گوی = 'the wound of speech' (زخم governed by گوی, i.e. harm coming from words). Word order reflects poetic inversion: object before verb, then the protecting/prepositional phrase.
segments.s165-g258: بران سان که آیین بود بر دو روی is an adverbial-relative clause modifying the previous clause. بران سان ('in that manner/thereupon') + سان ('like/as') is an archaic fixed phrase; که introduces the relative clause 'which/that'; آیین بود is past copula 'was the custom'; بر دو روی = 'upon both sides' (بر + دو روی). The clause states that the companions should act 'as was customary on both sides.'
segments.s165-g259: سپهبد چو بشنید زو داستان is a temporal clause. سپهبد is the subject; چو = 'when'; بشنید is simple past 'he heard'; زو is the poetic contraction of از + او ('from him/it') marking the source of what was heard; داستان is the object 'the tale/story.' The clause means 'when the spahbed heard the story from him.'
segments.s165-g260: بران داستان گشت هم‌داستان is the result clause. بران داستان ('thereupon/because of that story') is an adverbial phrase; گشت is past 'became/turned'; هم‌داستان is a predicate adjective 'of the same mind/like-minded.' Subject (سپهبد) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s165-g261: سیاوش از ایرانیان هفت مرد is a noun phrase with subject Siawash implied to be acting: سیاوش (proper name); از ایرانیان = 'from the Iranians' (source or group); هفت مرد = 'seven men' (the selected individuals). Word order places the source phrase before the counted noun phrase.
segments.s165-g262: گزین کرد شایسته کارکرد is the verbal clause. گزین کرد is a two-word past active ('chose / selected' — گزین + کرد, with کرد functioning as the light verb making the past); شایسته is an adjective 'worthy/fit'; کارکرد is a noun 'performance / carrying out of the work' and here completes the idea 'worthy to carry out the task.' The phrase means 'he selected (them), worthy to perform the work.'
segments.s165-g263: خروش تبیره ز میدان بخاست is a past narrative clause. خروش = 'a roar/uproar' (subject); تبیره modifies or specifies that roar (an older/poetic modifier); ز میدان = 'from the field/arena'; بخاست is the past simple with the b‑prefix 'arose/rose up.' The verb is intransitive and the phrase pictures a sound rising from the battlefield.
segments.s165-g264: همی خاک با آسمان گشت راست is a compact descriptive clause. همی is an old/poetic adverbial intensifier (here 'indeed/altogether'); خاک = 'dust/earth' (here 'dust' kicked up); با آسمان = 'with the sky' (together/up to the sky); گشت is past 'became' or 'turned'; راست = 'straight/level' describing the alignment. Together it conveys that the dust rose so high it seemed to meet or become level with the sky.
segments.s165-g137: ازین پس (از + این + پس) is a fixed temporal phrase 'from now on/after this'. نه ... نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. آشوب خیزد uses خیزد (present/simple-future-like ‘arise’ or ‘begin’) with آشوب as its subject; the verb stands before the second negated noun (poetic word order).
segments.s165-g138: به آبشخور آیند is a clause meaning 'come to the watering-place': به + مكان and آیند (3rd‑pl. present) 'come'. The subject میش و پلنگ (ewe and leopard) follows the verb, a common poetic V‑S order in Ferdowsi.
segments.s165-g139: برآشفت is a past/result verb 'was stirred/grew agitated'. گیتی is the subject; ز introduces the cause ('because of'), followed by تور دلیر 'Tūr the brave' as the causal agent. Word order V S PP (verb before subject) is a poetic inversion.
segments.s165-g140: کنون 'now' fronts the line. روی گیتی 'the face/surface of the world' is the noun phrase; شد ... سیر is a copular construction 'became sated/filled', with از جنگ marking the thing it is sated of ('sated with war'). شد is perfective/past 'became'.
segments.s165-g141: دو کشور 'two countries/realms' is the subject. سراسر 'throughout' modifies the subject; پر از شور بود is the predicate 'was full of tumult' (پر از = 'full of', شور = 'tumult/commotion'), with بود marking past tense.
segments.s165-g142: جهان را places 'the world' as a topical/emphatic element with the object marker را; دل ... کور بود is the core clause 'the heart was blind'. از introduces the thing the heart is blind to ('آشتی' = reconciliation), so دل از آشتی کور بود = 'the heart was blind to reconciliation.'
segments.s165-g143: به تو رام گردد زمانه is an optative/wish construction: گردد (subjunctive of 'become') expresses a desire 'may the time/age become tame to you', with به تو marking the beneficiary and زمانه the subject. کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb.
segments.s165-g144: برآساید is another optative/subjunctive verb 'may it find repose / be appeased'. از جنگ 'from war' and وز جوش خون together mean 'and from the surge/boiling of blood' — وز is a poetic contraction (و + ز) meaning 'and from'; جوش خون is 'the boiling/surge of blood' (i.e. bloodshed). The whole phrase prays for release from war and bloodshed.
segments.s165-g81: یکی introduces an enumerated item (‘one [thing]’). آنک is a relative/pointing particle meaning ‘that’ or ‘which’. از is the preposition ‘from’ and تخمه is used as ‘offspring/seed’ (i.e. lineage source); کیقباد is a proper name (Kay Qobad). The whole phrase functions as a topic: ‘one (is) that from the seed of Kay Qobad…’.
segments.s165-g82: همی is an emphatic/continuative particle used with verbs (‘would/continually’). از تو گیرند is a verb phrase with گیرند (3rd-person plural present/subjunctive/indicative in Classical Persian) meaning ‘they take/derive from you’ and از تو marks source. گویی here is a parenthetical 'one might say / it seems', and نژاد is the noun ‘lineage/descent’ completing the idea. (Note: گویی’s conversational senses in the supplied list do not match the literary 'it seems' usage.)
segments.s165-g83: و دیگر introduces a second item (‘and another’ or ‘another thing’). زبانی is a substantive meaning ‘a tongue/speech’ (an attribute or thing expressed). The sequence written as ب + دین in the data corresponds to the prepositional/demonstrative combination in بدین (‘in/with this’), and راستی means ‘truth’ — together: ‘in this truth’. (The split of بدین in the word list makes دین’s supplied senses irrelevant here.)
segments.s165-g84: به is the preposition ‘in/with’ linking to گفتار (‘speech’), نیکو modifies گفتار (‘good/fine speech’), and بیاراستی is an archaic verb form from آراستن meaning ‘you have adorned/you set forth’ — here functioning as the predicator (‘you expressed/adorned [it]’). Word order is preposition + noun phrase followed by the verb form in an older poetic register.
segments.s165-g85: سه دیگر (‘three others’) announces three additional items. که introduces the subordinate content clause. گویی functions parenthetically as ‘as if’ or ‘one would say’ (the supplied senses do not capture this literary use). که... از چهر تو begins the clause ‘that from your face…’, with از marking source and چهر تو ‘your face’.
segments.s165-g86: ببارد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive verb meaning ‘would rain/fall’ (used metaphorically). همی is the continuous/emphatic particle, بر زمین is the locative phrase ‘upon the earth’, and مهر تو (‘your love/affection’) is the thing that falls; note Persian places the possessor after the noun. The clause reads ‘it would constantly rain your love upon the earth.’
segments.s165-g87: چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’. داد is the past tense verb ‘gave’ and پاسـخ is its object ‘answer’ — together ‘gave such an answer’. سیاووش is the subject placed after the verb (poetic word order). بدوی in the word list does not match a clear sense here (likely a manuscript/segmentation issue), so its supplied senses are not used.
segments.s165-g88: که‌ای introduces direct speech or a vocative and here functions like ‘saying, “O…”’. پیر is a vocative ‘old/elder’ (respectful), پاکیزه is an epithet ‘pure/clean’ and راست‌گوی is an epithet ‘truth-telling/honest’. Together they form the address: ‘O pure and truthful elder!’
segments.s165-g745: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a clause describing an antecedent. سالار پیران is a nominal phrase 'leader (سالار) of the elders (پیران)'; in older epic Persian the ezāfe linking is often not written, so the relation is shown by word order. ازان is the archaic contraction of از آن ('of/from that'), and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s165-g746: که again functions as a relative 'who/which'. از introduces the source/measure ('from' or 'because of') and combines with بدگمانیش ('his suspiciousness' — بدگمان + possessive ‑ش). بی‌بهر is an older compound meaning 'deprived of / without portion/benefit', and بود is the past copula; the whole means he was without (or unaffected by) that suspiciousness.
segments.s165-g747: همی + بود is an epic/imperfective past construction meaning 'he was/kept being' (stative or continuous past). یکماه is a duration 'one month' and مهمان او 'a guest of him/his guest' places the possessor after the noun with the pronoun او.
segments.s165-g748: بران is an adverbial 'thereupon/upon that' and سر here functions idiomatically ('on that head/about that point'); چنین بود 'thus was' is the predicative phrase, and پیمان او 'his covenant/pledge' places the possessor after the noun. Word order is adverbial phrase → predicate → possessed noun.
segments.s165-g749: ز (poetic from از) + خوردن is an ablative/gerundial phrase 'from/with eating'. نیاسود is the negative past participial/verb 'did not rest/was not satisfied'. The temporal phrase یک روز ('one day') and the subject شاه ('the king') follow in the typical flexible epic word order: temporal → predicate → subject.
segments.s165-g750: گهی is an adverb 'sometimes/now and then'. رود here is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'he would go' (the verb 'to go' in a subjunctive/habitual sense); و is the conjunction 'and'; می‌گاه can mean 'occasion/time' (or a wine‑place in other contexts) and is linked to نخچیرگاه 'hunting-ground' as the destination. The sequence gives habitual movement: occasionally he went (to) the mey‑place and the hunting-ground.
segments.s165-g751: سر ماه is an idiomatic temporal phrase 'at the head/start of the month' (سر = start). برخاست is the past intransitive 'rose/was raised'. آوای کوس is a possessive construction 'the sound (آوا) of the drum (کوس)'; in older Persian the linking ezāfe is often absent but the relation is clear.
segments.s165-g752: برانگه is an adverb 'thereupon/then'; که introduces a subordinate clause. خیزد is a present/habitual verb 'arises/begins to rise' (poetic present), and خروش خروس is a noun phrase 'the cry/clamor (خروش) of the cock (خروس)'. The clause says that immediately the cock's cry arose or began.
segments.s165-g905: This short clause begins with a prefixed imperative/attention marker 'ب' + 'دان' (here read as a verbal root meaning 'know' or 'be mindful'), forming 'بدان' (literally 'know/consider this'). 'آفرین' is used poetically as a nominal/agentive form related to creation ('Creator' / 'praise') rather than the exclamatory 'bravo', and 'کان' is an archaic connective/relative linking to the following verb; 'چنان' ('thus') is an adverb modifying the verb 'آفرید' that closes the clause.
segments.s165-g906: 'ابا' here is a terse/poetic particle whose exact force is opaque in this line (an archaic or emphatic element); 'آشکارا نهان' are two coordinate adjectives/adverbs (the manifest and the hidden) that together form the direct objects modified by the verb 'آفرید' ('created'). The final 'آفرید' is a past tense (perfect) verb agreeing with the implied Creator mentioned earlier.
segments.s165-g907: نبایست is an older/poetic negative form built on the modal 'بایست/باید' with the negative prefix 'ن-' and the archaic copular/ending '-ست', giving the sense 'there is no need' or 'it is not proper/necessary'. The nouns 'یار' (companion) and 'آموزگار' (teacher) are listed as things he requires not; the conjunction 'و' simply links them.
segments.s165-g908: This clause places a prepositional phrase 'بر همه کار' ('over/on all work/affairs') before the descriptive adjectives 'دشوار خوار'. The initial 'برو' is a terser, poetic verb form (here functioning as a verbless/stative notion 'he is above' or 'he stands over'), followed by 'بر' (indicating 'over/above'), and then 'همه کار' ('every affair'); 'دشوار' and 'خوار' are adjectives describing the affairs (difficult and humiliating). The construction is elliptical and typical of epic diction.
segments.s165-g909: The clause begins with the restrictive particle 'جز' ('except'), followed by the pronoun 'او' ('him'). The postposed object marker 'را' emphasizes the object. 'مخوان' here is an imperative/negative poetic form meaning 'do not call/assign' (cf. negative imperative usage), so the sense is 'do not call anyone (Creator) except Him.' 'کردگار جهان' is a nominal phrase 'Maker/Doer of the world' functioning as the predicate/name that must not be ascribed to others.
segments.s165-g910: This parallel clause repeats the restriction: 'جز او را' ('except him') with 'مدان' as an imperative/prohibitive verb meaning roughly 'do not consider/know' (an archaic negative form). The adjectives 'آشکار و نهان' ('manifest and hidden') qualify Him, i.e., He alone is both the manifest and the hidden. The coordination 'و' links the two adjectives.
segments.s165-g911: 'به' is the dative/prepositional marker 'to/for,' taking 'پیغمبرش' ('his prophet') as its object. 'بر' functions as a preposition here ('upon' / 'toward'), and 'کنیم' is the 1st‑person plural subjunctive/optative of 'کردن/کن' meaning 'let us do' or 'we should do.' 'آفرین' is used as 'praise/acclamation' — so the clause reads literally 'to his prophet upon [him] let us do praise' = 'let us praise his prophet.'
segments.s165-g912: This clause continues with 'بیارانش' — a poetic/orthographic form here representing 'به یارانش' ('to his companions') — followed by 'بر' ('upon/to') and the distributive 'هر یکی' ('each one'). 'همچنین' ('likewise') closes the clause. Grammatically it is a dative construction parallel to the previous segment, asking that praise also be given to each companion.
segments.s165-g97: بدو is a prepositional/clitic phrase meaning “to him” (prepositional object placed before the verb). گفت is the past 3rd-singular verb “said” (word-order here: indirect object + verb + subject). پیران is the subject (the name Pīrān); که introduces the subordinate content clause that follows. مندیش زین is an archaic/poetic formulation: مندیش is a short, older-form imperative/negative or verbal phrase linked to زین (here ‘this’ or literally ‘saddle’), so the clause means roughly “do not (leave/abandon) this (saddle).”
segments.s165-g98: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” اندر is the locative/prepositional particle “in/into” (here part of the verbal phrase meaning to pass out of a place). گذشتی is the 2nd/3rd-person past of گذشتن “you/he passed.” ز is the poetic form of از “from.” ایران‌زمین is the compound noun “land of Iran.” Together: “when you/he pass(ed) out of the land of Iran.”
segments.s165-g99: مگردان is a negative imperative form meaning “do not turn (away).” دل is the direct object “heart.” از introduces the source/relationship “from/of.” مهر here means “love, affection.” افراسیاب is the proper name Afrāsīāb — the phrase = “do not turn your heart away from love for Afrāsīāb.”
segments.s165-g100: مکن is another negative imperative “do not (do).” هیچ‌گونه is an adverbial intensifier “in no way/at all.” به رفتن is an infinitival phrase “to going/for going” (the preposition + verbal noun). شتاب here functions as “haste/to hasten” — combined with مکن it yields “do not hasten to go.” Word order places the negative auxiliary (مکن) up front, then the adverbial qualifier and the infinitive phrase.
segments.s165-g101: پراگنده is an adjective “widespread.” نامش is the noun نام “name/reputation” with the 3rd-person possessive clitic -ش “his.” به گیتی is the locative phrase “in the world.” بدی‌ست is the predicate: بدی (badness/ill) + the archaic present copula -ست (is) — “his widespread name in the world is bad.”
segments.s165-g102: ولیکن is the adversative conjunction “but/yet.” جز اینست literally “except/aside from this” (جز followed by demonstrative اینست). مرد ایزدی‌ست is the following predication: مرد “man/hero” + ایزدی‌ست “is divine/heavenly” (ایزدی ‘of the divine’ + copula -ست). Overall: “But aside from that, he is a divine man.”
segments.s165-g103: خرد دارد is a simple predicate: خرد “wisdom” + دارد “he has/possesses” (present-tense, 3rd singular). The coordinated nouns رای “opinion/counsel” and هوش “intellect” follow, with بلند “high/lofty” placed after هوش as an attributive adjective (poetic word order). The line lists qualities he possesses.
segments.s165-g104: به خیره نیاید is an idiomatic negative clause: به + خیره (“into a daze/astonishment”) + نیاید (3rd-singular present negative of آمدن “to come,” used here as “does not come/become”) → “he does not become bewildered.” به راه گزند is a directional/goal phrase “to the road of harm” (= “into danger”); together: “he does not enter a stunned state that would lead to harm.”
segments.s165-g921: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'); 'از رنج' is a prepositional phrase 'from/of suffering'; 'دیگر' here means 'other/another'; 'کسی' is the indefinite subject 'someone/anyone'; and 'برخورد' is used as a verbal idea 'to meet/encounter' (classical Persian often places the verb before its subject). Together this segment expresses the notion 'that someone should encounter another suffering.'
segments.s165-g922: 'جهانجوی دشمن' is a compound noun phrase: 'جهانجوی' (world‑seeking, ambitious) modifies 'دشمن' (enemy). 'چرا' is the interrogative 'why', placed before the verb, and 'پرورد' is an archaic/past poetic form of 'پرورْدن' ('to rear/nurture' or 'to raise'). The clause reads 'why did the world‑seeking enemy rear (him)?'
segments.s165-g923: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; 'خرم شود' is a predicate with the adjective 'خرم' ('joyful/prosperous') and the verb 'شود' ('becomes'); 'جای' is the noun 'place' (the subject), and 'آراسته' is a participial/adjectival form 'adorned'. Word order is typical classical Persian (verb before subject/adjective phrase).
segments.s165-g924: 'پدید آید' is a verb phrase meaning 'appear/come into view' (پدید + آید); 'از هر سوی' is a prepositional phrase 'from every direction/side'; and 'خواسته' is an adjectival or nominal form meaning 'desired' or 'the wished‑for'. The clause means that what is desired appears from all sides.
segments.s165-g925: 'نباشد' is a negative jussive/optative form meaning roughly 'may there not be' or 'let there not be'; 'مرا بودن' functions as a noun phrase 'my being / my existence'; 'ایدر' is the poetic form of 'here'; and 'بسی' means 'much/long' or 'to a great extent'. The whole unit expresses a wish that the speaker's life/existence here not be long.
segments.s165-g926: 'نشیند' is the jussive/subjunctive of 'نشستن' ('may [someone] sit' / 'let [someone] sit'); 'برین' is 'بر + این' (on/onto this) written together as a poetic form; 'جای' is 'place'; and 'دیگر کسی' literally 'other someone' = 'any other person'. The clause therefore means 'may no other person sit upon this place.'
segments.s165-g927: The paired negation 'نه ... نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. 'من' is the first 'I'; 'شاد باشم' uses the present/subjunctive form 'باشم' with the adjective 'شاد' ('happy') to express 'I be happy' (here negated); 'فرزند من' is 'my child' as the second conjunct. The structure lists things the speaker denies/wishes not to happen.
segments.s165-g928: 'نه' continues the negation; 'پرمایه' is an adjective meaning 'substantial, well‑endowed' (here 'prosperous/wealthy'); 'گردی' in this line is a problematic form for the supplied sense list (it functions here as a verbal/derivational element meaning 'come to be' or 'one who appears'), so no exact match is in the provided senses; 'ز پیوند من' = 'from my bond/lineage' (ز = from, پیوند = link/lineage, من = my). The clause says that no prosperous/wealthy person will arise from the speaker's line.
segments.s165-g457: گر is the conditional particle 'if' introducing a hypothetical. او is the third-person pronoun 'he/that one.' باشدم is an archaic subjunctive/optative 1st-person form ('were I / if I were'), used here after گر to mark the counterfactual. نازش is ناز + the enclitic -ش (his) = 'his nâz' (his affection/favor); جان و تن is a fixed pair meaning 'soul and body' (the whole person) and follows the possessive to specify what the speaker would be.
segments.s165-g458: نخواهم is the 1st-person negative present/future form 'I will not / I do not want' (here: 'I would not want'). جزو کس is a nominal phrase ('any person' / 'no one' in negative contexts) where جزو means 'member/part' and کس means 'person/anyone'; together after the verb it yields 'I would want no one.' ازین انجمن is the prepositional phrase 'from this assembly,' where از + ین (an older/poetic form of این) marks origin/source and انجمن is 'assembly/company.'
segments.s165-g459: سپاسی is the noun 'thanks/praise' and functions as the object affected by the prohibition. نهی here is a negative imperative/prohibitive form meaning 'do not withhold / do not give' (classical/poetic usage). زین is a compressed/poetic form (cf. 'از این') used to point to 'this' matter; همی is an archaic particle meaning 'ever/indeed' that adds emphasis, and بر سرم literally 'upon my head' is an idiom meaning 'to my account/regard.' Together the phrase is a prohibition addressed to the addressee: 'Do not (place/heap) praise on me for this.'
segments.s165-g460: که introduces a clause of explanation/reason ('for' / 'because' / 'that'). تا زنده‌ام is a temporal clause 'as long as I am alive' (زنده 'alive' + enclitic ام = 'I am alive'). حق آن is 'that right/due' (حق = right/entitlement, آن = that/demonstrative). نسپرم is the 1st-person negative present/subjunctive form of سپردن ('to entrust/hand over') with the negative prefix ن-: 'I will not hand it over' or 'I will not surrender it.' The verb comes at the end of the clause, as is typical in Persian.
segments.s165-g461: "پس آنگاه" are stacked adverbs meaning "then/after that" (a common poetic redundancy). "پیران" is the subject "the elders." "ز نزدیک اوی" is a prepositional phrase "from near him" where "ز" = "from," "نزدیک" = "near/close," and "اوی" is an archaic/enclitic third‑person pronoun "him/his."
segments.s165-g462: "سوی" is the preposition "toward(s)." "خانه خویش" = "their own house," here split as "خوی + ش"; together they form the reflexive possessive "خویش" (own). "بنهاد" is the past form (بنهادن) meaning "placed/turned" and combines with "روی" (face) to mean "to turn one’s face toward." Note the verb appears in an older/poetic past form (بنهاد).
segments.s165-g463: "چو" is the temporal conjunction "when." "پیران" is the subject "the elders." "ز پیش" means "from before/in the presence of" (here "from the presence of Siavash"). "برفت" is an older past form of رفتن (he/they went/left); in epic poetry the 3rd‑person past form may appear with plural subjects.
segments.s165-g464: "به نزدیک" = "to/into the vicinity of." "گلشهر" is a place name (Gulshahr). "تازید" is the past of تازیدن meaning "rushed/charged; galloped" and the following word "تفت" here functions as the subject (name Taft) placed after the verb — a permissible poetic word order (V S).
segments.s165-g721: Subject (فرستاده) precedes the past verb آمد; the prepositional phrase ز نزدیک شاه ('from near the king') follows the verb. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', نزدیک functions adverbially/relationally ('near'), and آمد is simple past (classical narrative past).
segments.s165-g722: به نزد is a doubled directional/prepositional expression meaning 'to / into the presence of'; نزد here marks the person addressed. یکی نیک‌خواه is an appositive phrase: یکی ('a certain' / indefinite) + نیک‌خواه ('well-wisher'); the indefinite precedes the noun in classical style.
segments.s165-g723: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('who/that'). پرسـد is the classical/habitual-present form 'asks' (narrative-present used in epic). همی is an older habitual marker reinforcing continuous action. شاه را uses the definite object marker را; شهریار is an appositive/title clarifying/renaming شاه (typical classical apposition after را).
segments.s165-g724: همی گوید is another habitual-present construction 'he says/keeps saying' (همی marks continuative aspect). ای introduces a vocative ('O'); مهتر نامدار is the vocative title 'renowned / illustrious chief' (adjective following the title). Note: گوید here functions as the reporting verb 'says'.
segments.s165-g725: بود is existential/past 'there was'. کت is the noun 'coat' (the thing that exists). ز من ('from me') with poetic z indicates the effect upon the speaker; دل بگیرد is a semi-idiomatic clause 'the heart is seized / the heart is taken' (بگیرد here is a present/habitual form expressing repeated or characteristic effect), and همی again marks continuous/habitual aspect.
segments.s165-g726: وزین should be read as 'و زین' (and from this / and of this) where زین refers to the weighty/dignified seat; برنشستن is the verbal-noun/infinitival phrase 'to sit upon' and گزیرد (habitual/present) means 'he avoids/escapes' doing that. همی again signals habitual action. Word order places the negated/avoided verbal-noun before the verb of avoidance.
segments.s165-g727: از ایدر ترا داده‌ام is a perfect construction 'I have given/sent you from here.' The enclitic ت is the 2nd-person object suffix attached (rendered separately here) and را is the definite object marker; داده‌ام is 1st-person perfect 'I have given/sent'. تا ... چین uses تا + به with the sense 'as far as' (extent) and چین is 'China' as destination.
segments.s165-g728: یکی here functions adverbially 'once/one time.' گرد + برگرد forms an imperative-like instruction 'turn/go around' (گرد 'around', برگرد imperative/verb 'turn/return'); the conjunction و links the next imperative بنگر ('look' — 2nd-person imperative), and زمین is the object 'the earth/land' to be observed.
segments.s165-g305: یکی here functions as an indefinite/individuating word ('someone/somewhat' or 'one [thing]') placed before the noun; گوی (literally 'sphere' or 'ball') is the direct object of بینداختند (they threw). The verb بینداختند is past plural and completes the clause 'they threw X' with the actor ترکان (the Turks) preceding the verb, reflecting the flexible subject–object–verb order of classical Persian.
segments.s165-g306: به کردار آتش is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in the manner of fire' (به used here in the instrumental/manner sense), کردار names the manner or conduct, and آتش is the simile-base. همی is the classical progressive/continuous particle that accompanies past tense to give a vivid or iterative sense, and تاختند is past plural 'they charged/attacked', so the whole clause reads 'they charged like fire'.
segments.s165-g307: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'); سپهبد is the subject (the spahbed, a high commander), آواز ترکان is the object phrase 'the Turks' cry/voice', and شنود is an archaic 3rd-person verb meaning 'he heard' (classical form). The clause therefore means 'when the spahbed heard the Turks' cry'.
segments.s165-g308: بدانست is past tense 'he knew/realized' where the prefix به- is fused into the verb; کان here functions as a demonstrative/relativizer pointing to 'that/so-called', پهلوانی is the noun 'heroism/valour', چه بود is a free relative ('what it was'). Together: 'he understood/realized what that heroism was.' Note the classical word order: verb (بدانست) before the explanatory clause.
segments.s165-g309: چنین ... گفت پس opens with 'thus' or 'in this way' (چنین) and گفت (here functioning as the verb 'said'); پس is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. شاه توران is the nominative 'the king of Turan' and سپاه (here the indirect object) marks the addressee 'to the army' — classical Persian often places the addressee after the subject before the verb.
segments.s165-g310: که here introduces reported content ('that'); گفته‌ست is perfective/periphrastic 'it has been said' or 'he has said' (reporting the information). با من 'with me' indicates the king's relation to that report, یکی is an indefinite 'one/someone' and نیک‌خواه is the predicative noun 'a well-wisher' — the whole subordinate clause: 'that it is said someone is a well-wisher of mine.'
segments.s165-g311: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'); او را uses the object/contrast marker را to emphasize 'him'; ز گیتی کسی نیست reads literally 'from the world no one is' — idiomatically 'there is no one in the world equal to him' (کسی نیست = 'there is no one'). جفت here means 'pair/equal' in the sense 'match/peer'.
segments.s165-g312: به تیر و کمان is an instrumental phrase 'with bow and arrow' (به used adverbially), چون introduces a simile/temporal 'when' or 'as', گشاید is the verb 'he loosens/opens/releases' (here the action of shooting or drawing), and سفت qualifies the shot as 'firm/fierce'. The clause describes his archery: 'when he lets fly with bow and arrow, he sends two powerful/firm [shots].' Note classical omission of an explicit word for 'arrow(s)' — the instruments and adjective suffice to imply it.
segments.s165-g225: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun (subject). باشی is a poetic/optative/present form meaning “be” (here: an instruction or assignment of position: “be/stand”). بدان‌روی is a compound adverbial phrase meaning “on that side/with that face” (literally “with that aspect”); و joins the two parallel locatives; زین‌روی من places زین‌روی (“on this side/from this aspect”) before the pronoun من for emphasis (in Persian the pronoun can follow the locative for contrast). The verb is implied again for the second clause (copula omitted, as is common in poetry): “You be there and I (be) here.”
segments.s165-g226: بدو is the dative pronoun/adverb “to him/unto him” (not the imperative ‘run’ here). نیم is the noun/adjective “half” used predicatively with an omitted copula (i.e. “I am half/part of him”); هم adds “also/too.” زین here qualifies نشان (a token or sign) — although زین can mean a saddle/trappings, in this poetic collocation it marks a specific token — and انجمن names the fellowship or assembly the token symbolizes. The clause therefore reads as a compressed, poetical statement: “I am also half to him — this token of the fellowship.”
segments.s165-g227: سیاوش is the speaker’s name (subject). بدو is the dative “to him/unto him”; گفت is the past tense verb “said” (here introducing direct speech). کای is the vocative particle “O” used to address a king or lord, and شهریار is the addressee title “sovereign/king.” The sequence is a standard reported-speech formula: “Siyâvash said to him, ‘O king…’.”
segments.s165-g228: کجا is the interrogative/adverb “where.” باشدم is an archaic/poetic form (optative or past) related to “to be” (here rhetorical: “where should I be / where would I be?”). دست و چوگان form a compound object “hand and polo-mallet” (دست here denotes skill or the hand used in play; چوگان is the polo-mallet). به کار is a fixed phrase meaning “of use / in use / useful.” Together the clause asks rhetorically about the usefulness of his hand and mallet: “Where would my hand and mallet be of use?”
segments.s165-g229: برابر here means “equal” or “on a par.” نیارم in this context is a poetic negative first-person verb meaning “I cannot match / I do not equal” (the supplied lemma senses do not include this verbal sense, so the correct sense is marked separately). زدن is the infinitive “to strike” (here: to hit the ball), با تو means “with you” (i.e. in contest with you), and گوی is the “ball” (literally “sphere”). The clause asserts inability to match the addressee at striking the ball: “I am not your equal in striking the ball.”
segments.s165-g230: به میدان sets the location “to/into the field/arena.” هم‌آورد is a noun meaning “equal/rival” (one who is a match). دیگر is “another/other,” and بجوی is the 2nd-person imperative/subjunctive of جستن “seek!” (addressing the addressee). The clause is an imperative: “Seek another who is your equal in the field.”
segments.s165-g231: چو is a conjunction meaning “as/when/since” (here causal: “since”). هستم is the copula “I am” (poetic form of بودن), and سزاوار is an adjective “worthy/fit.” یار is “companion/ally,” and توام (a fused form meaning “I am with you / I am your companion”) closes the clause. The clause therefore gives the reason or premise: “Since I am worthy to be your companion…” (note the somewhat compressed word order typical of poetry).
segments.s165-g232: بر + این (written here as برین) is a locative demonstrative “on this/at this.” پهن میدان is the noun phrase “wide/broad field/arena.” سوار is a noun/adjective “rider/mounted (one)” and توام repeats the fused form meaning “I am with you / I accompany you” (here: “I am your mounted man”). Word order places the locative first for emphasis: “On this broad field I am your rider.”
segments.s165-g1033: This is an exhortative clause using the prepositional phrase + imperative pattern: 'به شادی بباش' and 'به نیکی بمان'. The initial به + noun ('به شادی', 'به نیکی') marks the state or sphere ('in joy', 'in goodness'), and the verbs بباش and بمان are imperative forms (poetic/archaic) meaning 'be' / 'remain' — the prefix بـ (here part of the imperative form) adds an emphatic/exhortative tone.
segments.s165-g1034: The clause is elliptical (a common poetic move): 'تو شادان' addresses the addressee ('you, be joyful') and the next phrase names the adversary/qualifier: 'بداندیش تو با غمان' (lit. ‘the ill‑minded toward you, with sorrow’). The verb 'is' is omitted — the noun/adj. construction (بداندیش تو) and the prepositional phrase (با غمان) stand as a verbless nominal predicate meaning 'your ill‑wisher is full of grief'.
segments.s165-g1035: بدان پادشاهی: the first word is the fused/prepositional expression indicating 'to/into that' or 'toward that' (classical poetic contraction). پادشاهی is the object 'kingship/kingdom'. همی is the archaic progressive/continuous particle giving a habitual or immediate sense, and بازگرد is an imperative/infinitive meaning 'return' or 'go back'. Together they command or announce returning to that kingship.
segments.s165-g1036: This is an imperative infinitival phrase: سر (the head) + بدسگال (an adjective 'ill‑omened'/'ill‑fated') is the direct object, and اندرآور (imperative/command form of آوردن) means 'bring in' or 'insert'. The final prepositional unit به گرد ('into/around the circle') specifies direction/place. Word order is object before verb (poetic flexibility) and the verb اندرآور governs the following locative phrase.
segments.s165-g1037: Subject + verb + conjunction + verb: 'سیاوش' is the subject, 'سپه برگرفت' uses برگرفت (past form) meaning 'raised/gathered (the) army' and then و links to 'برفت' (an older past-tense form of رفتن) meaning 'he went/left'. The sequence reports two consecutive past actions.
segments.s165-g1038: بدان سو is a fixed classical locative: the prefixed b- + دان here yields 'to/that side' (toward that direction). که introduces a relative/clausal subordinator 'that/which', and فرمود (past) = 'he ordered/said' modifies سالار (the commander). The final word تفت appears as a name or specific instruction tied to سالار; its meaning is context‑dependent (not a common lexical verb here).
segments.s165-g1039: This clause describes an action of loading: 'صد (one hundred) اشتر' — in classical Persian اشتر usually 'camels' — 'ز گنج و درم بار کرد' literally 'from the treasury he loaded with dirhams.' ز is the poetic form of 'from', گنج is the treasure/treasury, درم (dirham) names the coin, بار is 'load', and کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made' meaning 'he loaded'. Word order is numeral + noun + source phrase + instrument/contents + verb.
segments.s165-g1040: چهل را همه بار دینار کرد is a compact accusative construction: 'چهل را' (the forty — with را marking a definite object or set) + 'همه' ('all') + 'بار' ('loads') + 'دینار' (dinar coins) + کرد ('he made/filled'); together: 'he made/filled all forty (loads) with dinars.' The object marker را emphasizes the definite group, and the verb کرد closes the clause in past tense.
segments.s165-g33: بشد is the past/preterite form of شدن here meaning 'he became' or 'he sprang'; it takes the following adjective تیز as its predicative complement ('became/grew swift'). The coordinated verb phrase بگرفتش (بَ + گرفت + -ش) follows with the object-suffix -ش 'him' (he seized him). اندر کنار is a prepositional phrase 'at/at the side' placed after the verb as the location.
segments.s165-g34: بپرسیدش is a past-form verb 'asked him' (ب- prefix + پرسید + -ش object suffix = 'they/one asked him'). از here means 'concerning/about' taking the following NP نامور شهریار 'the famed king' as its object.
segments.s165-g35: بدو is a prepositional/locative clitic phrase 'to him' (به + او collapsed as بدو) and functions as the indirect object; گفت is the past tense of گفتن 'he said' introduced after the indirect object (classical word order). کای is the vocative marker 'O' addressing the addressee, followed by the title/noun phrase پهلوان سپاه 'champion of the army'.
segments.s165-g36: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. رنجه is used as a verbal base meaning 'to cause suffering / to distress'; کردی is the 2nd-person past 'you did' (you caused). روان را is the definite object (روان + object marker را) — 'the caravan / the people / the traveling party' or literally 'the caravan/soul' — and به راه is the adverbial phrase 'on the road'.
segments.s165-g37: همه بر دل literally 'upon every heart' (همه = all/everyone, بر = on/upon, دل = heart) sets the scene for what came into people's minds. اندیشه 'thought' is the head noun, and این بد نخست is a compressed modifier order ('this bad first' = 'this bad thought first'), with نخست 'first' closing the phrase — a common archaic/poetic word order.
segments.s165-g38: که introduces a content clause 'that/which'. بیند is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive of دیدن here with an optative flavor 'may (one) see' or 'should see'. دو چشمم is 'my two eyes' with the possessive suffix -م split in the segmentation; تَ (here shown as ت) plus را form the direct object 'you' (ت/تَ + را = ترا). تندرست is the predicate adjective 'healthy'. The clause expresses the wish/vision that 'my two eyes may see you healthy.'
segments.s165-g39: ببوسید is the past tense 'kissed' (verb). In poetic prose the verb may appear before its subject; پیران here is the proper name 'Piran' (the spahbed) acting as the agent. سر و پای او is the coordinated direct object 'his head and feet' (او = him).
segments.s165-g40: همان is a demonstrative 'that very/the same' emphasizing the following description. خوب‌چهر is a compound adjective 'fair‑faced' and دلارای is an epithet 'beloved/charming' modifying او ('him'); the sequence is a tightly packed descriptive tag restating who was kissed.
segments.s165-g377: سپاهی is a collective noun 'a host/army' (subject). ز هرگونه is a poetic prepositional/genitive phrase ('of/from every kind') modifying سپاهی. با او is the accompaniment 'with him'. برفت is a past tense verb (archaic/poetic form equivalent to رفت) placed at the end as Persian normally puts the verb after its constituents; it means 'went'.
segments.s165-g378: از ایران و توران is a prepositional phrase giving origin or the groups involved ('from Iran and Turan'). بنخچیر functions as a noun of purpose/goal 'to the hunt' (نخچیر = prey/hunt) and is attached here with the prepositional/poetic prefix بِ- pattern. تفت appears in the verb slot at the end and is an archaic/poetic verbal form; none of the supplied senses match the hunting sense, so its exact lexical sense in the provided list doesn't apply (see senses).
segments.s165-g379: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). به + noun (به دشت اندرون) marks a locative/directional phrase 'into/within the plain' (اندرون = 'inner/within'). گور is the direct object 'a grave'. دید is the past-tense verb 'saw/beheld' placed at the end, completing the clause.
segments.s165-g380: چو is a conjunction/adverb meaning 'when' or 'as'. باد is used in a simile 'like the wind' (here image/adverbial). از میان سپه is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the midst of the army' (میان = 'middle', سپه = 'army'). بردمید is a poetic past verb meaning roughly 'swept away/off' or 'bore off/seized' and functions as the clause's verb; the whole segment describes a sudden movement 'when, like the wind, he swept from among the host.'
segments.s165-g381: This is two coordinated nominal-predicate clauses. سبک شد عنان = 'the reins became light' where سبک 'light' is predicate adjective and شد is the past/auxiliary 'became'. و is the coordinating 'and'. گران شد رکیب = 'the rider became heavy' with the same predicate structure (گران 'heavy' + شد). Note the adjective + شد pattern is the common way to express 'became X' in Persian.
segments.s165-g382: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'. تاخت is the past verb 'galloped/rushed'. اندر introduces the locative phrase 'in/over' and فراز و نشیب is a paired noun phrase 'heights and depths' (ups and downs), so the clause says he continually galloped over rises and falls.
segments.s165-g383: یکی را is the marked direct object 'one / someone' (را is the definite/direct-object marker). به شمشیر زد is an instrumental construction 'struck with the sword' (به marking instrument, زد past of زدن 'to strike'). بدو نیم is a compact clause: بدو is the dative 'to him' and نیم 'half', yielding the idiomatic result 'struck him in half' (i.e. cleft him). Word order places the object and instrument before the verb and then result phrase after it, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s165-g384: دو دستش is 'his two hands' (دو 'two' + دست 'hand' + enclitic ش 'his'). ترازو is a noun 'scale/balance' used metaphorically here; بد is an adjective 'bad/ill' modifying ترازو (so 'an ill-balanced scale' or 'a poor balance'). و is 'and'. گور سیم is a noun + modifier 'grave of silver' (گور 'grave' followed by سیم 'silver' as a material/descriptor). The line strings these images together elliptically: his two hands / a poor balance / and a silver grave.
segments.s165-g1017: ز هر سو = a prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز + هر + سو). سپاه انجمن شد is literal 'the hosts became an assembly/assembled': انجمن شد uses شد ('became') with a noun to mean 'assembled' in an older/poetic style. به روی = a locative/relational phrase 'before/in front of' (به + روی) specifying where they assembled.
segments.s165-g1018: یکی لشکری گشت: 'یکی' = 'one' (a single), 'لشکری' = 'an army/host' (adjectival/collective noun), and 'گشت' is the past/preterite 'became' or 'turned into'. پرخاش جوی is a compound adjective: 'پرخاش' (attack/aggression) + 'جوی' (‑seeking) = 'attack‑seeking' or 'aggressive'; the modifier follows the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s165-g1019: به نزد سیاوش = 'to/at Siavash's presence' (به + نزد + proper name). بسی here is an intensifier 'many/much' (classical usage) and خواسته functions as a noun/past participle 'requests' or 'those who asked' (i.e. 'many requests/petitioners came'). Word order: the locative phrase precedes the comment about the number/requests.
segments.s165-g1020: ز دینار و اسپان آراسته = 'adorned/arrayed with dinars and horses.' ز is the instrumental/associative preposition 'with/from', دینار و اسپان list the items, and آراسته is a past participle/adjective 'adorned' describing the petitioners or their gifts.
segments.s165-g1021: به هنگام پدرود کردن = 'at the time of bidding farewell' (به هنگام + noun phrase پدرود کردن, with کردن forming the verbal noun). بماند is a narrative past/past participle meaning 'he remained/ stayed behind' (بـ‑ + ماند in classical narrative usage).
segments.s165-g1022: به فرمان برفت = 'by/at the command he went' (به فرمان = 'by order'; برفت is a poetical/past form of رفتن 'to go' used here to mean 'departed'). و سپه را براند = 'and (he) drove off the army': را is the definite object marker, and براند (past) = 'drove away/put to flight.'
segments.s165-g1023: هیونی ز نزدیک افراسیاب = 'the (savage) hyuni/creatures from near Afrasiab.' هیونی is a plural/collective name (poetic) for fierce creatures or savage fighters; ز + نزدیک indicates their origin 'from near (Afrasiab).' The word order places the origin after the noun.
segments.s165-g1024: چو آتش بیامد = 'they came like fire' (چو = 'as/like', آتش = 'fire', بیامد = past 'came' with the poetic prefix بی‑). به هنگام خواب = temporal phrase 'at the time of sleep/while (they) slept.' The clause is a simile comparing the advance to fire.
segments.s165-g113: فدای تو بادا is an optative clause: بادا is a wish/optative ('may it be'), with فدای تو ('devoted/sacrificed to you') as the predicative phrase. The following همه هرچ هست supplies the implicit subject — 'all/whatever exists' — so the whole means 'May all that exists be devoted to you.'
segments.s165-g114: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/diminutive particle ('by chance / a little'), and سازی here names a musical instrument or musical playing. به شادی نشست is a light verbal phrase where نشست ('settled/sat') means 'began/settled into' — together 'the instrument began to play in joy.'
segments.s165-g115: پذیرفتم is first-person past 'I accepted' / 'I received'. از پاک یزدان gives the source/attribution 'from the Pure Yezdan (God)'. ترا is the object (ت = 2nd-person suffix + را = object/emphatic marker), so the clause literally: 'I accepted you from the Pure God.'
segments.s165-g116: به here marks the means or accompaniment ('by/with'). رای و دل هوشمندان is a coordinated phrase 'the counsel/opinion and the hearts of the wise' — i.e. 'with the consent and affection of the wise.' ترا repeats the object with the accusative marker را for emphasis.
segments.s165-g117: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. بر تو نیاید is a negative jussive/present ('may not come/befall you') — نیاید here expresses 'should not come'. ز بدها گزند places the source of danger 'harm from bad things/evils.' The whole: 'that no harm from evils come upon you.'
segments.s165-g118: نداند is the classical negative-present form 'does not know' (the negation here yields 'no one knows' in combination with کسی). Word order is inverted (verb before کسی); راز چرخ بلند is the object phrase 'the secret/mystery of the high sky/heavens.'
segments.s165-g119: مگر functions as 'unless/except'. کز is the classical contracted preposition 'from which / from' and with تو gives 'from you'. آشوب خیزد is a present/subjunctive 'turmoil/unrest arises' (خیزد = 'arise/begin'), and به شهر marks the direction 'into the city.'
segments.s165-g120: بیامیزی is an older-form prohibition/nominalized negative ('non-mixing' / 'do not mix' in context) instructing against mixing; از دور gives the source/placement 'from afar'; تریاک و زهر are 'opium and poison.' Together the clause warns against bringing/mixing opium and poison from afar.
segments.s165-g153: سپه‌دار is the nominal subject 'the commander'. دست سیاوش is a possessive noun phrase 'Siyâvash's hand' (دست + proper name), and به‌دست here functions as a circumstantial/relational phrase 'into the hand / by the hand' that links this phrase to the following verb (it expresses that the commander grasped or came holding Siyâvash's hand). The whole chunk is a pre-verbal circumstantial phrase describing how the commander arrived.
segments.s165-g154: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به تخت مهی is a destination phrase with به marking 'to/onto the throne' and مهی is an epithet 'radiant/beautiful'; بر نشست is a verb phrase meaning 'sat upon' where بر (on/upon) combines with the past verb نشست 'sat' to give the final action. Together these verbs continue the action introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s165-g155: به روی سیاوش is a prepositional phrase 'at the face/of the person Sîyâvaš' (به + روی + name = 'towards the face of'); نگه کرد is a light-verb construction where نگه (gaze) + کرد (did) = 'he looked/gazed'. و گفت simply coordinates the next action 'and said', introducing reported speech.
