segments.s165-g156: که introduces the content clause 'that…'. این را uses the demonstrative این with the object-marker را for emphasis ('this one'); به گیتی is an adverbial locative where به is used poetically to mean 'in' or 'within' the world (گیتی). کسی نیست جفت is the predication 'there is no one who is a match' — کسی نیست = 'no one exists' and جفت = 'a pair/match', so 'no one is his equal.'
segments.s165-g157: نه negates the clause. زین‌گونه مردم بود در جهان literally 'there were no people of this sort in the world': زین‌گونه (of this kind) modifies مردم (people), بود is the past copula 'there were/was', and در جهان gives the locative 'in the world'. Word order places the copula after the noun phrase, typical of Persian narrative style.
segments.s165-g158: چنین is a qualifier 'such' modifying the following nominal list. The sequence روی و بالا و فر و مهان is a paratactic listing of attributes joined by و (and): روی 'face', بالا 'height/loftiness', فر 'royal glory', مهان 'guests/hosts' (here used as an attribute). This list functions as the specification of what makes the people 'of that sort' in the previous clause.
segments.s165-g159: ازان پس is a temporal sequence 'after that/thereupon'. به پیران marks the indirect object 'to the elders' (پیران = elders). چنین گفت رد is a compact expression 'he said thus a reply' where گفت is the verb 'said' and رد is a noun 'reply/rejection' functioning as the object — i.e., 'he replied thus' or 'he gave this rejection.'
segments.s165-g160: که again introduces reported content: 'that…'. کاووس is the subject name. تندست is a predicate describing Kavus ('tendast' here = impetuous/quick in action), joined by و to the next predicate. اندک خرد is a noun phrase 'little wisdom' (اندک modifying خرد), so the clause states two qualities: 'Kavus is impetuous and of little wisdom.'
segments.s165-g1145: که is the relative/complementizer 'that' linking to the previous clause. از introduces the cause/means ('by, through'); داد here is a noun 'justice' (not a verb), followed by شه 'the king' (possessive relationship: 'the king's justice'). گشت is the past-tense intransitive verb 'became' (third-person singular), and آباد بوم is the predicate 'prosperous/inhabited land' (آباد adjective + بوم 'homeland'). Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic: cause ('از داد شه') precedes the verb and predicate.
segments.s165-g1146: ز (poetic form of از) marks the starting point 'from'; دریای چین is a genitive construction 'Sea of China' (دریای + چین). تا ... به marks extent 'as far as / up to'; تا به دریای روم = 'as far as the Sea of Rome'. The doubled particles تا + به is a common classical combination meaning 'up to/towards'.
segments.s165-g1147: وزانجا is a compound of و + زانجا 'and from there' (linking back to the prior place). به کار سیاوش is the noun phrase 'the affair/matter of Siyâvash' (به here is part of the dative/locative flavor marking the nominal 'matter' as topic). رسید is the past verb 'reached/arrived' (3rd sg); the clause means 'from there the matter of Siyâvash arrived/reached (them or the court)'.
segments.s165-g1148: سراسر همه is an emphatic adverbial 'entirely / completely'; یاد کرد is a light-verb construction where یاد (literally 'remembrance/mention') plus کرد 'made/did' = 'related/recounted'. آنچ is the relative 'that which / all that', and دید is the past verb 'he saw'. Together: 'he fully recounted all that he had seen.'
segments.s165-g1149: ز here = 'about / concerning' (poetic variant). کار سیاوش = 'the affair/act of Siyâvash' (noun + genitive). بپرسید is the past simple 'asked/enquired' (3rd person singular, literary form). شاه placed after the verb is the subject in postverbal position, common in epic/poetic word order: 'the king asked about Siyâvash's matter.'
segments.s165-g1150: وزان = 'and from that / and about that' (linking particle). شهر و آن کشور و جایگاه is a coordinated list of direct objects/queries: 'the city and that country and the site/location.' The conjunction و links the items; آن before کشور marks it as 'that country' (demonstrative).
segments.s165-g1151: بدو = 'to him' (dative pronoun). گفت (here a past-tense reporting verb) introduces direct speech: 'the elders said to him'. پیران is the plural subject 'the elders'. که begins the reported clause. خرم بهشت is a noun phrase with adjective before noun: 'joyful/paradisiacal paradise' or more naturally 'paradise is joyful.'
segments.s165-g1152: کسی is an indefinite pronoun used here with the following negative sense to mean 'no one'. کاو is an archaic/poetic relative/pronoun ('that/its like') not represented by the provided senses (hence -1). نبیند is the present-subjunctive/negative form of 'to see' used as a general statement 'sees' in a clause implying 'no one sees...' (classical Persian uses this form for general negation). به اردیبهشت locates the comparison in 'Ardibehesht' (here the divine/ideal realm rather than merely the month). The whole line means roughly 'no one sees its like in Ardibehesht.'
segments.s165-g633: Literal word order is possessor + را + possessed: 'سیاووش را دل ... بود' = 'Siavash had a heart ...'. را here follows the possessor (an older/poetic possessive/object marking). پر is an adjectival predicate meaning 'full (of)', آزرم is an archaic/poetic noun here (its precise sense is literary/obscure) and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s165-g634: This clause begins with the preposition ز ('from' / 'in the presence of' in older style) introducing the cause or locus; پیران is the noun 'elders'. رخانش is a poetic/compound form (here functioning as a word for his visage/countenance in front of them) and is therefore archaic; پر از شرم is the predicate 'full of shame' (پر + از + noun), and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s165-g635: بدو is the prepositional/indirect object 'to him'. گفت here is the speech verb ('said') introducing direct speech (the lexicon entry given is conversational but the function is 'he said'). رو here is elliptical/poetic (an imperative or particle meaning 'go/do' in context) and connects to the command that follows; هرچ = 'whatever', باید = a modal 'must/ought', and بساز is an imperative 'make/arrange/do'.
segments.s165-g636: تو دانی = 'you know' (تو = you; دانی = present/past knowledge verb in narrative register). که introduces a subordinate clause: 'that ...'. از تو = literally 'from you' (here part of the idiom meaning 'between you and me'), م + را is the clitic pronoun/morpheme sequence representing 'me' with the object marker, نیست = 'is not/there is no', راز = 'secret'. The whole clause means 'you know there is no secret from you to me' → 'there is no secret between us.'
segments.s165-g637: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'heard'. پیران is the subject 'the elders'. سوی + خانه = 'toward the house' (سوی = toward; خانه = house). رفت is the past 'went'. The clause thus gives a time-and-motion event: 'when the elders heard, they went to the house.'
segments.s165-g638: This is a compact nominal+verb phrase: دل و جان = 'heart and soul' (two nouns joined for emphasis). ببست is a past verb meaning 'he bound / he tied' (here 'dedicated' or 'bound' figuratively). اندر آن کار = 'in/within that task/affair' (اندر = in; آن کار = that work) and تفت is a descriptive noun/adj (poetic) meaning something like 'scorching/ardent' — modifying the nature of the task. Grammatically, it's a simple past clause: he bound his heart and soul to that task.
segments.s165-g639: در خانه = 'in the house'. جامه نابرید is a compact clause: جامه = 'garment' and نابرید here is a negative/past form meaning 'did not cut / did not strip / did not alter' (in context: he did not change or remove his garment while at home). This is an instance of compressed narrative style common in epic Persian.
segments.s165-g640: به گلشهر = 'to Golshahr' (به = to/for; گلشهر = place name). بسپرد is the verb 'entrusted/handed over' (past), پیران = 'the elders' (indirect object/recipients), and کلید = 'key' (the thing entrusted). Word order is typical Persian: direction/place → verb → indirect object → direct object.
segments.s165-g849: The clause starts with the noun 'بیابان' (the desert/wilderness) followed by the classical narrative verb form 'بیاید' (here functioning as 'appears/comes'). 'چو' introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when', and 'دریا گذشت' is a simple past clause 'the sea passed'. The sequence is poetic but transparent: 'a desert appears when the sea passed.'
segments.s165-g850: 'ببینی' is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/imperative form of دیدن used in narrative contexts to mean 'you will see' or 'you see.' The noun phrase 'یکی پهن بی‌آب دشت' is indefinite ('a certain/wide waterless plain'): 'یکی' = 'a certain', 'پهن' = 'wide', 'بی‌آب' is a compound adjective 'without water', and 'دشت' is the head noun 'plain.' Note the adjective(s) follow or cluster with the noun in poetic order.
segments.s165-g851: 'کزین' is a locator meaning 'from this' or 'when you pass this.' 'بگذری' is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive (used narratively: 'you pass' / 'you go past'). 'بینی' again is 2nd-person present ('you see'). The adjective and noun 'آباد شهر' form the object 'a prosperous (آباد) city (شهر)': Persian poetry allows flexible order, so 'بینی آباد شهر' = 'you see a city, prosperous.'
segments.s165-g852: 'کزان' = 'from which' picks up a source relation to the previously mentioned place. 'شهرها' is plural 'the cities.' 'بر توان داشت' combines the preposition 'بر' + noun 'توان' ('power/ability') + past verb 'داشت' ('had'), i.e. 'had power/strength over/derived from (it)'. 'بهر' here is a prepositional/ purposive element ('for' / 'to'), so the clause means that those cities derived strength/benefit from it. Word order is relatively free: object/agent and prepositional phrases follow the relative particle.
segments.s165-g853: 'ازان پس' is a temporal adverbial 'after that.' 'یکی' is the indefinite 'a certain' and precedes the noun 'کوه' (mountain). 'بینی' is again 2nd-person present 'you see.' The adjective 'بلند' ('tall/high') follows the noun in this poetic order, describing the mountain.
segments.s165-g854: The relative particle 'که' introduces a clause modifying 'کوه.' 'بالای او' = 'its summit/top' (literally 'the top of it'), where 'او' refers back to the mountain. 'برتر از' is a comparative 'higher than,' and 'چون و چند' is an idiomatic poetic sequence meaning 'many/various others' or 'any number of others.' Together: 'whose summit is higher than many others.'
segments.s165-g855: This phrase names the mountain/locale and then locates a fortress inside it: I read 'مرین' here as a proper name (a specific mountain), followed by 'کوه را' (the mountain, with the object/topic marker 'را'), then the compound 'گنگ دژ' where 'گنگ' ('mute' / 'silent' / 'impassive') qualifies 'دژ' ('fortress, citadel'). 'در میان' is the locative 'in the middle/within.' The poetic order places the defining adjectives and locatives after the head noun.
segments.s165-g856: This clause states that no harm comes to that fortress from knowledge. 'ب' is the old/prepositional form 'به' ('to/for') fused here; 'دان' is the verb-root 'to know' appearing as part of the phrase; 'کت' is an archaic/poetic word (here read as 'book' / 'record' or a term for the citadel in context), and 'ز دانش' literally 'from knowledge.' 'نیاید' is the negative-present classical form 'does not come,' and 'زیان' = 'harm/loss.' Word order is poetic: goal/recipient + source + negative verb + patient.
segments.s165-g833: تو (you) is the topical/emphatic subject (poetic 'thou'). چنگ (harp) is the direct object; classical Persian often places object before verb. فزونی here is a noun used adverbially ('with abundance/excess') modifying زدی. زدی is a simple past/perfect of زدن 'to strike/play' and governs the object. بر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'over/upon the world' giving the scope or arena of the action.
segments.s165-g834: گذشتند is perfect/past plural 'they passed / passed by / went by'. بر تو is the prepositional complement 'past/over you' (classical usage often puts بر + pronoun to indicate passing relative to someone). بسی is an adverb here conveying abundance/extent. همرهان is plural 'companions' (an older form related to همراه); the whole clause says many companions passed by (or left) you.
segments.s165-g835: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. زان is a demonstrative/comparative element ('of those / from that') pointing to the famous people just mentioned. نامداران جهان is a genitive phrase 'the famed/renowned ones of the world'. شد تهی is a light verb construction: شد (became) + تهی (empty/devoid) — 'became empty' (an idiom for passing away or being gone).
segments.s165-g836: This is a negative question addressed to 'تو'. تو is subject; تاج فزونی is a noun phrase 'crown of excess/abundance' (فزونی modifying تاج). چرا introduces a rhetorical question 'why'. برنهی is an archaic/poetic verbal phrase here meaning 'do you not cast off / do you not set aside' — it functions as the predicate of the question (see note: the exact lexical entry shown is limited, so the form is interpreted as an older compound meaning 'cast off/remove').
segments.s165-g837: نباشی is a negative imperative/optative 'do not be' (second person). The phrase بدین گفته is 'to/with this saying' (بدین = به + این, 'to/with this'; گفته = 'saying/statement') and functions as the complement describing what to avoid. همداستان is a predicate/adjective 'like‑minded / of the same mind' — together the clause means 'do not be in agreement with this statement.'
segments.s165-g838: یکی شو is an imperative sequence: یکی 'one / together' + شو (imperative of شدن) 'become' — 'become one' or 'unite'. بخوان is the imperative of خواندن 'read/recite' and takes نامه (object) 'book/record'. باستان is a postpositive adjective 'ancient' qualifying نامه. The two verbs are sequential imperatives urging union and then reading the old record.
segments.s165-g839: کزیشان is a fused classical form (کز + ایشان) meaning 'from them / because of them' (a causal/relative marker). جهان is the subject 'the world'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly'. آباد is a predicate adjective 'prosperous/inhabited'. بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order here is typical of classical Persian: relative/cause + subject + adverb + predicate + copula.
segments.s165-g840: بدانگه is a temporal/discourse adverb 'at that time / thereupon'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that'. اندر is an archaic preposition 'in/within'. جهان is the locative complement 'in the world'. داد is the noun 'justice' (not a verbal form here), and بود is again the past copula 'was' — so the clause means 'when there was justice in the world.'
segments.s165-g953: بدان is an imperative formed from ب + دان (be‑prefix + دانِدن ‘to know’): here it means “know (this).” تا introduces purpose/result (“so that”). نگویی is ن (negation) + گویی (2nd‑person present/subjunctive of گفتن/گوییدن) and functions as a negative admonition “you should not say” or “do not say.” چو بینی is a temporal clause (چو = “when”) with بینی (2nd‑person singular present of دیدن) “when you see,” and جهان is the object of بینی (“the world”).
segments.s165-g954: که is the subordinating conjunction “that.” این is the demonstrative “this.” بر before a person (بر سیاوش) functions classically as “to/for/against” that person (here: ‘from/for Siavash’ in the sense ‘hidden from Siavash’). چرا is the interrogative “why,” شد is the past/resultive verb ‘became/happened,’ and نهان is an adjective/adverb “hidden” — together: “that this became hidden from Siavash.”
segments.s165-g955: تو‌ای is a vocative address (“O you”/“thou”), poetic in register. گرد here names the group or circle around someone (literally “around/assembly”), followed by پیران “elders.” بسیار modifies پیران as “many” (a large number of elders). هوش is a noun meaning “attention/awareness” and functions as an imperative exhortation (“be attentive”).
segments.s165-g956: بدین is a fused locution (به + این) meaning “to/with these” or “to this (matter).” گفتها is the plural noun “sayings/utterances” (the object). پهن is an adverb/adjective “wide/broad” modifying the verb that follows. بگشای is an imperative (2nd‑person singular) from گشودن “open” (here transitive), and گوش (“ear”) is used idiomatically for attention: ‘open wide your ears’ = ‘listen closely.’
segments.s165-g957: فراوان is an adverb/adjective meaning “often/many (times)” or “abundantly.” بدین here again is به + این “with/at this”; it links back to the foregoing clause (“with this / because of this”). نگذرد is the negative present/subjunctive/optative of گذشتن “to pass” (literally “does not/ will not pass”), and روزگار is an abstract noun often meaning ‘time’ or ‘fate’ — together indicating that time/fate often does not pass without what follows.
segments.s165-g958: که introduces the subordinate clause explaining what does not pass. بر دست is a prepositional phrase literally “upon (the) hand,” idiomatically “by the hand of” or “at the action of.” بیداردل is a compound adjective (بیدار + دل) meaning “wakeful‑hearted” or “tender/alert‑hearted” describing the شهریار (king), which names the agent who ‘raises his hand’ or acts — i.e. the compassionate/alert king intervenes.
segments.s165-g959: شوم is a 1st‑person subjunctive/wish of شدن (“may I become” / “let me be”); in classical poetic usage it expresses a wish or lament (“would that I were…” / “woe to me”). زار is an adjective “wretched,” and من is the 1st‑person pronoun “I.” کشته is a past participle/adjective “killed/slain.” بر introduces the following circumstance (here ‘in relation to’ / ‘while’), and بی‌گناه describes the speaker as “innocent.” The sequence is an exclamatory lamentation: “Woe is me, wretched, slain while innocent.”
segments.s165-g960: کسی دیگر means “some other person” (کسی = someone; دیگر = other). آراید is a present/subjunctive/optative of آراستن “to adorn/put on” used here in the sense “may (someone) put on/assume.” این تاج و گاه is the object: این تاج “this crown” and گاه is a poetic word often used with تاج to mean the seat/throne or sovereignty; together they mean the crown and rulership that another may take. The verb expresses a lamenting wish that another will receive the crown and seat.
segments.s165-g1129: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'came'. به شادی is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('with joy' / 'joyfully'); here به functions adverbially. به ایوان خویش is a goal phrase 'to his iwan' where خویش = خوی + ش (possessive suffix) means 'his own'.
segments.s165-g1130: همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately'. شد is the past/formal verb 'became' or 'was' used here as the main intransitive verb ('was, came to be'). در شبستان خویش is a locative prepositional phrase 'in his bedchamber' with خویش again the possessive form (خوی + ش).
segments.s165-g1131: به marks the indirect object 'to Golshahr' (به گلشهر = 'to Golshahr'). گفت is the past tense 'said' (here 'he said'). آنک introduces the reported content ('that'); خرم بهشت is a noun phrase 'the joyful/blissful Paradise' with the adjective خرم placed before the noun in poetic order.
segments.s165-g1132: ندید is the negative past 'did not see'. و is the coordinating 'and'. نداند is a literary/persian present or timeless negative form 'does not know' (here used in a general statement). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that …'. رضوان is a personified figure (Ridwan, here the agent associated with paradise) and چه کشت is an interrogative/indefinite phrase 'what (it) planted/what was planted' — کشت functions as 'planting/cultivation' in this context rather than 'killing'.
segments.s165-g1133: چو is the temporal 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بر گاه = 'on the hour/at the time' (بر = on/upon, گاه = time/occasion). فرخ is an epithet 'auspicious/fortunate' qualifying سروش (Soroush, here the angel/messenger); the adjective precedes the noun in poetic style.
segments.s165-g1134: نشسته is a participial/predicative form 'seated / having sat' used as the main descriptive verb. به آیین is an adverbial phrase 'in/with ceremony' (به used adverbially). و با فر و هوش coordinates two complements introduced by با 'with' — فر (royal/divine glory) and هوش (awareness/perception) — giving 'with glory and awareness'.
segments.s165-g1135: به رامش is an adverbial phrase 'in tranquillity / with peace' (به used to make an adverbial phrase). بپیمای is an imperative/optative form 'traverse/go across' (classical imperative/subjunctive of پیمودن). لختی is an adverb meaning 'for a short while' and زمین is the object 'the earth' — together 'traverse the earth for a little while in peace.'
segments.s165-g1136: برو is the imperative 'go.' شارستان سیاوش is the object phrase 'the city (or stronghold) of Siavash' with the noun شارستان followed by the proper name. ببین is the imperative 'see' (i.e. 'go and see'). The two imperatives form a short instruction: 'go — see the city of Siavash.'
segments.s165-g673: The clause is subject + predication + extent: زمین (subject) + باغ گشت (predicate: باغ + گشت, where گشت is the past third-person of گشتن 'to become') and the adverbial span از کران تا کران ('from edge to edge') follows to indicate extent. گشت is a poetic past tense here meaning 'became'.
segments.s165-g674: ز is the poetic preposition meaning 'from' / 'with' indicating cause or accompaniment; it governs a coordinated noun phrase (شادی و آوای رامشگران). آوای رامشگران is a genitive construction: 'the voice/melody of the رامشگران'. The whole segment functions adverbially: 'with joy and with the melodies of the رامشگران'.
segments.s165-g675: به پیوستگی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in succession' or 'continually' (به + پیوستگی). بر گوا ساختند literally 'they made (it) upon witness' — ساختند is the 3rd-plural past of ساختن 'to make/compose', and گوا here functions as 'witness' (object/role assigned). Overall: 'they made it a continuous testimony.'
segments.s165-g676: چو = 'when'. زین here is a demonstrative 'this' in sense of 'this (aforementioned)' but that demonstrative sense is not in the provided sense list (so marked -1). عهد و پیمان is a paired noun phrase 'oath and covenant/pledge', and بپرداختند is the past 3rd-plural of پرداختن used here in the sense 'to fulfil/attend to/perform' (they fulfilled/kept). The clause gives the temporal condition: 'when they fulfilled this oath and covenant.'
segments.s165-g677: پیامی is the indefinite/direct-object 'a message'. فرستاد is the past 3rd‑person singular 'sent'. پیران here is the proper name Pīrān (not the plural 'elders'), so although the provided senses list gives only 'elders' it is a personal name in context (marked -1). چو دود is a simile 'like smoke' (چو = 'as/like', دود = 'smoke'), indicating the message's swiftness or spread.
segments.s165-g678: به + place: به گلشهر 'to Golshahr' (destination). گفتا is the past tense 'he said'. The following words give the content of the utterance in an elliptical, vocative/imperative-like form: فرنگیس (the addressee/name) and زود 'quickly/soon' — i.e. 'Tell/bring Ferangis quickly' or vocatively 'Ferangis — quickly.'
segments.s165-g679: هم امشب combines هم ('also/even') with امشب ('tonight') to mark time/emphasis. به کاخ سیاوش is the destination phrase 'to Siavash's palace' (به + noun phrase with possessive ordering). رود here is a verb 'he goes / will go' (3rd person); the verb form رود is identical in spelling to the noun 'river' but is a verbal present/subjunctive used narratively to indicate future or imminent action.
segments.s165-g680: This enumerates qualities of the (implied) subject: خردمند و بیدار و خامش 'wise and awake and silent'. The final رود repeats the verb and applies to the whole coordinated predicate: '(he) will go, wise, alert, and silent.' Note خامش is an adjective meaning 'silent', and رود again is the verbal form (verb-sense not listed in the provided noun senses, thus marked -1).
segments.s165-g1137: خداوند ازان شهر نیکوترست is a nominal phrase plus a predicative adjective. Here ازان (= از + آن) works in a genitive/relational sense ('of that' / 'from that') linking خداوند to شهر: 'the lord of that city'. نیکوترست is an older fused form of نیکو + تر + است (comparative/superlative + copula), meaning 'is better / is the finest'. Word order places the possessor phrase before the predicate.
segments.s165-g1138: تو گویی here is an idiomatic, somewhat impersonal phrase meaning 'one would say' or 'it seems as if' (literally 'you would say'); گویی is used as a quotative/impersonal verb. فروزنده is an adjectival/participial modifier 'shining, radiant' and خاورست is a predicate (خاور + -ست 'is'), literally 'is the East' — together giving the metaphor 'one would say (he) is the shining East.'
segments.s165-g1139: وزان is a fused classical particle (= و + ز + آن) meaning 'and/from that' or 'from that place'; جایگه is a noun 'place/spot'; نزد + name is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / to (the house of) Afrasiab' — so the phrase sets the origin/location and the addressee: 'from that place to/at Afrasiab.'
segments.s165-g1140: همی رفت is an older/perfective-past continuous phrasing meaning 'he went / he proceeded' (همی marks ongoing or emphatic action in older Persian). برسان here functions as a verb meaning 'to bring/put (onto)' or 'to launch' and is governed by رفت — 'he went and put/launched'. کشتی بر آب is the object plus a locative/goal: بر + آب = 'onto/on the water', so 'he launched the ship onto the water.'
segments.s165-g1141: بیامد is simple past 'he came'; بگفت is past 'he said' with the older b- prefix; آن کجا کرده بود is a clause functioning as reported content: آن 'that', کجا 'where', کرده بود is the pluperfect (کرده = past participle + بود auxiliary) meaning 'had done/been' — together 'he came and said where he had been/done it.'
segments.s165-g1142: همان is a demonstrative 'that very' or 'the same'; باژ (a poetic word) means 'cry/clamor/report' and کشور 'realm/country' attaches as its domain. The relative clause که آورده بود ('which he had brought') uses آوردن in the pluperfect (آورده = past participle + بود), so the whole segment means 'the very cry/report of the country that he had brought.'
segments.s165-g1143: بیاورد is a past-tense form 'he brought' (classical past). پیشش = پیش + ـش is a locative/benefactive meaning 'before him / in his presence'; همه سربسر is an emphatic collocation meaning 'all completely / everything' (سربسر intensifies همه). Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase and the emphatic noun phrase after it.
segments.s165-g1144: بدادش here is an older past form meaning 'he gave to him' (بداد = داد + b- prefix in classical usage, with enclitic -ش 'to him'); ز کشور سراسر خبر is a prepositional phrase 'news from throughout the country' (ز = from, کشور = country, سراسر = throughout/all over, خبر = news/report). The clause thus means 'and he gave him news from all over the land.'
segments.s165-g593: چرا introduces a rhetorical question ('why'). بر گمان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on (the) supposition' with the preposition بر before its noun. باید + چشید is a modal construction: باید (impersonal 'one must/ought to') followed by the verb چشید (infinitive/present-subjunctive form of 'to taste/try') yielding 'one should taste/try' — here used ironically to ask why one would do that.
segments.s165-g594: دم مار is a noun phrase 'the tip/edge (i.e. tail) of the snake' — in poetry the ezāfe (دمِ مار) is often reduced to adjacent nouns. خیره is an adjective applied to the snake ('stunned/astonished' or 'gazing'), placed before the predicate. نباید گزید is an impersonal prohibition: نباید ('one must not/should not') + گزید (infinitive/past-stem of گزیدن) meaning 'pluck/poke/choose' here 'pick at' or 'provoke'.
segments.s165-g595: بدو گفـت is an inverted, terse word order common in poetry: بدو (به + او) 'to him' precedes گفت 'said', and پیران is the subject 'the elders' (so: 'the elders said to him'). که‌ای شهریار is a vocative formula (که ای = 'O') addressing the king: که‌ای = 'O' + شهریار 'king'.
segments.s165-g596: دلت را = دل + 2nd-person suffix -ت + object marker را: 'your heart' as the direct object. بدین کار = بدین (به + این, 'about/over this') + کار 'affair/deed' — 'over this affair.' غمگین مدار is a negative imperative: غمگین (adjective 'sad') + مدار, an archaic imperative/negative-particle construction meaning 'do not make/become' — together 'do not make your heart sad.'
segments.s165-g597: کسی 'someone' begins an indefinite clause. کز (archaic form of که + ز) here introduces a relative/origin phrase 'who/that from (the) lineage of …' so کز نژاد سیاوش بود = 'who was of Siavash's line.' بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s165-g598: A string of predicates joined by و: خردمند 'wise', بیدار 'awake/alert', خامش 'silent/taciturn', followed by the copula بود 'was' which applies to the whole series. This is plain predication: adjectives listed, then the past verb.
segments.s165-g599: بگفت 'he said' (past tense) with the speaker noun ستاره‌شمر 'the astrologer' following the verb (inverted poetic order). مگرو is an archaic/conjunctive form meaning 'except/but' (مگر + او contracted), and ایچ is an emphatic particle 'nothing/not at all' (here functioning as a dismissive 'no/never/mind that').
segments.s165-g600: خردگیر is an adjective or verbal-adjective meaning 'prudent/wise' (predicate). و links the next phrase. کار سیاوش is a genitive phrase 'the deeds/affairs of Siavash' (ezāfe omitted in poetry). بسیچ is an adverb/quantifier 'very/many' placed after the noun for emphasis: 'Siavash's deeds are many.'
segments.s165-g233: Noun subject + prepositional phrase + verb: سپهبد (the spahbed) is the grammatical subject; ز گفتار او is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = از/’because of, from’) modifying the verb, and شاد شد is the past-tense predicate (شد = became). Word order is topical (subject first), and شد is the ordinary past/resultative of شدن meaning “became/was made.”
segments.s165-g234: Verbal noun clause + subject phrase + optative/periphrastic particle: سخن گفتن is a verbal noun phrase 'speaking/to speak', هر کسی is the indefinite/ distributive 'everyone/each person' acting as subject, and باد شد combines the poetic optative particle باد (a wish/‘may’) with شد to yield the sense 'came to be that / began to' — here functioning like 'they took to speaking' or 'everyone began to speak.'
segments.s165-g235: Prepositional oath phrase + indirect object + reporting verb: به جان و سر is an oath formula ('by (my) life and head') where the possessor is implicit; شاه کاووس is the addressee (to King Kavus), and گفت is the reporting verb 'said'. Note poetic word order places the oath before the addressee and the verb.
segments.s165-g236: Quotative/subordinate clause introduced by که: که introduces what was said. با من تو باشی uses با من ('with me') then the pronoun تو and the present-subjunctive/optative form باشی of بودن — here a jussive/wish meaning 'may you be/be thou with me.' هم‌آورد و جفت are predicate nouns in apposition describing تو ('my equal and pair/mate').
segments.s165-g237: Imperative command + locative phrase + predicate: هنر کن is an imperative construction where the verbal noun هنر ('skill') combines with the imperative verb کن ('do/show') to mean 'show your skill.' به پیش سواران is a locative phrase 'in front of the horsemen/riders' and پدید is a predicate/adverbial 'manifest/appear' — together: 'make your skill appear before the riders.'
segments.s165-g238: Demonstrative/verb compound + purpose clause: پدید بدان (punned across the clause boundary) is effectively 'make it manifest / let it be known' (the first element ب‑ is the verbal prefix and دان from دانستن 'to know' here implies 'cause to be known' or 'show it'); تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that' and نگویند is the negative jussive/subjunctive 'they do not say' (i.e. 'may they not say'); کاو is the referent (Kav/that one) and بد گزید is a predicate 'chose badly.'
segments.s165-g239: Verb (3rd plural) + object + prepositional phrase + subject (poetic inversion): کنند is a 3rd plural present/subjunctive form used here to express a future or prospective action 'they will/may do'; آفرین is the acclamation 'bravo/praise' as direct object, بر تو marks the target 'to you,' and مردان من ('my men') is the agent placed after the verb in inverted poetic order.
segments.s165-g240: Predicate adjective + auxiliary + nominal phrase: شگفته is a predicate adjective 'enraptured/bloomed' placed before شود (become), and روی خندان من is the nominal phrase 'my smiling face' that undergoes that change. شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن used here to mean 'will become' or 'will be made.' The order is poetic (predicate before verb before the affected noun).
segments.s165-g297: سیاووش is the subject (the legendary prince). غمی گشت is a nominal predicate with گشت used as a copular/auxiliary meaning “became” (noun غم + گشت = “became sorrowful”). ز introduces the cause (“because of”) and ایرانیان is the oblique noun expressing the source of his grief.
segments.s165-g298: سخن functions as the noun ‘speech/words’ and گفـت is the verb ‘said/spoke’ (poetic past). بر پهلوانی زبان is a prepositional phrase: بر = “about/on”, پهلوانی modifies زبان, and زبان here means “speech/utterance” — together “words about heroism” or “in a heroic tongue.” The clause order (noun before verb) is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s165-g299: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause (“for/that”). میدان is the subject ‘arena/field’. بازیست‌گر is a compressed poetic sequence equivalent to بازی است گر — “it is a game if…”: بازی است = “is play/game,” with گر = “if/when” linking to the following noun کارزار (“battle”). This is an archaic/poetic concatenation.
segments.s165-g300: بر این is the prepositional phrase “upon this / in this regard” (here written as br + ین). گردش و بخشش form a coordinated noun phrase “the turning and the granting/forgiveness,” and روزگار is the genitive noun governing both (i.e. “of fate/time”); together: “on the turnings and mercies of fate.”
segments.s165-g301: چو = “when.” میدان سرآید is a temporal subordinate clause: میدان (the contest/field) + سرآید (comes to an end; here سرآید = “cease/end”). بتابید is a past action meaning “they turned” (from تابیدن, here ‘turn/turn toward’) and روی = “face” (i.e. they turned their faces).
segments.s165-g302: بدیشان is the prepositional phrase ‘to them’ (ب + ایشان). سپارید is the past verb “they entrusted/handed (over).” یک‌بار qualifies the action (“once”), and گوی is the object “ball/orb” (here meaning the game-ball). Word order is somewhat poetic but conveys “they handed the ball to them once.”
segments.s165-g303: سواران is the plural subject “the riders.” عنانها (عنَان + ها) is the plural object “the reins.” کشیدند is the simple past plural “they pulled.” نرم here is an adverb/adjectival notion “gently/softly” which links into the following negative (نرم نکردند) — the poets place it before the negative verb for emphasis.
segments.s165-g304: نکردند is the negated past verb “they did not (do)” and combines with the preceding نرم to give “they did not ease/loosen/ride gently.” زان پس = “after that/thereupon.” گسی is an indefinite “a certain/some,” modifying اسپ (“steed/horse”), and گرم is an adjective “hot/ardent” used metaphorically for a spirited horse; the copular verb (‘became’) is omitted, a typical poetic ellipsis: “after that a certain horse became spirited.”
segments.s165-g65: ز ایران places the locus/cause before the verb in a poetic prepositional phrase ('ز' = from/about). دلش یاد کرد is literal word order ‘heart‑his remembered’ (subject = دلش, verb = یاد کرد) — a common Persian idiom meaning ‘he remembered (with his heart)’. و بسوخت is a simple past intransitive clause joined by و: بسوخت (ب‑ prefix + سوخت) here means emotional burning (was consumed by grief/longing).
segments.s165-g66: به کردار is an instrumental/comparative use of به + noun phrase ('in the manner/character of'). آتش رخش is a genitive grouping where آتش modifies رخش (Rakhsh the horse described as ‘fiery’). برفروخت is a past form meaning ‘was set alight / was inflamed’ (poetic use of بر‑فروختن); the implied subject continues the emotional state of the previous clause (he becomes further inflamed by the horse's fiery bearing).
segments.s165-g67: ز پیران marks the source/against whom he acts ('from the elders'). بپیچید is past of پیچیدن with the prefixed ب‑ (classical narrative form) meaning ‘turned aside/turned away from’. و پوشید روی strings a second past action: پوشید here means ‘covered/concealed’ and روی (face) is the object — together ‘he hid his face’.
segments.s165-g68: This is a straightforward nominal clause: سپهبد (spahbed) is the subject and بدید is the past tense ‘saw’. آن غم و درد اوی is the direct object phrase — آن points to ‘that’ sorrow, و joins ‘grief and pain’, and اوی is an archaic/poetic possessive meaning ‘his’ (referring back to the person who hid his face).
segments.s165-g69: بدانست is the simple past ‘he knew/perceived’. The following clause is an embedded clause marked by را after کاو (the casting/target of perception) and the interrogative چه + آمد meaning ‘what came / what happened’. بیاد at the end qualifies the mental locus: ‘into memory / to mind’. Together: ‘he knew what had come to his mind’.
segments.s165-g70: غمی گشت uses گشت (from گشتن) in the sense ‘became / arose’ — ‘a grief arose’. و links the second action: دندان به لب بر نهاد is a light-verb construction with نهاد (past of نهادن ‘to place’) plus the preverb بر, literally ‘placed the tooth to the lip’; idiomatically it means he clenched/pressed his teeth to his lip (a sign of restraint or pain).
segments.s165-g71: به + a name/title (قچقار باشی) marks destination or the addressee: ‘to Qachqar‑bashi’ (here قچقار باشی is a compound title/name). فرود آمدند is the past plural ‘they descended / alighted’ — a simple motion verb describing arrival at that place/person.
segments.s165-g72: نشستند is a past plural verb ‘they sat down’ (the lemma senses supplied in the list do not match the intended meaning here; context shows it is the verb ‘نشستن’). و links the next coordinated action; یکبار دم بر زدند is an idiomatic phrase: یکبار ‘at once/one time’, دم ‘breath/utterance’, بر زدند (زدند with بر‑) = they struck out/uttered — together ‘they immediately uttered a cry/blew a blast’.
segments.s165-g649: ز is the preposition 'from'. گستردنیها is a plural noun formed with the adjectival/nominal stem 'گستردنی' + plural -ها meaning 'things to be spread' (i.e. spreads or carpets). شتروار uses the suffix -وار ('-like, in the manner of') so شتروار = 'in camel-loads' or 'by camel-loads'. شست here is an archaic numeral placed after the counted unit (sixty); it quantifies the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s165-g650: ز = 'from'. زربفت is a compound meaning 'gold-brocade' (fabric woven with gold thread). پوشیدینها is a plural noun (the -ها plural) meaning 'garments / things worn'. سه دست is a numerical phrase 'three dast' where دست functions as 'a set' (here 'three sets' of garments); the number precedes the noun in normal order.
segments.s165-g651: همه 'all/entire' modifies پیکرش 'his body/figure' (possessive -ش). سرخ‌کرده is a participial adjective 'made red / reddened' describing the body. به here means 'by/with' (instrumental) so به زر = 'with gold' (i.e. ornamented in gold).
segments.s165-g652: برو بافته is a verbal/adjectival cluster meaning 'woven/embroidered upon' (بافته = 'woven'); برو is an old/poetic connective here and does not carry the common imperative sense 'go' (the lexical senses given do not match this poetic use). چند گونه = 'several kinds' (چند 'several', گونه 'kinds'). گهر = 'gem/jewel' and is the head noun modified by the preceding phrase.
segments.s165-g653: ز = 'from/of'. و = coordinating 'and'. زرین = adjective 'golden'. شتربار is a compound meaning 'camel-load' (goods carried by camels); here it is followed by the numeral سی 'thirty' which quantifies the camel-loads.
segments.s165-g654: طبقها (تابق + plural -ها) means 'plates/trays/dishes' and is the item being counted. و = 'and'. از = 'from/of' introducing the next category. جامه 'garment/robe' and پارسی 'Persian' modifies it ('Persian garments'). Word order is noun then adjective here (common in poetry).
segments.s165-g655: یکی = 'one/a certain' introduces the count. تخت زرین = 'golden throne' (noun + adjective). و = 'and'. کرسی چهار = 'four seats' where the numeral follows the noun (poetic inversion) but together mean 'and four chairs/seats'.
segments.s165-g656: سه نعلین = 'three sandals' (number + noun). زرین = 'golden' qualifying the sandals. زبرجد نگار is a compound where زبرجد 'peridot' names the gemstone and نگار is a descriptive/ornamental word indicating 'set with / ornamented by' (poetic usage). The whole phrase means 'three golden sandals set with peridot.'
segments.s165-g273: This clause is an older/poetic word order: subject (سیاوش) + locative/relational adverb (بران) + noun (گوی) + preposition (بر) + past verb (داد) + object noun (بوس). Here داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' used with the noun بوس ('a kiss') to mean 'kissed' (literally 'gave a kiss'). بران (literally 'on that' / 'upon that') and بر ('on') together locate the action onto the ball (i.e. 'upon that ball he gave a kiss').
segments.s165-g274: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth' placed before its following verbal-nominal phrase. خروشیدن is an infinitive/masdar-like form 'the roaring' used as a noun ('the outcry/roar'), followed by the instruments نای و کوس ('flute and drum') as the thing that rose. The clause therefore means 'the roar/sounding of flute and drum arose.'
segments.s165-g275: Subject (سیاوش) + prepositional phrase (به اسپی دگر = 'onto/into another horse') + past verb برنشست ('mounted/sat upon'). برنشست is a prefixed past verb meaning 'took a seat on / mounted' and governs the prepositional phrase indicating what he mounted.
segments.s165-g276: بیانداخت is a past verb 'threw/cast' (here 'tossed'), followed by the demonstrative آن 'that' and the noun phrase گویِ خسرو 'the king's ball' (خسرو here as common noun 'king/sovereign' in genitive position). The phrase به دست ('to hand/into the hand') shows the recipient; the construction means 'he flung that ball into the king's hand.'
segments.s165-g277: ازان پس is a formulaic temporal phrase 'after that / thereupon.' The sequence به چوگان برو کار کرد contains به + چوگان ('to/at the mallet' — here 'chougan' as the polo-mallet) and برو (literally 'went') used adverbially with کار کرد ('did work/acted') to mean 'he went on to work at the mallet' or more loosely 'he took up the mallet and played.' کار کرد is the light-verb combination 'did work / performed the action.'
segments.s165-g278: چنان شد که is a common rhetorical clause 'it came to be such that / so it happened that' introducing a subordinate clause. The subordinate clause با ماه دیدار کرد uses با ('with') + ماه ('the moon') + the noun دیدار ('a meeting') plus the light verb کرد ('did') to mean 'he met (had an encounter) with the moon.' This is literal here but likely metaphorical in context.
segments.s165-g279: This is a sensor-result clause: ز چوگان او ('from his mallet') provides the source, followed by the subject گوی ('the ball') and the copular/auxiliary شد ('became') plus the predicate adjective ناپدید ('vanished'). Word order reflects older/poetic sequencing: prepositional source + possessor phrase + subject + predicate.
segments.s165-g280: تو گفتی is a past reporting parenthesis 'you said' (introducing reported speech). The reported clause سپهرش همی برکشید has سپهر + possessive clitic -ش ('his/its sky' or generically 'the heavens'), the archaic/poetic particle همی (here emphasizing or progressive), and the past verb برکشید 'lifted/raised.' Grammatically this reads 'you said (that) his/its heaven raised it' or more idiomatically 'you said the heavens had raised it.'
segments.s165-g89: خنیده is a past participle (literally 'sung') used adjectivally/passively to mean 'celebrated' or 'sung of'. به گیتی is an adverbial phrase indicating scope/place ('in/throughout the world'); the second به before مهر marks the quality or cause ('for/through love'). مهر و وفا are coordinated nouns 'love and fidelity' describing the characteristic for which he is celebrated.
segments.s165-g90: ز introduces separation or removal ('from'); آهرمنی is an adjective/noun referring to Ahrimanic/demonic evil. دور و دور is a poetic reduplication intensifying distance ('far, far'); the second phrase از جفا repeats the 'from' relation with از to indicate 'far from cruelty/injustice'. The structure lists things he is distant from.
segments.s165-g91: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a small temporal/modal particle ('now/at this point' in older Persian) modifying the condition. با من تو پیمان کنی is a standard Persian order where the prepositional phrase با من ('with me') precedes the subject pronoun تو ('you', emphatic here) and the verb کنی ('you make' in the present/subjunctive). کنی is subjunctive/optative after the conditional.
segments.s165-g92: شناسم is a first-person clause meaning 'I shall/will know' or 'I will recognize' (present/future sense). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. پیمان من is a possessive construction ('my covenant') with the possessor after the noun (typical Persian order), and مشکنی is an adjectival predicate meaning 'musk-scented' describing that covenant.
segments.s165-g93: گر begins a conditional 'if'. از + verbal noun بودن ('from/that of being' → 'to be') forms a gerundial clause meaning 'to be'. ایدر is locative 'here'. مرا (split in the segmentation as م + را) is the oblique 'for me'/'to me' and نیکویست is a nominal/adjectival predicate 'is good' (archaic -ست ending); together: 'if my being here is good for me' i.e. 'if it is right that I remain here'.
segments.s165-g94: برین is 'بر این' ('upon this' / 'in view of this'); کرده is the perfect participle 'having done' (literally 'having made/caused this'). خود is reflexive/emphatic 'oneself' or 'personally'. نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / it is not proper to' and گریست is the infinitive/masdar-verb 'to weep'—together 'one should not weep' or 'it is not proper to weep.'
segments.s165-g95: وگر 'and if' links the alternative condition. نیست negates existence/presence ('is not' / 'there is no'). فرمای here functions as a verb meaning 'grant/allow' (imperative/optative nuance in older Persian: 'do grant' / 'permit'); together the sequence means 'and if you do not grant permission'. تا introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that', and بگذرم is first-person singular subjunctive/purpose 'that I may pass'.
segments.s165-g96: نمایی is a verb form meaning 'show (me)' or 'may you show' (optative/subjunctive); ره is the noun 'way/road'; کشوری qualifies it 'of a country' (genitive/adjective use 'country‑(related)'); دیگرم (دیگر + م) yields 'another (for me)' / 'to another' — overall 'show me the road to another country.' The word order places the verb before its object for poetic effect.
segments.s165-g1185: چو/چنان جلوهٔ قیدی: 'بران' is an adverbial/locative particle meaning 'thereupon' or 'from that' and links this phrase to what came before. 'خرمی' is a noun meaning 'delight/mirth' that is the content of that adverbial phrase. 'بر' here is the locative preposition 'on/upon' and 'یکی خارستان' is a noun phrase 'a certain thorny thicket' (یکی = 'a certain'). Word order is poetic (adverbial + noun + locative phrase) but syntactically it means 'thereupon, from that delight, on a certain thorn-thicket...'.
segments.s165-g1186: 'همی' here functions as a discourse/temporal marker with a progressive/continuous flavour ('then/so' or 'still'), introducing the subsequent action. 'بوم' is 'dwelling, abode' (the thing set up). The verb 'سازد' is the main verb 'to build/make' and 'بر' before it again marks the place ('upon' it). The second coordinated noun 'شارستان' ('town, township') is joined by و and is governed by the same verb: literally 'he sets up a dwelling and builds upon it a town.' The construction omits an explicit pronoun or object marker due to poetic compression.
segments.s165-g1187: 'فرنگیس را' uses را to mark Ferangis as the beneficiary/definite person affected by the action (classical Persian often uses را with a beneficiary). 'کاخهای بلند' is the noun phrase 'lofty/tall palaces' (کاخهای is plural). The phrase as a whole is the object/beneficiary phrase of the following verb 'برآورد'.
segments.s165-g1188: 'برآورد' is the verbal form 'he raised/caused to be brought up' (here: 'he erected/raised'). The conjunction 'و' links the next clause. 'دارد' is a present-tense verb 'he holds/has' and takes the adjective 'ارجمند' as its predicative complement meaning 'esteemed/honored'; 'همی' here gives a continuous or emphatic sense ('keeps/continually'). So syntactically: 'he raised palaces ... and keeps her always esteemed.'
segments.s165-g1189: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'بینی' is 2nd-person singular present 'you see'. The prepositional phrase 'به خوبی' means 'of/regarding the goodness' (خوبی = 'goodness'), and 'فراوان' is an adverb 'plentifully/abundantly' modifying the verb. 'بگوی' here is an exhortation 'speak/say' (imperative/jussive) addressed to the listener; in the provided senses list no verbal sense is given, so the lexical sense index below is -1. The order places the object/phrase before the verb (poetic order) but the relation is 'When you see, say much about her goodness.'
segments.s165-g1190: 'به چشم بزرگی' is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with the eye of greatness' (بزرگی here carries the sense of magnanimity/grandeur). 'نگه کن' is the imperative 'keep/look' formed by noun/verb + کن (do), i.e. 'look/keep your gaze.' The final 'به روی' ('toward the face') indicates direction—i.e. 'look toward her face.' Together: 'Look upon her with a magnanimous eye toward her face.'
segments.s165-g1191: 'چو' again is 'when'. 'نخچیر' means 'game, prey' (the subject whose presence is noted). 'می‌باشد' is the present existential 'there is/is present' (classical present form). The coordinated nouns 'دشت و کوه' specify places 'plain and mountain' where the game is found. The clause thus reads, 'When game is present, and (in) plain and mountain...'.
segments.s165-g1192: 'نشینند' is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive 'they sit/dwell' (here 'they take their seats / assemble'). 'پیشت' means 'before you, in your presence'. The preposition 'ز' is the poetic form of 'from', so 'ز ایران' = 'from Iran'. 'گروه' is the subject 'group/band'. Together: 'groups from Iran sit before you.' The verb-subject agreement and word order reflect classical poetic phrasing.
segments.s165-g49: This is an address to 'you' (تو) followed by the adjectival phrase بی‌کام دل 'heart unsatisfied' (noun دل modified by بی‌کام). هیچ دم is an emphatic negative temporal phrase 'not even for a moment / never', and بر مزن is an imperative/negative imperative construction 'do not strike/lay upon' where بر is the prepositional particle and مزن is the negative imperative of زدن.
segments.s165-g50: ترا here is the object form (ت + را) emphasizing the person addressed ('you' as object/topic). بنده باشد is an optative/hortative sense ('may there be servants' / 'you shall have servants') with باشد used in a wish/statement-of-existence sense. همی is an archaic particle adding continuity/emphasis, and مرد و زن lists the two classes 'men and women' as the servants.
segments.s165-g51: مراگر (a fused conditional 'مرا گر') = 'if you take/accept me' (object مرا placed before the verb, a common poetic order). پذیری is the (subjunctive/optative or present) form 'you accept'. تو is the explicit pronoun. با + پیر سر is a prepositional phrase 'with the elder' (پیر 'old/elder', سر here 'head' used with پیر as 'old-headed/elder' or simply 'the elder').
segments.s165-g52: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' (ز = 'from/for' used with بهر 'for'). پرستش is the object 'worship'. ببندم کمر is a first‑person present/subjunctive construction 'I will gird my loins' (ب‑ prefix + بندن root forming a volitional/performative action), literally 'I bind my waist' i.e. prepare/devote myself.
segments.s165-g53: برفتند is the simple past 'they went/they departed'. هر دو is the idiom 'both (of them)' with هر used distributively before دو. به شادی is a circumstantial phrase 'in/with joy', and به هم (here) means 'to one another/together' indicating mutual action.
segments.s165-g54: سخن یاد کردند is a verb phrase meaning 'they spoke/they remembered/took up speech' (سخن 'speech/topic' + یاد کردن 'to recall/mention'), with کردند as the past plural auxiliary 'they did/made'. بر بیش و کم uses بر as 'about/on' and بیش و کم 'more and less' = 'greater and lesser matters' i.e. 'things great and small'.
segments.s165-g55: همه ره here means 'all along the road' or 'everyone on the way' (همه 'all/everyone' + ره 'way, road'). ز آوای is 'from the sound' (ز = 'from' used poetically; آوای = 'the sound/melody of'), followed by the instruments چنگ و رباب (harp and rebab).
segments.s165-g56: همی is an archaic/poetic particle giving immediacy/emphasis. خفته is a past participle used as a substantive 'the sleeper(s) / those asleep' and را marks them as the topic/object. سر برآمد is a compound meaning 'the head rose' (i.e. they lifted their heads), where برآمد is past 'rose/issued forth'. ز خواب 'from sleep' indicates the source/condition they rose out of.
segments.s165-g249: دگر here means 'another/other' and comes before the proper name اندریمان to introduce a new person. سوار دلیر is an appositive noun phrase 'a brave rider/horseman' describing اندریمان; Persian poetry often places modifiers around names in this compressed way.
segments.s165-g250: چو is the simile particle 'like/as' introducing a comparison with ارجاسپ. اسپ is 'horse/steed' and افگن is an adjectival/poetic word 'radiant/shining'; نره شیر is an apposition meaning 'male lion' (i.e. lion-like male). The clause omits a copula ('was') as is normal in Persian poetry, so the sequence describes his horse as being, like Arjasp's, a shining/ferocious male-lion (metaphorically).
segments.s165-g251: سیاوش (Siavash) is the subject and چنین گفت is the reporting clause 'thus said/said thus.' کای نامجوی is a vocative/title (Kay Namjuy) addressed by the speaker; in classical Persian epic the title کای precedes royal names.
segments.s165-g252: ازیشان is 'از ایشان' = 'of/from them/among them'. که is the relative pronoun 'who/that'. یارد شدن is the verb phrase 'to become a yār' (yār = companion/helper), where شدن = 'become'. پیش‌گوی ('soothsayer') is the predicate role being asked about: literally 'which of them that will become (my) companion is the soothsayer?' — word order and omission of possessive pronouns are typical in poetic compression.
segments.s165-g253: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone'. یار here means 'companions/comrades'. شاهند is a compressed poetic form meaning 'of the shah / belonging to the king' (i.e. 'the king's'); the clause as a whole reads 'They are all the king's companions.' The copula/possessive relationship is compacted in epic style.
segments.s165-g254: نگهبان is 'guardian/keeper', and چوگان refers to the polo game or polo-mallet; یکتا 'unique/sole' modifies نگهبان to stress exclusivity. منم ('I am') is emphatic and repeated to assert that the speaker alone holds that office. The whole phrase therefore means 'I alone am the sole keeper of the polo.'
segments.s165-g255: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning something like 'now/at this time' (common in epic diction). فرمان دهد شهریار is an inversion typical of poetry: 'if the king gives the command' (فرمان = command, دهد = 'gives', شهریار = 'the king').
segments.s165-g256: بیارم is the poetically-compressed form of بیاورم 'I will bring' (subjunctive/volitional used in the apodosis of a conditional). به میدان = 'to the field/arena'. ز ایران سوار = 'riders from Iran' or 'mounted men of Iran' (ز = from; سوار = mounted warrior). The direct object ('them') is omitted but understood.
segments.s165-g145: کنون is a temporal adverb placed clause‑initially: “now.” شهر توران is a noun phrase meaning “the city/people of Turan” (noun followed by proper name as genitive). ترا را is the 2nd‑person object clitic split here as ت + را and marks the person to whom servitude is directed; بنده‌اند is a predicate (بنده + ‑اند) in the 3rd person plural present meaning “they are servants / they are in servitude.” Word order is topical (time → topic → indirect object clitic → predicate).
segments.s165-g146: همه دل is an indefinite expression “every/all heart(s)” (همه quantifying دل). به مهر تو is a prepositional phrase “with/for the love of you” (مهر = affection, تو = you). آگنده‌اند is a present‑state predicate (آگنده + ‑اند) meaning “are filled (with).” The clause literally reads “all hearts are filled with your love.”
segments.s165-g147: مرا here functions as the experiencer/topic (“to me” / “for me,” roughly equivalent to “I have” in this construction). چیز is the item/thing being offered. با جان = “with (my) life”; همی is an archaic/emphatic present particle (here giving a continuous or earnest tone); پیش تست literally “is before you” — پیش “in the presence of/before” + تست, an archaic 2nd‑person copula (“thou art” / “is before you”) used to place the thing before the addressee. The whole segment expresses offering/presenting something to you together with the speaker’s life.
segments.s165-g148: سپهبد is the subject (“the spahbed / commander”). به جان و به تن خویش is a pair of prepositional phrases literally “to/for (his) soul and to/for (his) body” where خویش + ش (here split as خوی + ش) is the reflexive/possessive “his own.” تست again functions as an archaic copula meaning roughly “is yours” / “belongs to you” (i.e. ‘is devoted to you, body and soul’). The sequence stresses total devotion: the commander belongs to you in soul and body.
segments.s165-g149: سیاوش is the subject. برو آفرین here is an idiomatic/exclamatory phrase meaning vigorous praise (آفرین = acclamation/bravo, with برو functioning as part of the formula). کرد is the past tense 3rd‑person singular “did/uttered,” and سخت is an adverb “strongly/vehemently” modifying کرد. The clause says that Siavash strongly uttered praise.
segments.s165-g150: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause: “for/that.” از گوهر تو is a genitive expression “from your essence/very being” (از = from, گوهر = essence), تو = your. مگر here is a conjectural/rhetorical particle (used like “perhaps/indeed/was it not that”), and داد is the past‑tense verb “gave.” بخت is the subject “fortune/fate.” Word order is slightly inverted (literal: “that from your essence gave fortune”), a common poetic inversion meaning “it seemed as if fortune had been given out of your essence.”
segments.s165-g151: سپاس is a noun/exclamation “thanks” or “praise.” از خدای is the prepositional phrase “to/from God” (here idiomatically “thanks to God”). جهان‌آفرین is an appositive epithet “creator of the world” qualifying خدای. The sequence is a short thanksgiving formula.
segments.s165-g152: کزویست is a fused/poetic form meaning “that is from him / from whom (it is).” It introduces the list of things that proceed from God: آرام (calmness), و پرخاش (and reproach/aggression), و کین (and vengeance/hatred). The three nouns are coordinated by و; the construction asserts that calm, reproach, and enmity are from God’s will/dispensation.
segments.s165-g433: پس here is a spatial/temporal 'after/behind' placed before the noun phrase; پرده is the head noun 'curtain/screen' and شهریار جهان is a genitive apposition 'the sovereign (ruler) of the world' modifying پرده — literally 'behind the curtain of the world's sovereign.' Word order is classical: locative/particle + head noun + genitive modifier.
segments.s165-g434: This clause says 'there are three moons.' سه (three) precedes the noun phrase; ماهست is an archaic/poetic contraction of 'ماه است' (literally 'is moon' or 'are moons' in context) functioning as the existential/predicative verb; with numbers classical Persian often uses singular-form predicates. با زیور اندر نهان is a prepositional/adverbial phrase: با introduces accompaniment 'adorned with', اندر 'within/in', and نهان 'hidden' describes their state.
segments.s165-g435: اگر introduces a counterfactual conditional. ماه را (definite object marked by را) is the object 'the moon' but here refers back to the beautiful 'moons' just mentioned (an epithet for beauties). دیده بودی is past perfect/pluperfect ('had seen') formed with the past participle دیده plus the past-tense auxiliary بودی; سیاه is a predicate adjective left in elliptical form (i.e. '(had been) black').
segments.s165-g436: نه برداشتی is the negative of a past/factual inference verb (you would not have taken/inferred). از ایشان 'from them' marks the source/standard of comparison, and چشم ماه 'the moon's eye' is the phrase being judged. The clause literally says 'you would not have taken (interpreted) the moon's eye from them' — i.e. you would not have judged the moon (or its radiance) to be dark by comparison with them.
segments.s165-g437: سه (three) is the numeral; اندر شبستان is a locative phrase 'in the inner chamber/bedchamber' (اندر = in/within). گرسیوزاند is a (poetic/older) verb form meaning 'set on fire' or 'caused to burn' (causative sense) describing what those three did — classical Persian often uses compact verb-forms like this in narrative verse.
segments.s165-g438: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/that.' از مام وز باب is an expression meaning 'from mother and father' — here مام = mother and باب is used as 'father' in an older register; وز between them is an archaic connective (equivalent to و 'and'). با پروزاند: با 'with/by' and پروزاند is a causative verb 'set (it) flying' or 'sent flying/exalted.' The whole clause gives the consequence: by mother and father (i.e. even the parents) they were sent away/exalted/removed.
segments.s165-g439: نبیره فریدون is a nominative noun phrase 'scion/offspring of Fereydun' (نبیره = descendant/heir). The following و فرزند شاه is an additional appositive 'and son/descendant of the king.' Classical Persian often stacks kinship/descent titles like this for emphasis.
segments.s165-g440: که introduces a relative clause 'who.' هم ... هم ... is the correlative pair 'both ... and ...' modifying what they possess: جاه 'rank/status' and تاج 'crown' (with گاه added as 'standing/occasion/place' — here 'گاه' functions as 'position/standing'). دارند is the present plural verb 'they have/possess.' The clause therefore says they possess both honor/status and sovereignty/seat.
segments.s165-g353: This clause has two coordinated past verbs (خوردند, گشتند) with their shared subjects implied (they). چند is a quantifier modifying the eating/drinking (i.e. 'some/a few'), and شاد is a predicative adjective after گشتند ('became happy'). The initial می‌ی is archaic/uncertain in form here (poetic contraction); it precedes the quantifier and verb and should be read as an adverbial/lexical relic rather than a full independent verb.
segments.s165-g354: به نام سیاووش is a prepositional phrase ('to/for the name of Siyâvush') functioning as the object of the light‑verb construction یاد کردن. کردند is the past 3rd plural of کردن; یاد (a noun) combines with کردن to mean 'remember' (literally 'made remembrance'). Word order here is slightly inverted for poetic effect (prepositional phrase fronted before the verb).
segments.s165-g355: بخوان is an imperative/performative verb ('sing' or 'recite') used here as a stage direction or address to the bard. بر یکی خلعت is the phrase 'upon one, a robe (of honour)' where بر marks the recipient/target (upon/onto) and یکی = 'one/a certain person'. آراست is the past 3rd‑person singular 'adorned', and شاه is the subject placed after the verb (verb–subject order is common in poetic prose).
segments.s165-g356: This is an enumerative prepositional phrase introduced by از ('from/of') listing items associated with the gift: اسپ (horse) and other valuables. The repeated conjunctions (و) and the poetic variant ز (for از) join the list items; the sequence ends with تخت و کلاه (throne and hat/crown). The whole phrase modifies or specifies what the king bestowed or what accompanied the gift.
segments.s165-g357: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same' modifying the noun phrase دست زر (lit. 'gold hand' = 'golden hand' or 'hand of gold' — a poetic epithet). جامه is the object 'garment/robe'. نابرید is a past verbal/adjectival form attached to the clause; in classical/poetic uses its precise nuance can be idiomatic (here best read as part of the predicate describing what the hand did to the garment). Word order places modifier (همان) before the noun phrase and the predicate after it.
segments.s165-g358: که introduces a relative clause modifying جامه ('which'). اندر جهان = 'in the world'; پیش ازان ('before that') sets the time reference. کس ندید is a negated past statement 'no one saw' (کس with the negated verb ندید yields 'no one had seen'). The clause as a whole: 'which no one in the world had seen before.'
segments.s165-g359: This phrase is the start of an enumeration introduced by the poetic preposition ز (= از) listing monetary items: دینار (dinar) and درم/درم (dirham) appear here; the intervening forms (وز, بدرهای) are part of the poetic enumeration and connect the coin terms. The phrase functions to enumerate the material wealth (coins) that accompanied the gift.
segments.s165-g360: A continuation of the list, again introduced by ز ('of/with'): یاقوت (ruby) and پیروزه (turquoise) are named, and the clause closes with بیش و کم ('more and less' = varying quantities). The conjunction و simply links the items; بیش و کم is an idiomatic pair meaning 'in greater or lesser measure'.
segments.s165-g17: This short noun phrase names the animals presented. بیاراسته is an adjectival/participial qualifier placed before the counted phrase (here glossed as 'unadorned' or 'simply arranged'); چار (numeral) precedes پیل (noun) as the usual number + noun sequence; سپید follows پیل as a postpositive adjective 'white' (poetic word order). The whole phrase functions as the direct object/topic of the following action.
segments.s165-g18: سپه را is marked with the object marker را; here it behaves as the (indirect) recipient of the news. همه qualifies سپه as 'the whole/entire army.' داد is the simple past 'gave' and یکسر is an adverb 'at once/entirely' modifying داد. نوید is the direct object 'good news' (the thing that was given); Persian frequently omits a copula and allows this order: Recipient (سپه را همه) + verb (داد) + thing given (نوید).
segments.s165-g19: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one / a certain (man)'. برنهاده is a past participle / perfect meaning 'set down' or 'placed'. ز + پیروزه is a poetic material/compositional phrase 'of/from turquoise' (i.e. 'turquoise‑made' or 'turquoise‑coloured'), and تخت is the head noun 'throne.' Word order is poetic: material/quality follows the preposition ز and precedes the noun it modifies.
segments.s165-g20: درفشنده is a noun 'standard‑bearer' placed before the proper name in apposition; مهدی is the bearer’s name. The simile بسان + noun ('like a...') follows, so بسان درخت means 'like a tree.' There is no copula — this is a verbless equational line typical of epic poetry: 'Mahdi the standard‑bearer (stood) like a tree.'
segments.s165-g21: سرش is 'his head/top' with the enclitic possessive ش. ماه زرین is a predicate nominal 'a golden moon' (a metaphor); Persian omits the copula here. و links the next predicate: بومش ('his bōm' = his banner's field/canvas) + بنفش 'purple' is another predicate nominal describing the banner's colouring.
segments.s165-g22: به زر بافته is a participial phrase 'woven with gold' where به +past participle marks the instrument/material of weaving. پرنیایی is a descriptive/attributive name/adjective (a poetic name form) modifying درفش 'banner/standard.' The phrase therefore reads 'the Pernia (or Pernia‑made) banner, woven with gold.'
segments.s165-g23: ابا is an archaic/obscure form (lexically related to reluctance or refusal) used here as a verbal root; in this line it functions as a finite verb meaning 'he refused' or 'he shrank from' and takes تخت زرین 'the golden throne' as its object. سه پیل دگر is a noun phrase 'three other elephants' (سه numeral + noun + دگر 'other'). The clause is elliptical / compressed, typical of epic diction.
segments.s165-g24: صد از introduces a partitive 'a hundred of...' (صد = 'a hundred', از = 'of/from') and ماه‌رویان is a plural descriptive 'moon‑faced' i.e. beautiful‑faced people. زرین کمر is a compound attributive 'gold‑belted' (زرین modifies کمر 'waist/girdle'), describing how these people were attired. The whole phrase is therefore 'a hundred moon‑faced, gold‑belted (men)' and continues the catalogue begun earlier.
segments.s165-g329: This phrase sets the scene/topic: 'به توران و ایران' = 'in/among Turan and Iran' (به here indicating realm/area). 'کس' is the subject meaning 'no one' in the negative clause to follow. 'این را' is the definite object marked by را. 'به چنگ' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'into the grasp / by (the) grip' (به here functioning as 'by' + چنگ 'grasp, claw' used figuratively). Word order places the locale first, then the (negative) subject, then the marked object and the idiom 'به چنگ.'
segments.s165-g330: This is the predicate: 'نیارد گرفتن به هنگام جنگ'. 'گرفتن' is the verb 'to take/seize' and here forms the core action. 'به هنگام جنگ' = 'at the time of battle/war' (هنگام = 'at the time of'). The form 'نیارد' is an archaic/poetic negative/future-type form (here supplying the negation 'will not / cannot'); its exact morphological identity is archaic and not directly listed in the provided senses, so it is treated as irregular/uncertain in the senses map.
segments.s165-g331: 'بر و یال و کتف سیاوش' is a nominal list of body-parts with the genitive/name after them: 'بر' (on/forehead), 'یال' (mane), 'کتف' (shoulder) followed by the possessor 'سیاوش' (Siyâvash). 'جزین' is a contracted poetic form of 'جز این' = 'except this' or 'nothing but this', placed after the list for emphasis.
segments.s165-g332: This clause completes the thought. 'نخواهد' is the future/auxiliary verb (here with negation prefix) meaning 'will not' or 'would not' in older/poetic usage. 'کمان' is the noun 'bow' and is topicalized before the verb. 'نیز' = 'also'. 'بر دشت کین' is a locative phrase 'on/upon the plain of vengeance' (بر = on, دشت = plain, کین = vengeance/vengeance-field). The verb placement after the topical object is typical older word order.
segments.s165-g333: 'نشانی نهادند' is a past plural verb phrase: 'they placed/set a sign/mark'. 'بر اسپریس' = 'upon Aspres' (a proper name). The verb 'نهادند' is the simple past plural of 'نهادن' (to place), and the object ('نشانی') precedes the verb in the usual Persian order.
segments.s165-g334: This is a negative past statement about Siyâvash: 'سیاوش نکرد ایچ با کس مکیس'. 'نکرد' = 'did not do' (past negative). 'ایچ' = 'nothing / not at all' (an emphatic negative). 'با کس مکیس' = 'with the man Makis' — here 'کس' functions as 'person/man' and is followed by the proper name 'مکیس' (appositive or specifying who the 'person' is). The whole clause means 'Siyâvash did nothing at all with Makis.'
segments.s165-g335: 'نشست از بر' is an older compound meaning 'alighted from / dismounted from (the back of)'. 'بادپایی' is an epithet meaning 'the swift-footed one' (likely a steed). 'چو دیو' is a simile 'like a demon'. The verb 'نشست' is past tense 'he sat / alit'.
segments.s165-g336: 'برافشارد ران' — 'برافشارد' (scattered/struck/threw) with 'ران' (thigh / haunch) yields the image of striking out or tossing the haunches (often of a horse). 'و' = 'and'. 'برآمد غریو' = 'a cry/roar arose' (برآمد = arose/issued forth, غریو = shout/clamor). The clause sequence is simple past actions producing a vivid battle image.
segments.s165-g761: This is a causal/prepositional phrase introduced by the poetic preposition ز (’because of, from’) followed by بس ‘so much’ that quantifies the two coordinated nouns رامش و ناله کرنای (‘tranquility and the trumpet’s lament’). کرنای is in the construct/genitive relation to ناله (the trumpet’s lament) and the whole phrase supplies the reason for what follows.
segments.s165-g762: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past (you said), with the pronoun تو preceding the verb گفتی in normal Persian word order; what follows is direct reported content. بجنبد is a 3rd-person present/habitual form (here future/gnomic sense) meaning ‘will/does move’ and is accompanied by the archaic progressive/emphatic particle همی. دل is the grammatical subject/affected theme and ز جای is a prepositional phrase ‘from its place’ marking the origin of the movement.
segments.s165-g763: بجایی رسیدند is a verb phrase meaning ‘they reached/arrived at a timely/appropriate place’ (بجایی here = ‘timely/appropriate’). The following کاباد بود is a simple nominal clause with the proper name کاباد as subject and بود the past copula ‘was,’ so the line states that at that place there was (or it was) Kabâd.
segments.s165-g764: یکی is an indefinite numeral ‘a certain/one’ introducing the noun phrase. خوب فرخنده are two adjectival modifiers (good, auspicious) placed before the head noun بنیاد ‘foundation,’ and بود is the past copula ‘was,’ so the whole segment asserts that ‘there was a certain good, auspicious foundation.’
segments.s165-g765: به introduces orientation; به یک روی دریا و یک روی کوه literally ‘with one face toward the sea and one face toward the mountain.’ روی functions as ‘side/face’ and each noun (دریا, کوه) is the object of that orientation. The phrase describes the placement/orientation of whatever follows.
segments.s165-g766: This is a compact verbal clause in which گروه ‘troop/band’ is the substantive and گشته is a perfect/participial form ‘having gone/being turned’ that modifies or completes it. بر (preposition) with برو (verb root ‘go’) forms a poetic/compound sense of advancing or moving upon, and ز نخچیر indicates the object/purpose ‘toward the spoil/prey.’ The overall sense: a band had gone forth toward the booty.
segments.s165-g767: This is a simple coordinated noun phrase: درختان بسیار ‘many trees’ (بسیار as a quantifier) conjoined with و and followed by آب روان ‘running/flowing water’ (روان as an adjective/epithet of آب). The phrase sets a scene of abundant trees and flowing water.
segments.s165-g768: همی شد is a past/gnomic construction meaning ‘became’ (همی adds habitual/emphatic colouring). دل is the subject ‘the heart’; سالخورده describes its prior state ‘timeworn/aged,’ and جوان is the resultant predicate ‘young.’ Grammatically, the adjective سالخورده gives the initial condition and شد + جوان expresses the change of state: the aged heart became young.
segments.s165-g769: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). به پیران is a prepositional/indirect-object phrase meaning 'to Pîrân' or 'to the elders'. سخن is the object 'speech' placed before the verb for poetic word order. برگشاد is a past/poetic verb 'opened' or 'began' (برگشودن) used here to mean 'spoke/started speaking'.
segments.s165-g770: که introduces the reported content ('that'). اینت is an archaic/dialectal form of 'این' (this) serving as the clause's topic/subject. بر و بوم is a conventional compound meaning 'land and home/territory'. فرخ نهاد here is a poetic nominal phrase: فرخ ('fortunate') modifies نهاد used as 'disposition/nature' so the clause asserts that the land is 'fortunate-natured' (i.e. blessed/favorable).
segments.s165-g771: بسازم is first‑person present/subjunctive used as a future-like intention 'I will build' (ساختن). من is the explicit subject pronoun for emphasis. ایدر is an archaic form of 'here'. یکی خوب جای is a noun phrase 'one/a certain good place' (numeric/determiner + adjective + noun); adjective follows the numeral in this order.
segments.s165-g772: که introduces the purpose/relative clause 'that'. باشد is the present subjunctive/coprial form 'may be / be' expressing the intended state. به شادی is a prepositional phrase 'for/to (my) joy'. م and را are a split of the clitic 'مرا' (object 'me') where را is the definite/object marker; the segmentation given treats م irregularly — here it functions as the object pronoun 'me' attached to رهنمای. رهنمای is a noun 'guide' functioning as the predicate of the clause ('that it be a guide to me').
segments.s165-g773: برآرم is a 1st‑person verb 'I will raise/erect' (برآوردن/برآرم). یکی شارستان فراخ is the object phrase 'a certain/well‑one city, spacious' with the adjective فراخ 'wide/spacious' placed after the noun (poetic order).
segments.s165-g774: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'plentiful(ly)' placed before the verb for emphasis. کنم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن used here as 'I will make/do'. اندرو is the archaic compound for 'در آن' meaning 'within it' or 'inside'. باغ و کاخ is a coordinated object: 'gardens and palaces.'
segments.s165-g775: نشستن‌گهی is a compound noun 'a place/seat for sitting'. برفرازم is a fused locative/possessive form meaning 'on high above me / lofty above me' (برفراز + ـم). به ماه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/by the moon' used as a simile or locative image (the moon as a mark of beauty/loftiness).
segments.s165-g776: چنان چون is a duplicative simile 'such as / just as'. بود is the past copula 'was' used in the sense 'such as was/that would be'. در خور is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'befitting / worthy of' (در + خور), followed by تاج و گاه 'crown and throne/time' as the things of which it is worthy. Grammatically, the clause states a comparison and then asserts that the seat befits crown and throne.
segments.s165-g217: همی is a literary adverb marking habitual/continuous action (’continually/always’) placed before the rest of the clause; از تو is a prepositional phrase ‘from you’ indicating source; شاهان هنر is the subject plus its object/compliment (شاهان = ‘kings’ as subject, هنر = the thing sought, ‘skill’), and جویند is the 3rd-person plural present tense of جستن ‘to seek’ (standard SOV flexibility in poetry: the adverb and prepositional phrase are fronted).
segments.s165-g218: که introduces a subordinate/result/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'); یابد is 3rd‑person singular present of یافتن ‘to find/attain’ (here ‘may find’/‘find’); به + هرکار is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘in/at every undertaking’ (به marking the domain); بر تو گذر is a nominal phrase where گذر ‘passage/way’ is governed by بر meaning ‘toward/to (you)’ — together 'find a passage to you'.
segments.s165-g219: مرا here marks the experiencer (’for me’ / 'to me'); روز روشن is a nominal phrase 'the bright day' (روز = day, روشن = bright/clear) serving as the predicative image; به دیدار تست is a light prepositional phrase 'at/with the sight/meeting of you' — تست (classical contraction of توست) functions as a copular/cliticized form asserting the relation ('the bright day is at your sight'). The overall order is poetic but reads as 'to me the day is bright at seeing you.'
segments.s165-g220: همی again marks habitual or emphatic aspect ('always'); از تو gives the source ('from you'); خواهم is 1st-person singular auxiliary/future 'I will' or 'I intend'; بد و نیک are coordinated objects 'bad and good' (bad and good things); جست is the verbal root meaning 'seek' (here used with خواهم to yield 'I will seek'). The literal structure is 'I will always from you seek bad and good.'
