segments.s165-g221: به + شبگیر گردان is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the night-roving troop' (شبگیر = nocturnal/night-roving, گردان = troop/unit); the second به introduces میدان 'into/to the field'; شدند is the 3rd-person plural past of شدن 'to become/come/appear' used here as a perfective intransitive verb 'they came/appeared' (poetic ordering places prepositional phrases before the verb).
segments.s165-g222: This is an enumerative nominal series linked by و: گرازان (boars), تازان (the charging/attacking ones or raiders), and خندان (the laughing/merry ones) — each noun/adjective is coordinated, and the clause-final شدند is again the 3rd‑person plural past of شدن, applying to the whole series ('appeared'/'came'). The sequence is descriptive and typical of epic listing.
segments.s165-g223: چنین گفت is the conventional literary formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); پس is a temporal/connecting particle 'then/afterwards'; شاه توران is the title 'the king of Turan' and بدوی here functions as a name or epithet attached to the title (in epic usage proper names or epithets often follow the title). The order is subject/title following the speech formula.
segments.s165-g224: که introduces reported content ('that' / 'that we should...'); یاران is the direct object 'companions' (whom they will choose); گزینیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/present of گزیدن 'to choose' and here functions as a hortative/jussive 'let us choose' or declarative 'we shall choose'; در + زخم گوی is a compact, poetic phrase: در marks the domain/context 'in' or 'for', زخم 'wound' and گوی (literally 'speaker' or 'making speak') together form a figurative phrase 'make the wound speak' — i.e., to inflict wounds or cause the wound to 'tell' (a poetic expression).
segments.s165-g417: A coordinated nominal phrase: 'بزرگی' (greatness) is conjoined with 'فرزند کاووس شاه' (the son of Kavus, the king). Word order is noun + modifier chain: 'فرزند' (noun) followed by the genitive/name 'کاووس شاه'. The conjunction 'و' simply links the two qualities attributed to the same implied subject.
segments.s165-g418: 'سر از بس هنرها رسیده به ماه' is a compact clause: 'سر' (head/top) is the theme, 'از' introduces the cause/measure ('from/through'), 'بس هنرها' = 'so many/much of the arts/skills', 'رسیده' is a perfect/past participle meaning 'has reached', and 'به ماه' 'to the moon' is the goal. Poetic word order places the cause before the verb; the clause reads 'the head, through so many arts, has reached the moon.'
segments.s165-g419: Two short clauses are juxtaposed without an explicit conjunction. 'پدر پیر سر شد' = 'the father, aged, became head/chief' where 'شد' is past 'became' and 'سر' functions as predicate 'head/chief.' Then 'تو برنا دلی' is an independent predication: 'تو' (you) + adjective 'برنا' (youthful) + 'دلی' ('-hearted' / 'in heart'), i.e. 'you are of youthful heart.'
segments.s165-g420: 'نگر' is the imperative 'look!' introducing an exhortation. The phrase 'سر ز تاج کیی نگسلی' literally places 'سر' (head/top) as subject, 'ز' (poetic 'از') 'from', 'تاج کیی' 'the Kayan crown', and 'نگسلی' is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'is broken/has split.' So: 'Look — the head from the Kayan crown is split.'
segments.s165-g421: Locative/identificational phrase: 'به ایران و توران' ('in Iran and Turan'), then 'توی شهریار' where 'توی' = 'in/among' and 'شهریار' (king) is the predicative noun. The copula ('are') is omitted in Persian poetry; the whole means 'you are a sovereign in Iran and Turan.'
segments.s165-g422: 'ز شاهان' ('of/from the kings' / 'among the kings') uses the poetic preposition 'ز' (= از). 'یکی' functions as 'one' or 'a single (figure),' 'پرهنر' is an adjective 'full of art/skill' modifying the following noun 'یادگار' (memorial/memento). Word order is adjective + noun; overall: 'one artful memorial among kings.'
segments.s165-g423: Imperative construction: 'بنه' (put/plant/establish) governs the object 'دل' (your heart). 'برین بوم' is a locative/adjectival phrase ('on/for this high land' — 'برین' = lofty/high + 'بوم' homeland/soil). The conjunction 'و' links to the next imperative 'بساز' ('build'), with 'جایی' as its object. Note: the printed 'بنه' here is an imperative verb; though the provided senses do not list the verbal sense, it functions as an instruction.
segments.s165-g424: 'چنان چون' is a correlative pair meaning 'such that / so that' or 'in such a way as'; 'بود' ('be/was') here has an optative/desired sense ('so that it be'), and 'درخور' means 'worthy/appropriate to.' The final phrase 'کام و ناز' lists the domains that the place should suit: 'کام' (desire) and 'ناز' (delight/affection/pampering).
segments.s165-g25: سپاهی بران سان is a nominal phrase meaning "an army / soldiers of that kind" (سپاهی = soldiers/army, بران سان = "of/upon that sort / such as that"). که گفتی is a parenthetical relative/quotative meaning "as you said" or "which you said"; the following noun سپهر is actually the subject of the verb in the next segment, so this segment begins the relative clause but leaves the verb to the next segment.
segments.s165-g26: بیاراست is a past, 3rd-person action verb here meaning "adorned / made splendid." روی زمین را is the definite object (روی = "face/surface", زمین = "earth") with را marking it as definite/object. به مهر is an adverbial phrase "with love/affection" (به used as a "by/with" preposition). Together: "the heavens adorned the face of the earth with love."
segments.s165-g27: صد اسپ گرانمایه is a quantitative + noun + adjective phrase: "a hundred precious/esteemed steeds" (اسپ = steed, گرانمایه = precious). با زین زر is a prepositional phrase "with saddles (trappings) of gold" (با = "with", زین = "saddle/trappings", زر = "gold").
segments.s165-g28: به دیبا bi‌آراسته is a participial/adjectival/verb phrase meaning "arrayed/garbed in brocade" (به + دیبا = "in brocade"); بیاراسته here describes their dressed/ornamented state. سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial "one and all / each and every / all alike." The word order places the material phrase before the predicate/adjectival idea.
segments.s165-g29: سیاووش بشنید is simple past "Siyâvash heard." The clause کامد سپاه is the reported-content clause "that the army was coming/arriving" (کامد = "came/arrived" in an archaic form, سپاه = "army"). Persian often places the verb of the subordinate clause before its subject in such poetic/arhaic word order.
segments.s165-g30: پذیره شدن را is a verbal-noun phrase meaning "the receiving / the reception" (پذیره = "reception/acceptance", شدن = verbalizing particle "to be / to happen"); را marks it as the definite object. بیاراست is the main verb in past tense "adorned / arranged / made splendid," and شاه after the verb is the subject "the king." The SOV-ish order here (object — verb — subject) is a poetic flexibility: "the king adorned/arranged the reception."
segments.s165-g31: درفش (banner/standard) followed by سپهدار پیران is a genitive/attributive sequence meaning "the standard of (the) commander Pîrân" (سپهدار = "commander", پیران here is a name/title). بدید is the past verb "saw." In this line the possessor/attributive phrase appears immediately after the head noun before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s165-g32: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning "the roaring/clamor" and functions as the object of شنید ("heard"). پیل و اسپان lists the sources of the sound: "elephants and horses." شنید is the past verb "he heard." The whole phrase means "he heard the roar/clamor of elephants and horses."
segments.s165-g1081: هرآنکس is an indefinite relative meaning “whoever/anyone”; که is the relative conjunction ‘who/that’ introducing the subordinate clause. او is the third-person pronoun ‘he’ (referring to the person previously mentioned). از در کار is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning being ‘in the service/affairs/court of’ someone (از + در + کار), and بود is the past copula ‘was’ stating the past condition.
segments.s165-g1082: بدان is the classical combination ب + آن (= 'to/that'), here functioning adverbially as ‘to that / of that’. مرز means ‘limit, rank, or boundary’ and is the thing in question; با او is a prepositional phrase ‘with him’ (linking the merit to that person). سزاوار بود is the adjective ‘deserving/fit’ with the past copula بود ‘was’ (so: ‘was considered deserving of that rank’).
segments.s165-g1083: هزار از is a partitive/quantifying construction ‘a thousand of…’. هنرمند is used as a collective/epithet ‘skillful’ or ‘skilled men’; گردان is the noun ‘troop/squadron’, and گرد is an adjectival/poetic element meaning ‘turning/around’ or simply intensifying the group (here forming a compound image of squadrons). Word order places the quantity + partitive before the group being described.
segments.s165-g1084: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when’. هنگامه رفتن is a poetic noun-phrase (هنگامه ‘tumult/moment’ + the infinitive رفتن ‘of going’), i.e. ‘the tumult/moment of departure’; آمد is the past verb ‘came’. ببرد (ب + برد) is a verbal form meaning ‘would/so as to take or carry (off)’ and here states the action that follows the coming of the tumult (they set out to carry him off). Note the compact/poetic sequence where the nominalized infinitive precedes آمد.
segments.s165-g1085: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. آمد is the past verb ‘(he) came’. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase ‘to near/near to’, with آن جایگاه ‘that place’ as the locative object — the demonstrative آن modifies جایگاه. The subject is implicit (he/he who is being described) and the clause situates the approach in time.
segments.s165-g1086: سیاوش is the proper name and subject of the following predicate. پذیره is a noun meaning ‘reception/being received’ (used nominally), and شدش is the past verb شد with the enclitic ـش = ‘to him/it’ (literally ‘the reception befell him’ → ‘he was received’). با سپاه is an instrumental/accompanying phrase ‘with an army’. The construction overall is a semi-passive: ‘Siyâvash was received with an army.’
segments.s165-g1087: چو is ‘when’. پیران is the plural subject ‘the elders’. به نزد is the classical prepositional phrase ‘to the presence of / up to’, followed by the name سیاوش as the locative object. رسید is the past verb ‘arrived/reached’. The clause reads ‘When the elders came to (the presence of) Siyâvash…’.
segments.s165-g1088: پیاده شد is an idiomatic verb phrase meaning ‘they dismounted’ or literally ‘became on-foot’ (پیاده ‘on foot’ + شد ‘became’). از دور is an adverbial phrase ‘from a distance/afar’. کاو را is the object phrase ‘him/that one’ in archaic usage (کاو here is an archaic demonstrative/pronoun; this sense is not listed in the provided sense set, so marked -1), with را the definite object marker. بدید is the past verb ‘saw’. Together: ‘they dismounted and from afar saw him.’
segments.s165-g929: نباشد is a negative jussive/optative (lit. 'may there not be' / 'let there not be') placed before its object; مرا is split here as م + را, functioning as the dative/object 'for me' (م = me, را = object marker). زندگانی دراز is the noun phrase 'a long life' serving as the thing that is wished not to exist for the speaker. Word order is verb (jussive) + indirect object + noun phrase.
segments.s165-g930: ز (poetic form of از) introduces the source/cause 'from' and precedes two coordinated loci 'کاخ و ایوان' ('palace and iwan/portico'); شوم is the first‑person present/subjunctive 'I may become' or 'I become' and functions as the main verb for this clause; بی‌نیاز is a predicative adjective 'free of need / self‑sufficient'. The prepositional phrase (ز کاخ و ز ایوان) comes before the verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s165-g931: شود is a third‑person present/subjunctive or narrative 'becomes' placed before its subject (poetic verb‑first order). تخت من is the possessed noun 'my throne' and گاه is an adverb 'sometimes/occasionally' modifying the event; افراسیاب is the proper name that functions as the eventual possessor/agent (i.e. 'becomes Afrasiab's').
segments.s165-g932: کند is the verbal auxiliary/main verb 'does/makes' here combining with the deverbal noun شتاب to express 'hasten(s)'; بی‌گنه modifies مرگ and means 'without guilt/undeserved' (qualifying the death). بر من is the prepositional phrase 'upon me', so the clause literally reads 'undeserved death makes haste upon me' = 'undeserved death hastens upon me.'
segments.s165-g933: چنین است ('thus is') is a copular phrase putting the following noun phrase into predication. رای سپهر بلند is a genitive compound 'the counsel/plan of the high heaven' (رای = counsel/plan, سپهر بلند = lofty heaven) and functions as the subject/predicate of the copula. The adjective بلند follows سپهر and qualifies it.
segments.s165-g934: گهی ... گهی is a correlative adverb pair 'at times ... at times ...' with an implicit subject (the 'سپهر بلند' from the previous segment). دارد is used in the sense 'gives/causes' or 'has' (present tense); شاد and مستمند are predicative adjectives/nouns meaning 'happy' and 'needy' respectively, showing the alternating effects of heaven's decree.
segments.s165-g935: بدو is a dative clitic phrase 'to him/unto him' (here 'the elders said to him'). گفت is the verbal root 'said' (narration), and پیران is the subject 'the elders'. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee and is followed by سرفراز, an epithet 'exalted/proud' used in direct address.
segments.s165-g936: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. خیره اندیشه is a verbal/nominal phrase where خیره ('astonished' / 'gaping') qualifies اندیشه ('thought') — together essentially meaning 'be not astonished / do not indulge astonished (foolish) thought'. دل دراز is an epithet 'long‑hearted' placed after the imperative phrase as an address or characterization of the addressee. The overall structure is negative imperative + verbal noun phrase + vocative/epithet.
segments.s165-g41: چنین گفت is a short inverted phrase meaning “thus said” (adverb ثمّ/چنین followed by the past verb گفت). کای is the vocative particle (ʻOʼ) addressing someone; شهریار جوان is the addressee (noun + adjective: “king, young”). The whole segment is the opening of direct speech.
segments.s165-g42: مراگر is an archaic contraction of مرا اگر (‘if to me’ / ‘if you to me’), i.e. introduces a conditional referring to the speaker. به خواب is a prepositional phrase here meaning ‘in (a) dream’; نمودی is 2nd-person past (نمود + -ی) = “you showed/revealed”; روان is a noun ‘soul/spirit’ serving as the thing shown. The clause therefore reads literally ‘if (to) me in a dream you showed this spirit’.
segments.s165-g43: ستایش کنم is a verb phrase where the noun ستایش (‘praise’) is followed by the present/subjunctive form کنم; in classical Persian this expresses an intention or resolve — “I will/let me praise.” پیش یزدان means “before God” (پیش = in the presence of), and نخست is an adverb ‘first’ setting the order.
segments.s165-g44: چو دیدم = ‘when I saw’ (چو = when; دیدم = I saw, past). ترا is the object ‘you’ formed from the clitic ت plus the object marker را (ت + را), placed after the verb in poetry; روشن و تندرست are coordinate adjectives ‘bright and healthy’ describing ترا. The whole is a temporal subordinate: “when I saw you bright and healthy.”
segments.s165-g45: ترا here functions as ‘to/for you’ (ت + را); چون = ‘as/like’; پدر = ‘father’. باشد is a classical third-person subjunctive/capricious copula used in conditional clauses (‘be, may be’), and افراسیاب is the proper name placed after the verb for poetic order/emphasis. The clause thus says ‘if Afrasiab is (to) you like a father’.
segments.s165-g46: همه بنده باشیم = ‘we shall all be servants’ (همه = all; بنده = servant; باشیم is 1st‑pl. subjunctive/jussive ‘may we be’ / ‘we will be’ used to express willingness or resolve). زین روی آب is a compact poetic phrase: زین (from ز + این = ‘from/for this’) + روی آب literally ‘on the surface/face (of the) water’, here used idiomatically to mean ‘for this reason, in the world’ or ‘in this life’ — a stock epic expression. The segment expresses that because of that, all will become servants.
segments.s165-g47: ز پیوستگان is a poetic order equivalent to از پیوستگان (‘of/among the followers’); هست is the copula ‘there is/are’; بیش از ... = ‘more than …’ with از marking the comparative. Word order is slightly rearranged for verse: ‘Of the followers there is more than a thousand.’
segments.s165-g48: پرستندگان‌ند is the plural noun ‘worshippers’ with the older verb/ending -ند attached as a predicate marker (they are worshippers / worshippers there are). با is the preposition ‘with’ and گوشوار is an ornament ‘earring’ (literally ‘ear‑ornament’); together they describe these followers as wearing ear‑ornaments.
segments.s165-g481: همی + بود is a classical/perfective progressive construction: همی (an emphatic/continuous particle) with the past verb بود yields 'kept being / remained'. The prepositional phrase با او follows the verb (poetic word order) and means 'with him'; شب و روز is a paired adverbial 'night and day' (and the coordinating و links the two nouns), and شاد is a predicative adjective 'happy'.
segments.s165-g482: نیامد is a past negative (literally 'did not come / did not occur') used here impersonally with یاد ('remembrance') as its subject: 'remembrance did not come'. ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'of/from' (here 'about / concerning'), کاووس is the name in the genitive relation, و links the next item, دستانش (dastān‑ash) is a possessive noun ('his dāstān' — here 'his tales/deeds'), and یاد is the noun 'memory/remembrance' that is the semantic subject of نیامد.
segments.s165-g483: برین is a poetic contraction of بر + این ('on/with this' or 'on this matter') and نیز means 'also'; چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. بگردید is the past form of گردیدن here meaning 'turned/was turned', and چرخ (placed after the verb in poetic word order) is the subject 'the wheel' — metonymically 'the wheel of fate/fortune'.
segments.s165-g484: سیاووش را is the topicalized direct object/affected person marker 'Siyavash' (را used to mark the affected entity). بد here functions as a noun/adjective meaning 'badness/misfortune', ز is the poetic 'از' meaning 'from/out of', نیکیش is 'his goodness' (نیک + ـش possessive), and برخ (an intransitive past) means 'arose/appeared' — together 'misfortune arose for Siyavash out of his goodness'.
segments.s165-g485: ورا is the old/poetic form meaning 'to him' (indirect object). هر زمان is a temporal phrase 'each/whenever', and پیش + افراسیاب means 'in the presence of Afrasiab' or 'before Afrasiab' (پیش here 'in front of / before / in the presence of'). Word order places the time and place modifiers before the rest of the clause, as is common in poetry.
segments.s165-g486: فزونتر is a comparative/adverbial 'more / increasingly'. بدی is used as a noun 'harm / ill' (so 'more harm'), and the coordinated nouns حشمت و جاه و آب are the items affected: 'majesty/pomp, rank/status, and آب (here figurative: standing/wealth/reputation)'. The clause thus means 'more and more harm to his majesty, rank, and standing'.
segments.s165-g487: یکی روز is the temporal phrase 'one day / on a certain day' (یکی = 'a certain'). پیران is the plural 'the elders'; به‌به is an interjection ('be-bah' = 'bravo! / how wonderful!') commenting on روزگار, which here means 'the times / the state of affairs'. The interjection and noun cluster describe the elders' reaction before they speak.
segments.s165-g488: سیاووش را is the addressee marked by را ('to Siyavash'). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here used with direct speech following); کای is a vocative 'O' (classical/formal) and نامدار is the adjective 'renowned/illustrious' addressing him. (Note: the listed senses for گفت in the metadata are noun-ish; here the form functions as the past-tense verb 'said'.)
segments.s165-g945: تو (subject) + noun phrase پیمان چنین + verb داری: word order is subject — object — verb, with the possessive/possessive-like verb داری (second person singular present) meaning “you have.” The coordinate phrase و رای راست follows داری as a second item you possess: رای (opinion/counsel) modified postpositively by راست (“right/just”).
segments.s165-g946: ولیکن introduces a contrast (‘but/yet’). فلک را uses را as an object/topic marker to personify the heavens/fate; جز اینست خواست is a nominal clause: اینست = ‘this is’ and خواست (here a noun) = ‘desire/will,’ so the clause says ‘the heavens’ will is nothing but this.’
segments.s165-g947: همه راز من = 'all my secret(s)' (possessive genitive with من). آشکارا is an adverb (‘openly/clearly’). به تست here functions as a dative/possessive phrase: به + تست (archaic clitic meaning essentially ‘is to you’ / ‘for you’), so the sense is ‘all my secrets are openly for/with you.’
segments.s165-g948: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'). The predicate is nominal: بیدار دل = 'awake/alert of heart' (adjective + noun), followed by بادی — an adjectival/epithetic word here giving a spirited/volatile nuance — and the final تندرست 'healthy.' The adjectives follow the noun, a typical Persian order.
segments.s165-g949: من is the subject 'I.' آگاهی is the direct object 'knowledge/information,' followed by the preposition از introducing the domain/topic (از فر یزدان = 'about/from the divine farr'). دهم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive form of دادن used here to mean 'I give / I will give / I tell.'
segments.s165-g950: هم 'also' fronted for emphasis. از introduces the topic 'of the secret(s)'. راز چرخ بلند is a noun phrase 'the secret(s) of the lofty/heavenly wheel (the heavens),' where بلند is an adjectival qualifier postposed to چرخ. آگهم is an older adjectival/verb form meaning 'I am aware' (آگه + first-person suffix).
segments.s165-g951: بگویم is the first-person present/subjunctive of گفتن used here as a prospective/future 'I will tell/I shall tell.' ت + را represents the object pronoun 'ترا' split into the clitic ت (second‑person object) plus را (object marker) meaning 'you' as the recipient. بودنیها درست is the object phrase meaning 'the beings/that which was' or idiomatically 'the true account of what happened' with درست qualifying it as 'correct/true.'
segments.s165-g952: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from/starting with.' ایوان و کاخ are coordinated places 'the iwan (portico) and the palace.' اندرآیم is the first-person form of entering/going in (here narrative/historical: 'I entered' or 'I came into'). نخـست is an adverb meaning 'first' and modifies the clause.
segments.s165-g553: Subject + object + adverb + verb + adverb: خردمند is the subject 'the wise (man)'; پاسخ چنین (literally 'an answer such') is a noun phrase 'such an answer'; داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' used idiomatically for 'replied'; باز is an adverb meaning 'again' or 'in return' (classical usage: 'answered back/again').
segments.s165-g554: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); مبادا is a negative optative/jussive particle meaning 'may it not be'/'lest'; از تو is topicalized prepositional phrase 'from/of you' placed before the predicate; جهان بی‌نیاز is the predicate 'the world [be] self-sufficient/without need.' Put together: 'that the world may not become without need of you.'
segments.s165-g555: مرا ... خواسته هست is a classical/periphrastic expression of desire/need: مرا (object/topic marker attached to the speaker) + خواسته (a noun/participle 'desire' or 'wanted') + هست (copula) — together functioning like 'I have a wish' or 'I desire'. The following nouns گنج و سپاه are the objects of that desire ('treasure and army'), coordinated by و.
segments.s165-g556: به بخت تو is a prepositional phrase 'to/for your fortune' indicating possession or attribution; the paired هم ... هم ... construction ('both ... and ...') links تیغ and تاج as things belonging to that fortune; گاه at the end is a noun meaning 'time/occasion' (here enumerated along with sword and crown, possibly 'and opportunity').
segments.s165-g557: ز بهر is a classical/poetic double preposition meaning 'for' or 'on behalf of' (ز + بهر); سیاوش is the beneficiary/name; پیامی دراز is a noun phrase 'a long message' (پیامی = 'a message', دراز = 'long') placed after the prepositional phrase as the thing being sent.
segments.s165-g558: رسانم is the first-person present/future-like form of رساندن 'I deliver / I will convey'; به گوش سپهبد is the idiom 'to the ear of the spahbed' (literally 'to the ear', i.e. to his attention); به راز is an adverbial phrase 'in secret' or 'privately'. Together: 'I will convey (it) to the spahbed's ear in secret.'
segments.s165-g559: This is reported imperative/discourse: مرا گفت = 'he said to me' (مرا marks the indirect object 'to me' in classical order); با شاه ترکان بگوی is an instruction 'say/tell to the king of the Turks' where بگوی is the verb 'say/tell' in the imperative/subjunctive sense and با شاه ترکان is the prepositional phrase indicating the addressee. Word order follows older Persian practice of placing the reported command after 'he said'.
segments.s165-g560: که introduces the content of what is to be said ('that ...'); من is the subject 'I'; شاد دل is a compound adjective 'glad-hearted' (شاد modifying دل); گشتم is the past tense of گشتن used copularly 'I became'; و links the next predicate نامجوی, which here means 'seeker of a name/fame' (i.e. 'seeking renown'). Together: 'that I have become glad-hearted and am seeking renown.'
segments.s165-g209: که is the subordinating conjunction introducing reported content ('that'). با گوی و چوگان is a prepositional phrase 'with ball and mallet' (گوی = ball/sphere, چوگان = polo‑mallet). به میدان is the directional phrase 'into/to the field' placed before the verb. شویم is the 1st‑person‑plural classical subjunctive/optative of شدن used here to express 'let us be/let us go/let us appear' (literary word order: prepositional phrase + verb).
segments.s165-g210: زمانی is an adverbial 'for a while' modifying the following verb. بتازیم is the present/subjunctive form (with the prefixed b‑) of تاختن meaning 'to rush, to gallop'—here 'let us gallop' or 'we shall gallop'. و links the two coordinated predications; خندان is an adjective 'smiling, merry' and شویم again is the 1st‑person‑plural subjunctive of شدن meaning 'we become / we shall be (merry)'.
segments.s165-g211: ز هر کس شنیدم is a terse classical phrasing meaning 'from every/each person I heard' (ز = 'from', هر کس = 'everyone/each person', شنیدم = past 'I heard'). که introduces the reported clause that follows. چوگان تو is the noun phrase 'your polo / your game or polo‑equipment' (چوگان can mean the game or the mallet here functioning as the thing people speak of).
segments.s165-g212: نبینند is the (classical) 3rd‑person‑plural present/subjunctive/optative form of دیدن with negation prefix ن‑ meaning 'they do not see' (here reported-general). گردان is the direct noun object; in context it reads as 'troop' or 'formation' (literary sense). به میدان تو is the locative/directional phrase 'in your field' (به + میدان + possessive تو). Word order places verb before object in this poetic register.
segments.s165-g213: تو فرزند مایی = 'you are our child/descendant'; here مایی is 'of us' formed with ما + the adjectival/genitive ending (classical possessive construction). و links the following epithet. زیبای گاه is a poetic genitive phrase 'the beauty of the time/age' (زیبای = 'beautiful one / beauty of', گاه = 'time/age' used poetically to mean 'of the era').
segments.s165-g214: تو تاج کیانی = 'you (are) the Kayanian crown' (تاج = crown; کیانی = Kayanian/royal, a dynastic adjective). و links the last appositive; پشت سپاه = 'the rear/support of the army' where پشت carries the extended meaning 'support/protector' of سپاه (army). The copula 'are' is omitted in Persian and understood from context.
segments.s165-g215: بدو گفت is the inverted, literary sequence 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him/unto it', گفت = past 'said'); شاها is the vocative 'O king!'. انوشه بدی here functions as a proper name or name‑phrase (Anoushe‑badi) addressed or reported—classical Persian often compounds names in this way.
segments.s165-g216: روان را uses روان ('soul, spirit' or a personal name) plus the object marker را, making روان the direct object. به دیدار = 'for/to the meeting/visit' (prepositional phrase). توشه بدی combines توشه 'provisions, stores' with بدی; in context this reads as 'gave (as) a provision'—classical Persian often places the verb (دادن 'to give') or its reduced form after the object, and بدی here functions as a past verbal form meaning 'gave' in the narrative (written in older orthography).
segments.s165-g577: This clause begins with the conjunction و (and), followed by دیگر که which introduces an additional remark ('and moreover / and another thing that...'). از پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from before' or 'beforehand' and precedes کندآوران, a plural agent noun (‑ان plural) meaning 'those who cause/bring about (slowness/retarding)' or 'the ones who act to slow/oppose'. Grammatically this whole segment is an initial nominal clause (conjunction + topic) that prepares the following noun phrase.
segments.s165-g578: ز کار is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the work / from the deed of...'; ستاره شمر is a genitive construction 'the star of Shamar' (star + proper name). بخردان here is ambiguous in form: morphologically it reads as a dative/plural address 'to the wise' (بِ خردان = 'to the wise ones') or as an old verbal form; in this line it functions as the complement/recipient phrase tied to the preceding preposition, so the entire segment is a noun/prepositional phrase that will link into the next line for its verbal predicate.
segments.s165-g579: شمار ستاره is a noun phrase 'the number / the host of stars' (شمار + noun), followed by the locative phrase به پیش پدر 'in front of / before the father' (به + پیش + noun). The order is noun + directional/location phrase; the whole phrase supplies the object/place around which the following verb clause operates.
segments.s165-g580: همی is an archaic/poetic adverb marking continuous or iterative aspect ('continually / kept on'); راندندی is an epic/older plural verbal form of راندن ('they drove/they put to flight') — here third‑person plural. همه دربدر is a result/target phrase 'all (everyone) astray/adrift' where دربدر is predicative/adverbial ('wandering, homeless'): the verb راندندی takes همه (all) as its patient and renders them دربدر.
segments.s165-g581: کزین is a fused prepositional/conjunctive form ('from this / of these'); دو نژاده is a compound noun 'two-born / two lineages' (نژاده = 'of a race / lineage'); یکی شهریار is an indefinite nominal clause 'one (of them) (a) king' — numeric + noun used as subject/predicate. Word order is demonstrative/prepositional phrase → description of the group → identification of one member as king.
segments.s165-g582: بیاید is an optative/epic-form third‑person singular meaning 'he/one may come' (future/uncertain event); بگیرد is the present/subjunctive of گرفتن 'to seize / take' and follows بیاید to express the coming agent's action. جهان is the direct object 'the world'; در کنار is a locative/adverbial phrase 'to his side / into his keeping' (در + کنار). The sequence verb(بیاید) + verb(بگیرد) + object is a common epic pattern where two verbs express coming and action in sequence.
segments.s165-g583: به توران is a dative/locative phrase 'for Turan / to Turan'; نماند is an existential negative 'there remained not / was not left'. برو بوم here functions as a small clause meaning 'a homeland to go to' (برو used with بوم to indicate 'to go to a home/place'); the clause thus says 'there remained no homeland for Turan to go to.' The conjunction و links the next short verb رست ('was saved / endured').
segments.s165-g584: کلاه من اندازد is a verbal clause in which اندازد (third‑person singular of انداختن) means 'he will throw / cast / put off' or figuratively 'strip (me) of (my) hat/crown'; کلاه من is the object 'my hat / my crown'. از کین نخست is an ablative/causal phrase 'from that first hatred / out of the first grudge' giving the motive or source for the action. Word order is object + verb + cause phrase (typical in poetry where topical objects can be fronted).
segments.s165-g265: از introduces a source ('from/at the sound of') and is followed by آوای + noun phrase (the ezāfe construction: آوای سنج و دم ... = 'the sound/voice of the sanj and the breath of ...'). سنج here is a musical instrument (a cymbal/metal percussion) — an older poetic word — and دم means 'breath/blast' (of a wind instrument). کره modifies نای in an older/poetic way (a diminutive/part of the reed-flute).
segments.s165-g266: تو گفتی is 'you said' (گفتی is the past 2nd-singular of گفتن). The clause بجنبید میدان ز جای reports the content: بجنبید is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'moved/was stirred' (here used as the reported action), and note the poetic inversion with the verb before its subject میدان; ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from its place' (ز = poetic 'from').
segments.s165-g267: This is a simple S-V-O order: سیاووش (subject) + برانگیخت (past tense 'roused/spurred') + اسپ نبرد, where اسپ is the archaic form of modern 'اسب' and نبرد ('battle') qualifies it as a compound 'war-horse/war-steed'.
segments.s165-g268: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'. گوی is the subject ('the orb/ball' here) and اندر آمد is the past tense 'came in/entered' (اندر = 'in/within'). به پیشش = 'into his presence / before him', and به گرد is an adverbial 'about/around' — the two به phrases mark direction/position (poetic repetition is normal).
segments.s165-g269: بزد is a past tense verb 'he struck/smote'. هم چنان چون is a fixed poetic sequence meaning 'just so / just as', and به میدان رسید completes the temporal clause 'just as he reached the field' (رسید = past 'arrived/reached'). Word order places the verb بزد before the comparative phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s165-g270: بران سان (= 'in that manner' / 'thereupon') + که introduces the result clause. از چشم شد ناپدید is an idiom: از چشم 'from the eye/sight', شد is the auxiliary 'became' and ناپدید 'invisible/vanished' — together 'became unseen / vanished from sight.'
segments.s165-g271: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense 'he commanded/said' (often used for kings). پس is 'then/afterward'. شهریار بلند is a noun + adjective 'the lofty/high king' (adjective follows noun, a normal poetic order).
segments.s165-g272: که introduces the subordinate (reported) clause. گویی here functions as the object 'گوی' with an archaic -ی ending (object marking) — 'the gūy' — and به نزد سیاوش برند is the verb phrase 'bring to the presence of Siyavash': به نزد = 'to the presence of', برند is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person plural subjunctive/imperative of بردن 'they bring / let them bring' used to express the ordered action.
segments.s165-g449: نخواهد here functions as a literary negative-existential (roughly ‘there is not / no one’). کسی را places ‘anyone/no one’ with the enclitic را used for emphasis/definiteness, and the relative clause که آن رای نیست (‘who is not of that opinion’) modifies کسی: آن رای is ‘that opinion/wish’ and نیست negates it. The whole segment reads literally ‘There is no one who is not of that opinion.’
segments.s165-g450: بجز marks an exception (‘except / apart from’). چهر شاهش is a genitive nominal phrase ‘the face/countenance of his king’ (شاهش contains the possessive -ش), and دلارای is used here as a substantive/epithet ‘the beloved, the charming one.’ The final نیست asserts nonexistence so the clause means ‘there is no (beloved) except the king’s visage.’
segments.s165-g451: ز is the poetic form of از meaning ‘from / among.’ خوبان = ‘the beautiful ones / beloveds.’ جریرست is a compound of the proper name جریر + است (contracted) and functions as the predicate ‘is Jarir’ (here Jarir is used metaphorically/epithetically). انباز is an archaic/uncertain epithet attached to تو (‘your …’), so the clause gives a flattering identification: ‘Among the beautiful ones, he is (your) Jarir, your anbāz.’
segments.s165-g452: بود is the past copula ‘was.’ روز رخشنده is a nominal phrase ‘the radiant/bright day’ and دمساز تو (literally ‘your damsāz’) is a compound meaning ‘your companion‑musician’ or minstrel/partner in song. Word order puts the descriptive nouns before the copula: ‘The radiant day was your companion‑musician.’
segments.s165-g453: اگر introduces a conditional (‘if’). رای باشد ترا = ‘if it be your will/wish’ (رای here = wish/plan and باشد is the subjunctive/present copula ‘be’). ترا (split here as ت + را) is the dative/accusative ‘to you.’ بنده‌ایست is the predicative ‘is a servant’ (بنده + است). So: ‘If it be your wish, he is a servant to you.’
segments.s165-g454: به پیش تو = ‘before/in the presence of you’ (به + پیش + possessive تو). اندر is a literary locative intensifier ‘in/among.’ پرستنده‌ایست is the predicate ‘is a worshipper/adorer/admirer’ (پرستنده + است). Together: ‘in your presence he is an admirer.’
segments.s165-g455: سیاوش is the speaking subject. بدو ‘to him’ is the dative; گفت is the past-tense reporting verb ‘said.’ دارم سپاس is a fixed phrasing meaning ‘I have thanks’ → ‘I am grateful’ or ‘I give thanks’ (دارم = ‘I have’, سپاس = ‘thanks’). The sequence is reported speech: ‘Siyâvash said to him, “I give thanks…”’
segments.s165-g456: مرا خود = ‘me myself’ (مرا = ‘me’ with emphasis خود ‘self’). ز فرزند برتر = ‘superior among the children/descendants’ (ز = from/among, فرزند = child/descendant, برتر = superior). شناس is an imperative/verb ‘know / recognize’ addressed to the addressee. Put together it is an instruction to recognize the speaker’s superiority: ‘Know that I myself am superior among the offspring.’
segments.s165-g1161: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The noun phrase کاخ فرنگیس (palace of Farangis) comes before the verb دیدم (I saw), which is a simple past first‑person form; ز دور is a post‑verbal prepositional phrase 'from afar' using the poetic preposition ز = 'from'.
segments.s165-g1162: This clause begins with a simile چو = 'as/like'. گنج گهر is a genitive grouping 'a treasure (گنج) of jewels (گهر)'; the short form بد here is a poetic/archaic contraction of بود 'was' (i.e. 'it was'), and به میدان سور places the image 'in the field/arena of the feast' (به = into/towards + میدان = arena/field, سور = feast).
segments.s165-g1163: بدان is a compound: ب- (a prefix) + دان (know) functioning here as an imperative/verb 'know' or 'be aware'. زیب و آیین is the phrase 'beauty and ceremony/ritual' and که introduces the subordinate clause داماد تست ('that you are the groom'); تست is the poetic spelling of توست 'you are' (copula).
segments.s165-g1164: ز introduces the causal/source 'because of' (ز خوبی = 'because of that goodness/beauty'). به کام دل is an idiomatic phrase 'to the heart's fulfillment' (به = to/for, کام = fulfilment/desire, دل = heart). شاد is adjective 'happy' and تست (again the copula 'tu-st') attaches to describe the subject of the previous clause ('you are happy').
segments.s165-g1165: گله کرد is the verbal phrase 'to complain' (گله = complaint used as verbal noun + کرد = 'did/make' forming the action). باید is a modal 'must/ought' that follows the verb in this poetic order. به گیتی یله is an adverbial phrase 'toward the world be lax/carefree' (به = toward, گیتی = the world, یله = loose/unrestrained), giving the manner or attitude one should take.
segments.s165-g1166: ترا is the object pronoun 'to you' (here shown as ت + را). چون introduces the reason 'since/because'. نباشد is a negative subjunctive/anticipatory form 'there will not be / is not'. ز گیتی گله = 'complaint from the world' (ز = from, گیتی = world, گله = complaint), so the whole clause says 'since there will be no complaint from the world against you.'
segments.s165-g1167: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an enclitic/uncertain adverb meaning roughly 'perhaps/if by chance' (a poetic particle). آید is the third‑person present/subjunctive 'comes' and ز مینو سروش is the agent/source 'a srosh (divine messenger) from heaven' (ز = from, مینو = heaven, سروش = messenger/angel).
segments.s165-g1168: نباشد is negative subjunctive 'there would not be / it will not be'. بدان here functions as 'in that / by that' (به + آن contracted in poetry) linking back to the hypothetical; فر و اورنگ و هوش lists what would not be present—فر = royal glory, اورنگ = throne/worldly splendor, هوش = intellect/understanding. The clause thus denies that such a divine arrival would add those things.
